title: Non-lactic acid bacteria probiotics isolated from intestine or various circumstances
summary: This article examines next-generation probiotics beyond traditional lactic acid bacteria, focusing on non-LAB strains isolated from the intestinal microbiota. Key intestinal-derived probiotics identified include strictly anaerobic species such as Akkermansia muciniphila, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Christensenella minuta, Anaerobutyricum soehngenii, and Oxalobacter formigenes, which present challenging culturing requirements compared to aerobic externally sourced strains like Bacillus and Saccharomyces cerevisiae. The non-LAB probiotics exhibit diverse health-promoting properties including antimicrobial, anticancer, antioxidant, and vitamin production activities that extend beyond conventional probiotic capabilities. These findings suggest that intestinal-derived non-LAB probiotics represent a promising therapeutic avenue for addressing microbiota dysbiosis-related conditions, potentially including endometriosis through restoration of beneficial microbial communities. The characterization of these next-generation strains provides a foundation for developing more effective microbiota-targeted interventions compared to LAB-dominated formulations.

title: Multi-omics analysis reveals associations between host gene expression, gut microbiota, and metabolites in chickens
summary:
# Multi-Omics Analysis of Host-Microbiota-Metabolite Interactions in Laying Chickens

This multi-omics study reveals a coordinated regulatory network linking the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, hepatic gene expression, gut microbial composition, and metabolite production during peak egg-laying in chickens. The hypothalamic-pituitary axis regulates egg-laying through multiple differentially expressed genes including PENK, NPY, AVP, PRL, RLN3, and FST, which collectively control female gonadal development and hormone secretion. During peak-laying, significant shifts occur in cecal microbiota composition, with Firmicutes abundance decreasing while Bacteroidota increases, accompanied by dramatic changes in metabolic pathways including amino acid biosynthesis, steroid hormone biosynthesis, and purine metabolism. Integrative analysis identified specific microbial taxa as functional contributors: the genus Megasphaera correlates with amino acid metabolites (3-phenyllactic acid, quinic acid, caffeic acid, and folic acid), while Hungatella shows strong correlation with estradiol production, directly linking microbial composition to steroid hormone biosynthesis. Hepatic gene expression patterns in lipid metabolism and efferocytosis further support the liver's role as a central metabolic hub producing precursors necessary for reproductive function. These findings demonstrate that egg-laying performance emerges from coordinated interactions between neuroendocrine signaling, host liver metabolism, and microbial-derived metabolites.

title: Sex Differences in Gut Microbiota, Hypertension, and Cardiovascular Risk
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbiota dysbiosis represents a critical mechanism linking microbial imbalance to multiple systemic diseases. Dysbiosis compromises intestinal barrier integrity and activates inflammatory pathways, triggering bacterial translocation, metabolic inflammation, and endotoxemia that contribute to cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, and inflammatory bowel disease. The gut microbiota influences host health through metabolite production, including short-chain fatty acids and trimethylamine-N-oxide, which regulate immune cell development and affect distant organs via the microbiota-gut-brain axis. Dietary composition shapes microbial structure, with plant-based foods promoting beneficial Prevotella species while high-protein, animal-fat diets favor pathogenic Bacteroides. Therapeutic interventions including prebiotics, probiotics, and synbiotics reduce systemic inflammation, decrease metabolic syndrome severity, and dampen pro-inflammatory Th17 pathways, while fecal microbiota transplantation effectively treats antibiotic-resistant Clostridium difficile infections. These findings establish microbiota-targeted therapies as promising approaches for preventing and managing chronic inflammatory and cardiovascular diseases.

title: Bacteria-tumor symbiosis destructible novel nanocatalysis drug delivery systems for effective tumor therapy
summary: Microbiota dysbiosis, characterized by an imbalance between protective probiotics and pathogenic bacteria, drives colorectal cancer development through accumulation of bacterial toxins and carcinogenic metabolites that promote abnormal cell proliferation. The opportunistic pathogen Fusobacterium nucleatum plays a particularly significant role, influencing colorectal cancer onset, progression, and metastasis through its symbiotic interaction with tumor cells. Nanomaterial-based drug delivery systems represent a novel therapeutic strategy capable of simultaneously treating both the underlying bacterial infection and the resultant cancer by directly disrupting the pathogenic Fusobacterium nucleatum-tumor symbiosis. This dual-action nanocatalytic approach addresses the root cause of dysbiosis-driven tumorigenesis rather than treating cancer alone. Such strategies highlight the importance of understanding the microbiota-tumor axis in developing more effective personalized cancer therapies that target both microbial and malignant components of the disease.

title: Methodological approaches in 16S sequencing of female reproductive tract in fertility patients: a review
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how 16S sequencing methodologies reveal the critical role of vaginal microbiota composition in female reproductive health and fertility outcomes. A healthy vaginal microbiome maintains homeostasis and supports reproductive function, while dysbiosis significantly impairs assisted reproductive technology (ART) results. Bacterial vaginosis and pathogenic species such as Gardnerella vaginalis and Atopobium vaginae are associated with reduced implantation rates, decreased biochemical pregnancy, and increased pregnancy loss during IVF-embryo transfer, with approximately 39% of infertile patients presenting abnormal flora. The review emphasizes that methodological factors substantially influence microbiota characterization, including DNA extraction methods, sampling site selection, and sample collection technique, with endometrial biopsy providing superior specificity by reducing contamination from lower reproductive tract flora. Next-generation sequencing has become the gold standard for microbiome analysis, enabling identification of key bacterial genera including Lactobacillus, Prevotella, Streptococcus, and various pathogenic species, though emerging third-generation sequencing technologies promise further improvements in microbiota-based reproductive research and clinical decision-making.

title: Research progress on the interaction between intestinal flora and microRNA in pelvic inflammatory diseases
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease results from pathogenic infections, particularly *Chlamydia trachomatis* and *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, which trigger immune inflammatory responses and can progress to serious complications including infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain if untreated. The pathogenesis involves complex interactions between gut microbiota dysbiosis and microRNA (miRNA) regulation, where dysregulated microbiota dysregulates immune function through activation of Toll-like receptors and NF-κB signaling, leading to increased pro-inflammatory cytokines and altered Th1/Th2 balance. Specific miRNAs regulate both immune responses and microbiota composition—for example, miR-181b-5p promotes anti-inflammatory M2 macrophage polarization while miR-200b-3p modulates microbiota structure—and chlamydia infection upregulates miR-142-3p, miR-520-3p, and miR-147a to exacerbate inflammation. Therapeutic approaches target microbiota restoration through probiotics like *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* and *Bifidobacterium longum*, which inhibit pathogenic biofilms, reduce pro-inflammatory markers, and restore intestinal barrier function, while prebiotics stimulate beneficial bacteria growth and short-chain fatty acid production to enhance immune tolerance. These findings establish the gut microbiota-miRNA axis as a critical mechanism in pelvic inflammatory disease pathogenesis and highlight microbiota-based interventions as promising treatment strategies.

title: Understanding the Associations of Urogenital Microbiomes With Fertility and In Vitro Fertilization
summary:
# Summary: Urogenital Microbiomes and Fertility in Reproductive Medicine

Urogenital microbiome composition critically influences both female and male fertility outcomes in natural conception and in vitro fertilization. Dysbiosis of the vaginal microbiome, characterized by loss of protective Lactobacillus species and overgrowth of anaerobes such as Gardnerella, Ureaplasma, and Atopobium, triggers aberrant inflammation and reduces embryo implantation success rates. In females, a Lactobacillus-dominant microbiome maintains vaginal pH and suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α), whereas dysbiotic states promote dendritic cell maturation and inflammatory responses that impair endometrial receptivity and increase implantation failure. In males, urogenital inflammation from dysbiosis and bacterial infections directly damages sperm quality through elevated inflammatory cytokines, reactive oxygen species, and DNA fragmentation, thereby compromising male fertility. The endometrial microbiome similarly associates with implantation outcomes, with dysbiosis defined as less than 90% Lactobacillus abundance linked to elevated IL-6 and decreased pregnancy rates. Overall, maintaining a Lactobacillus-dominant urogenital microbiome, particularly hydrogen peroxide-producing strains, emerges as essential for optimizing fertility and IVF success.

title: A comprehensive analysis of the uterine microbiome in endometrial cancer patients - identification of Anaerococcus as a potential biomarker and carcinogenic cofactor
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that endometrial cancer is associated with significant dysbiosis of the uterine microbiome, characterized by elevated bacterial diversity and a shift from the Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota found in healthy endometrial myoma samples. Anaerococcus emerged as a key pathogenic biomarker differentiating endometrial cancer from healthy controls, with enhanced ability to adhere to human endometrial fibroblasts and induce reactive oxygen species production compared to beneficial Lactobacillus jensenii. The carcinogenic mechanism involves Anaerococcus-driven oxidative stress and chronic inflammation, processes exacerbated by postmenopausal status, obesity, and increased uterine bacterial diversity. Multiple pathogenic taxa including Streptococcus, Prevotella, Gardnerella, Peptoniphilus, and Porphyromonas were enriched in endometrial cancer samples, collectively contributing to dysbiosis-associated cellular damage accumulation. These findings establish the uterine microbiome composition as a potential diagnostic signature for endometrial cancer and suggest that dysbiotic microbial communities may serve as carcinogenic cofactors in endometrial tumorigenesis.

title: Acupuncture mediates the “gut–testis axis” to improve asthenozoospermia
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that acupuncture improves asthenozoospermia (low sperm motility) through modulation of the gut–testis axis. Acupuncture enhances sperm quality and motility while ameliorating testicular pathology, effects mediated by upregulation of testosterone, follicle-stimulating hormone, and luteinizing hormone alongside improvements in both gut and blood–testis barriers. The underlying mechanism involves restoration of dysbiotic gut microbiota composition, as specific bacterial taxa (Bacteroidota, Firmicutes, Faecalibaculum, and Dubosiella) and serum/testicular metabolites (cytosine, N-oleyl-leucine, 5-fluorouridine, and 1-acetylimidazole) associate with sperm motility improvements. Functionally, the gut microbiota regulates androgen production, testicular hormone secretion, spermatogenesis, and immune homeostasis, while dysbiosis-induced oxidative stress deteriorates sperm quality—effects acupuncture counteracts by reducing oxidative stress and inflammatory responses while regulating the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis. Notably, fecal microbiota transplantation alone reproduced acupuncture's reproductive benefits, establishing the gut microbiota as a mechanistic mediator of acupuncture's therapeutic effects in asthenozoospermia.

title: Identification of distinct stool metabolites in women with endometriosis for non-invasive diagnosis and potential for microbiota-based therapies
summary:
# Summary

This study identified a distinctive stool metabolite signature in women with endometriosis, revealing dysbiosis characterized by decreased beneficial bacteria (Ruminococcus, Faecalibacterium, Alistipes, Roseburia) and increased pathogenic taxa (Erysipelatoclostridium, Streptococcus, Actinomyces), with metabolite patterns mirroring inflammatory bowel disease. The bacterial metabolite 4-hydroxyindole was significantly lower in endometriosis patients and functionally suppressed disease initiation and progression in murine models by reducing macrophage infiltration, pro-inflammatory cytokines (NF-κB, IL-6), and expression of endometriosis-promoting factors (IGF1, TGF-β1, GATA3). 4-hydroxyindole treatment also attenuated pain-related symptoms and reduced endometrial lesion size, cell proliferation, and endometrial gland formation in established disease. These findings establish gut microbiota dysbiosis as a mechanistic driver of endometriosis and demonstrate the therapeutic potential of microbiota-derived metabolites as a non-invasive diagnostic and treatment strategy for this chronic inflammatory disorder.

title: Uterine Microbiota and Bisphenols: Novel Influencers in Reproductive Health
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how bisphenols and uterine microbiota dysbiosis contribute to reproductive dysfunction, particularly endometriosis. Bisphenols, endocrine-disrupting chemicals, bind to estrogen, androgen, and thyroid hormone receptors, causing systemic endocrine disruption associated with infertility and adverse reproductive outcomes. Additionally, bisphenols alter both gut and uterine microbial composition by accumulating in bacterial membranes and disrupting membrane permeability, triggering dysbiosis. The resulting microbial imbalance impairs intestinal barrier function through dysregulation of Toll-like receptor signaling and tight junction proteins, and similarly compromises the uterine epithelial barrier integrity—processes critical for embryo implantation. Dysbiosis marked by Lactobacillus depletion and overgrowth of pathogenic bacteria (such as Gardnerella vaginalis) is associated with multiple reproductive failures including infertility, preterm birth, embryo implantation failure, chronic endometritis, and endometriosis. Conversely, Lactobacillus crispatus dominance promotes epithelial barrier integrity through lactic acid-mediated upregulation of tight junction proteins (claudins) and supports successful pregnancy outcomes. The authors propose that bisphenol exposure-induced microbiota dysbiosis represents a novel dual mechanism linking environmental chemical exposure to reproductive pathology.

title: The Urogenital System Microbiota: Is It a New Gamechanger in Urogenital Cancers?
summary:
# Urogenital Microbiota Dysbiosis and Urogenital Cancer Risk

Dysbiosis of the urogenital microbiota is increasingly recognized as a significant risk factor for multiple urogenital cancers, including bladder, kidney, cervical, endometrial, and ovarian cancers. Loss of protective Lactobacillus dominance in the vaginal and urinary microbiota creates an environment where pathogenic bacteria trigger chronic inflammatory responses through elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-17α, TNF-α) and disrupt epithelial barrier integrity, promoting cancer development. Specific dysbiotic bacteria such as Fannyhessea vaginae, Porphyromonas species, Lancefieldella parvulum, and Fusobacterium nucleatum directly activate oncogenic pathways by modulating cancer metabolic hallmarks and amplifying inflammation. Additionally, dysbiosis impairs estrogen metabolism through disruption of the gut-liver axis and enterohepatic circulation, elevating circulating estrogen levels and increasing risk for hormone-dependent cancers, while dysbiotic microbes produce genotoxic metabolites and nitrosamines that cause direct DNA damage. Coinfections with oncogenic pathogens like human papillomavirus and Chlamydia trachomatis are exacerbated by dysbiosis and create synergistic carcinogenic effects. These findings suggest that targeting microbial dysbiosis through interventions like probiotics or restoration of Lactobacillus dominance may represent a novel therapeutic strategy for preventing or managing urogenital cancers.

title: Gut–X axis
summary:
# Gut-X Axis: Microbiota Dysbiosis and Extraintestinal Pathology

The gut microbiota modulates pathophysiology of distant organs through translocation of bioactive chemicals and immune cells into the circulatory system, establishing a critical gut-organ axis relevant to multiple disease states. Dysbiotic shifts toward pathogenic bacteria like Enterococcus species promote disease progression through multiple mechanisms: Enterococcus activates toll-like receptor signaling and produces cytolysin, driving liver inflammation and hepatocellular carcinoma development, while microbial metabolites including trimethylamine N-oxide induce metabolic and cardiovascular abnormalities. Conversely, beneficial bacteria and their metabolites provide protection; short-chain fatty acids prevent organ injury, Akkermansia species reduce inflammation and support immunotherapy responsiveness, and tryptophan metabolites from Lactobacillus reuteri suppress tumorigenesis through aryl hydrocarbon receptor signaling. Specific bacterial metabolites exhibit dose-dependent effects, with secondary bile acids and indole derivatives promoting intestinal homeostasis and hepatoprotection, while certain indole metabolites paradoxically activate hepatic stellate cells. Therapeutic interventions targeting dysbiosis—including selective probiotics, bile acid modulators, and melatonin—restore intestinal farnesoid X receptor signaling and reduce both inflammation and fibrosis across liver disease spectrum.

title: Getting to the Heart of the Matter: Exploring the Intersection of Cardiovascular Disease, Sex and Race and How Exercise, and Gut Microbiota Influence these Relationships
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how exercise and gut microbiota composition influence cardiovascular health across biological sex and racial/ethnic groups. Physical inactivity is a major contributor to cardiovascular disease (CVD) incidence, while exercise demonstrates protective effects through multiple mechanisms including improved gut microbial diversity, increased abundance of beneficial bacteria (such as *Roseburia intestinalis*, *Faecalibacterium prausinitzii*, and *Akkermansia muciniphila*), and enhanced metabolite production that improves carbohydrate metabolism and insulin sensitivity. A healthy gut microbiota is essential for optimal human health and exercise performance, whereas dysbiosis contributes to chronic disease; however, cessation of exercise rapidly diminishes these beneficial microbial modifications. Biological sex significantly shapes both cardiovascular physiology and microbiota composition through hormonal regulation—males have larger hearts and higher hemoglobin concentrations, while estrogen and testosterone act as potent modulators of microbiota structure, with females showing higher *Ruminococcaceae* and males enriched in *Prevotellaceae*. The review emphasizes that regular exercise reduces CVD risk through lowered blood pressure, decreased triglycerides, increased HDL cholesterol, reduced arterial stiffness, and decreased depression risk, establishing exercise as a critical intervention for maintaining healthy aging across diverse populations.

title: Flagellin in blood and Bifidobacteriumpseudocatenulatum in gut are associated with live birth upon IVF-frozen embryo transfer
summary: This study reveals that serum flagellin levels and gut microbiota composition predict live birth outcomes in frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycles. Higher serum flagellin concentrations, primarily derived from the flagellated bacterium Roseburia inulinivorans, were significantly associated with failed pregnancy, with a tenfold increase in flagellin reducing odds of live birth by 82.9%. Conversely, enrichment of Bifidobacterium pseudocatenulatum in the gut microbiota predicted successful live birth and was negatively correlated with serum flagellin levels. The protective mechanism involves B. pseudocatenulatum's cystathionine β-synthase activity, which facilitates cysteine biosynthesis for hydrogen sulfide production, supporting gut barrier integrity and reducing bacterial translocation. This pathway also reduces circulating monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1), a marker of systemic inflammation. The combined measurement of serum flagellin and fecal abundance of R. inulinivorans and B. pseudocatenulatum provides a predictive biomarker for FET success, suggesting that targeted dietary interventions to promote beneficial Bifidobacterium species could improve reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction.

title: The Vaginal Microbiome and Reproductive Health in Adolescents and Adults
journal: Journal of pediatric and adolescent gynecology
summary:
# Summary

The vaginal microbiome is maintained in a state of health primarily through Lactobacillus dominance, which produces multiple antimicrobial compounds including lactic acid, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins that collectively inhibit pathogen growth and maintain acidic vaginal pH. Dysbiosis—a loss of this Lactobacillus-dominated state—is strongly associated with multiple adverse reproductive and gynecologic outcomes including premature birth, sexually transmitted infections, pelvic inflammatory disease, cervical cancer, and dysmenorrhea. The microbiome composition is dynamically influenced by the menstrual cycle, with menses promoting increased bacterial diversity through elevated pH and iron availability that favor anaerobic bacterial growth, while menstrual cup use paradoxically stabilizes the microbiome and reduces bacterial vaginosis risk compared to other menstrual products and douching. Multiple modifiable factors affect microbiome composition, including diet (vegetarian diets increase diversity), nutritional status (vitamins A, C, E, and β-carotene reduce bacterial vaginosis risk), contraceptive choice (oral hormonal contraceptives maintain lactobacilli while some IUDs reduce them), and lifestyle factors including exercise, BMI, sexual behavior, smoking, and psychological stress. Probiotics have demonstrated efficacy in counteracting dysbiosis and restoring microbiome homeostasis, offering a therapeutic option alongside careful selection of contraceptive and hygiene practices to maintain vaginal health.

title: Reproductive tract microbiome dysbiosis associated with gynecological diseases
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This article demonstrates that reproductive tract microbiota dysbiosis is strongly associated with endometriosis and other gynecological diseases. In healthy women, Lactobacillus dominates both the vaginal and endometrial microbiota, where it prevents pathogenic microorganism overgrowth; however, endometriosis patients exhibit significantly reduced Lactobacillus alongside elevated pathogenic bacteria including Corynebacterium, Enterobactericaea, Pseudomonas, Streptococcus, and notably Fusobacterium. Fusobacterium elevation is particularly significant as it activates transforming growth factor-β signaling and directly promotes endometriosis lesion development in mouse models. Microbiota imbalance disrupts immune homeostasis and elevates pro-inflammatory cytokines, with dysbiosis-associated bacterial lipopolysaccharides activating the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB inflammatory pathway. Importantly, therapeutic restoration of healthy vaginal microbiota through antibiotics or vaginal flora transplantation effectively treats endometriosis in mice by downregulating TLR4, MyD88, and NF-κB signaling while reducing peritoneal pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α. These findings establish microbiota restoration as a potential therapeutic strategy for endometriosis management.

title: Integrated microbiome and metabolomics analysis reveals the alleviating effect of Pediococcus acidilactici on colitis
summary:
# Summary

Pediococcus acidilactici alleviates colitis by restoring gut microbial homeostasis and reducing inflammatory cytokines. Administration of this probiotic strain reversed colitis-induced dysbiosis by restoring microbial diversity and abundance, as well as decreasing elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β) and improving intestinal barrier function. Colitis disrupts the balance of beneficial bacterial taxa including Bacteroidetes, Prevotellaceae, and Roseburia while promoting pathogenic organisms such as Turicibacter, Bilophila, and Escherichia_Shigella. Multiple metabolites including spermine, L-Arginine, and prostaglandin derivatives were identified as potential mediators of the therapeutic effect, alongside short-chain fatty acids and cholic acid that support microbial-driven host health regulation. These findings demonstrate that Pediococcus acidilactici functions as a therapeutic agent through integrated microbiota and metabolomic mechanisms to combat intestinal inflammation and restore gastrointestinal homeostasis.

title: The Gut Microbiota of the Greater Horseshoe Bat Confers Rapidly Corresponding Immune Cells in Mice
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

The gut microbiota of the Greater Horseshoe Bat (Rhinolophus ferrumequinum) rapidly regulates immune cell composition and function when transplanted into mice, demonstrating cross-species effects on immune homeostasis. The bat microbiota, characterized by high abundances of Proteobacteria and Firmicutes with lower overall diversity than mouse microbiota, stimulates natural killer cell activation and restores CD3+ T cells depleted by antibiotic treatment within seven days of transplantation. Notably, bat microbiota enriched in Enterobacteriaceae and other bacteria absent in mice—particularly Fusobacteriota—could not be fully retained in recipient mice due to genetic and dietary differences between species. These findings suggest that species-specific microbial compositions play distinct roles in shaping innate and adaptive immunity, with implications for understanding how dysbiotic microbiota alterations may impair immune regulation in conditions characterized by microbiota dysbiosis, such as endometriosis.

title: The Role of Endometrial Microbiota in Fertility and Reproductive Health: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

The endometrial microbiota plays a critical role in reproductive health and fertility, with dysbiosis linked to implantation failure, infertility, and recurrent miscarriage. Lactobacillus dominance, maintained by estrogen and progesterone during the menstrual cycle, promotes successful pregnancy outcomes, whereas dysbiosis characterized by reduction in Lactobacillus and colonization by pathogenic bacteria such as Gardnerella, Klebsiella, and Mycoplasma species increases clinical miscarriage risk and facilitates chronic endometritis. Endometrial microbiota composition dynamically changes in response to hormonal fluctuations, age, and immune responses, directly affecting endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation capability. Non-commensal microbiota disrupts the epithelial barrier integrity, restricting host defenses and allowing pathogenic organisms to access the endometrial stroma, while chronic endometritis caused by infections with Mycoplasma genitalium, E. coli, or Candida species causes recurrent implantation failure and pregnancy loss. The female reproductive tract microbiota, maintained by cellular immune responses and cytokines alongside beneficial bacteria, is essential for endometrial stability and optimal conditions for fetal development, suggesting that microbiota assessment and targeted interventions may improve reproductive outcomes in infertile and endometriosis-affected patients.

title: Gut microbiome-driven regulation of sex hormone homeostasis: a potential neuroendocrine connection
summary: This study demonstrates that the gut microbiome plays a critical regulatory role in sex hormone homeostasis through bidirectional communication with the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis. Using fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) from gonadectomized and hormone-supplemented donor mice, the researchers found that microbiota composition directly influences circulating gonadotropin levels and reproductive development, with transplant recipients of gonadectomized-associated microbiota showing paradoxically lower gonadotropins despite donor characteristics. The study identified specific bacterial taxa—including Akkermansia in males and Lachnospiraceae in females—as key nodes interconnected with HPG axis signaling, while bacterial-derived metabolites such as short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, isovalerate, and valerate) and indolepropionate mediate this neuroendocrine connection through altered metabolomic profiles. Mechanistically, bacterial beta-glucuronidase enzymes enhance enterohepatic recirculation of sex hormones by cleaving glucuronidated estrogen and testosterone, thereby modulating their bioavailability. These findings establish microbiota dysbiosis as a potential pathogenic mechanism for endometriosis and other hormone-dependent diseases, suggesting that therapeutic manipulation of the gut microbiome through probiotics or dietary interventions targeting short-chain fatty acid-producing bacteria could represent novel treatment strategies.

title: Unveiling the correlation between dietary fiber intake and endometriosis: a cross-sectional analysis of NHANES data
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional analysis of NHANES data reveals a surprising positive correlation between dietary fiber intake and endometriosis risk, with the highest fiber intake quartile (17.6–128.3 g/day) showing a 1.73-fold increased risk. Although dietary fiber is known to modulate weight, inflammation, insulin resistance, and hormonal balance through multiple mechanisms including gut microbiota composition and short-chain fatty acid production, the study's findings contradict expected protective effects from fiber-rich diets. The apparent paradox may be explained by the influence of specific fiber types and phytoestrogens on estrogen metabolism and the enterohepatic circulation, with certain vegetables like corn and cruciferous plants associated with higher endometriosis risk while citrus fruits show protective effects. Since endometriosis is a multifactorial condition involving genetic, immune, inflammatory, and hormonal dysregulation pathways, the complex relationship between dietary fiber composition, gut microbiota ecology, and systemic estrogen levels warrants further investigation to clarify whether specific fiber sources differentially affect disease risk.

title: Exploring the role of gut microbiota in antibiotic resistance and prevention
summary:
# Summary

Antibiotic use and multiple environmental stressors including physical and mental stress, radiation, gastrointestinal infections, and dietary changes all damage the beneficial gut microbiota, creating conditions favorable for colonization by multidrug-resistant bacteria and pathogenic organisms. The gut microbiota normally plays a crucial role in developing local and systemic immunity and preventing bacterial infections; therefore, antibiotic-induced dysbiosis compromises these protective functions and facilitates the spread of antibiotic resistance genes through horizontal gene transfer via mobile genetic elements, conjugative plasmids, and phage-mediated transduction. Resistant bacteria employ multiple resistance mechanisms including beta-lactamases, efflux pumps (such as MATE and Mac pumps), and biofilm formation, which protects them from antimicrobials and serves as an epicenter for horizontal gene transfer of resistance determinants like extended-spectrum beta-lactamases and carbapenemases. Pathogenic biofilm-forming organisms such as *Clostridium difficile*, *Helicobacter pylori*, and specific *E. coli* strains carrying genes like *pgaABCD* establish persistent infections through the formation of metabolically inactive persister cells that survive antibiotic treatment and repopulate when conditions improve. While beneficial bacteria such as *Bifidobacterium* and *Lactobacillus* form protective biofilms that support gut health, antibiotic treatment paradoxically increases the number of antibiotic resistance gene-carrying phages in the gut, amplifying the risk of resistance transfer among commensal and pathogenic microorganisms.

title: Changes in fecal microbiota of dairy cows with and without endometritis
summary:
# Summary

This study examined fecal microbiota composition in dairy cows with and without metritis, revealing significant dysbiosis associated with uterine inflammation. Cows with metritis showed elevated abundances of Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes phyla, with specific pathogenic genera including Olsenella, Succinivibrio, Atopobium, and members of the Lachnospiraceae family prominently increased, while healthy cows maintained higher levels of protective bacteria such as Treponema2, Ruminiclostridium9, and Caproiciproducens. The dysbiotic microbiota in affected animals correlated with upregulated genes involved in signal transduction and DNA replication, and produced elevated levels of succinic acid, a metabolite associated with endometritis. Multiple bacterial taxa, particularly Prevotella and Clostridium species, were linked to uterine infection and impaired pregnancy establishment, with several genera showing positive correlation with CXCL13, a chemokine associated with chronic endometrial inflammation. These findings suggest that gut microbiota dysbiosis directly influences intrauterine homeostasis through metabolic byproducts and excretory system pathways, positioning specific bacterial biomarkers as potential predictive indicators for metritis susceptibility and pregnancy outcomes in dairy cattle.

title: All Properties of Infertility Microbiome in a Review Article
summary:
# Summary: Microbiota Dysbiosis and Infertility

Microbiome composition plays a critical role in immunity, metabolism, and reproduction, with dysbiosis—an imbalance in microbial populations—emerging as a key factor in both male and female infertility. In women, dysbiosis of the vaginal and uterine microbiota is associated with reproductive disorders including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and implantation failure, often characterized by reduced Lactobacillus species and overgrowth of pathogenic bacteria such as Gardnerella vaginalis, Mycoplasma genitalium, and Prevotella species. Pathogenic bacterial overgrowth triggers inflammatory responses and increases reactive oxygen species production, which compromises sperm viability and motility while damaging reproductive tissues. In men, gut dysbiosis and infections with organisms like Mycoplasma genitalium reduce sperm quality and function through mechanisms including prostatitis and epididymitis. Therapeutic interventions targeting microbiome restoration—including dietary modifications, probiotic supplementation with Lactobacillus species, and fecal microbiota transfer—show promise in improving fertility outcomes and IVF success rates, suggesting that microbiome-based diagnostics and therapeutics represent an important frontier in treating infertility.

title: Translational Implications of The Gut Microbiome in Women with A Benign or Malignant Pelvic Mass
summary:
# Summary

This study examines the relationship between gut microbiota composition and pelvic pathology in women with benign or malignant pelvic masses. Women with pelvic masses demonstrated significantly reduced gut microbiota diversity and distinct microbial compositions compared to healthy controls, with elevated Proteobacteria and depleted Akkermansia genus associated with ovarian cancer. Gut dysbiosis promotes tumor growth and chemotherapy resistance in ovarian cancer through mechanisms involving intestinal epithelial dysfunction, while specific bacterial taxa including Corynebacterium, Dialister, Peptoniphilus, and Prevotella differentiate disease staging. The findings suggest that variations in microbial composition influence disease initiation, progression, and treatment response, supporting the potential use of microbiota-based tests as complementary diagnostic tools for identifying women at risk for ovarian malignancy and personalizing therapeutic strategies.

title: The Gut–Endometrium Axis: Exploring the Role of Microbiome in the Pathogenesis and Treatment of Endometrial Cancer—A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

The gut and endometrial microbiomes significantly influence endometrial cancer risk through multiple interconnected pathways involving systemic and local inflammation, immune dysregulation, and estrogen metabolism. Dysbiosis—an imbalance in microbial composition—promotes chronic inflammation and hormonal disruptions that create a pro-tumorigenic environment, while the healthy microbiome normally maintains reproductive immune homeostasis through metabolite production and immune modulation. Unopposed estrogen exposure, obesity, diabetes, and conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome drive endometrial cancer development partly through dysbiosis-mediated mechanisms, with obesity particularly elevating risk via adipose tissue aromatase-dependent estrone production. Emerging microbiota-based therapeutic interventions including probiotics, prebiotics, fecal microbiota transplantation, and dietary modifications such as Mediterranean diet consumption show promise in restoring eubiosis and reducing cancer risk. These findings underscore the critical gut–endometrium axis in endometrial cancer pathogenesis and suggest that microbiota-targeted strategies may complement conventional endometrial cancer prevention and treatment approaches.

title: A PRISMA Systematic Review of Sexual Dysfunction and Probiotics with Pathophysiological Mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review demonstrates that probiotics improve sexual function and fertility outcomes through modulation of the gut microbiota and the gut-brain axis. Specific probiotic strains such as Lactobacillus acidophilus, Bifidobacterium bifidus, and Bifidobacterium longum promote microbiome balance and enhance sexual health metrics including sperm motility, viability, and female sexual satisfaction, with combined treatments like Lactofem plus Letrozole or SSRIs showing particularly strong effects on pregnancy rates and sexual function indices. The underlying mechanisms involve probiotics reducing systemic inflammation and gut permeability, enhancing serotonin production, regulating hormonal balance through modulation of estrogen and testosterone, and restoring favorable LH-to-FSH ratios. Notably, dysbiosis exacerbates sexual dysfunction through increased inflammation and hormonal imbalances, while probiotics counter these effects with antioxidant properties and immune modulation, offering a safer alternative to hormone replacement therapy with minimal adverse effects. These findings suggest that probiotic supplementation represents a promising adjunctive therapeutic strategy for addressing sexual dysfunction, infertility, and menopausal symptoms through restoration of microbiota-mediated endocrine and neurological signaling.

title: A Potential Link Between Oral Microbiota and Female Reproductive Health
summary:
# Summary

This review establishes a significant link between oral microbiota dysbiosis and female reproductive health, with implications for endometriosis and other fertility disorders. Oral dysbiosis is associated with multiple inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and adverse pregnancy outcomes such as miscarriage, preterm delivery, and low birth weight. The dysbiosis involves pathogenic bacterial complexes, particularly the red complex (comprising *Porphyromonas gingivalis*, *Treponema denticola*, and *Tannerella forsythia*) and orange complex species, which produce pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha that promote systemic inflammation. Pregnancy itself induces oral dysbiosis through altered hormonal environments where estrogen and progesterone preferentially support growth of *Prevotella* species and pathogenic bacteria, creating a bidirectional relationship between reproductive hormones and oral microbiota composition. The systemic inflammation generated by oral dysbiosis can impair fertility by reducing implantation success and extending time to conception, while periodontitis specifically is associated with conception failure. These findings suggest that oral health maintenance may represent an underappreciated therapeutic target for improving reproductive outcomes in women with endometriosis and related conditions.

title: Lactobacillus Genus Complex Probiotic-Induced Changes on the Equine Clitoral Microbiome
summary:
# Summary

Dysbiosis of the equine lower reproductive tract contributes to reproductive diseases including endometritis and placentitis, with the clitoris serving as a potential reservoir for pathogenic bacteria such as Taylorella equigenitalis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Klebsiella pneumoniae. Local application of Lactobacillus genus complex (LGC) probiotic gel dynamically altered the clitoral microbiome by increasing Lactobacillus species at 12 hours while simultaneously decreasing Desulfobacterota and Mobiluncus species, though these shifts were partially transient. The LGC treatment altered beta diversity without affecting alpha diversity and did not interfere with fertility in healthy mares, which achieved an 85% pregnancy rate following treatment. These findings suggest that LGC probiotics may help restore eubiotic conditions in the lower reproductive tract by inhibiting growth of pathogenic species such as Streptococcus equi subspecies zooepidemicus and Escherichia coli, thereby potentially reducing the risk of upper reproductive tract colonization and associated endometrial disease.

title: A nomogram prediction model for embryo implantation outcomes based on the cervical microbiota of the infertile patients during IVF-FET
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a nomogram prediction model using cervical microbiota composition to predict embryo implantation outcomes in infertile women undergoing IVF-FET procedures. The research identified specific bacterial taxa—particularly Halomonas stevensii, Prevotella copri, and Veillonella—that were significantly enriched in non-pregnant patients and emerged as adverse predictors of implantation failure. In contrast, Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota were associated with higher clinical pregnancy rates, as Lactobacillus species produce lactic acid and create a protective barrier against pathogenic bacteria while beneficial strains like L. crispatus avoid activating pro-inflammatory NF-κB and IL-8 responses. The study demonstrates that microbiota dysbiosis—characterized by reduced Lactobacillus and increased pathogenic bacteria—contributes to embryo implantation failure through both direct competitive effects and inflammatory mechanisms, establishing cervical microbiota composition as a novel predictive biomarker for reproductive outcomes.

title: Gut microbiome in patients with early-stage and late-stage endometriosis
summary: This study reveals distinct gut microbiota signatures in early-stage versus late-stage endometriosis, with significant implications for disease progression and symptom severity. Early-stage endometriosis is characterized by a pro-inflammatory state with elevated macrophages and enriched bacterial genera like Saccharofermentans, while late-stage disease shifts toward immune tolerance with increased M2 macrophages, regulatory T cells, and enriched taxa including Bartonella and Gilliamella, alongside reduced beneficial bacteria like Bacteroides and Prevotella. The steroid biosynthesis pathway is suppressed in late-stage endometriosis, and dysmenorrhea severity is linked to specific microbial traits, with antibiotic treatment demonstrating efficacy in reducing both inflammation and pain symptoms. IL-27 accumulation in ectopic lesions drives immune suppression by inhibiting Th17 differentiation and promoting IL-10 production, establishing an immune tolerance pattern critical to disease progression. Notably, the gut microbiota composition emerges as a more meaningful diagnostic marker than cervical mucus and shows significant associations with dysmenorrhea, suggesting microbiota-mediated pain perception mechanisms involving taxa like Prevotella and Staphylococcus. These findings establish the gut microbiota as a central player in endometriosis pathogenesis and progression, offering potential targets for microbiota-based early diagnosis and therapeutic interventions.

title: Fiber-type prebiotics and gynecological and breast cancers risk: the PrebiotiCa study
summary:
# Summary

The PrebiotiCa study investigates how dietary fiber and prebiotics influence hormone-related cancer risk through modulation of gut microbiota and estrogen metabolism. The gut microbiota, particularly through bacterial β-glucuronidase enzyme and the estrobolome, plays a central role in estrogen metabolism by converting conjugated estrogens to their active forms for reabsorption, with elevated circulating estrogen levels directly associated with breast, endometrial, and ovarian cancer risk. While general fiber and plant-based diets were associated with reduced serum estrogen levels, specific prebiotic fiber types showed paradoxical results: higher nystose intake correlated with increased breast and ovarian cancer risk, whereas high 1F-β-fructofuranosylnystose intake was inversely associated with ovarian cancer risk. Most common prebiotics including inulin-type fructans, kestose, raffinose, and stachyose showed no significant associations with the three cancer types, and high starch intake was associated with increased breast and ovarian cancer risks. These findings suggest that the relationship between fiber-type prebiotics and hormone-related cancers is complex and specific to individual prebiotic compounds, with potential implications for personalized dietary recommendations in cancer prevention.

title: Deciphering the causality of gut microbiota, circulating metabolites and heart failure: a mediation mendelian
summary: Using Mendelian randomization analysis, this study reveals causal relationships between gut microbiota composition, circulating metabolites, and heart failure development. Nine microbial groups and fifteen metabolites showed causal associations with heart failure, with eight metabolites conferring protective effects. Notably, the metabolite campesterol mediated the increased heart failure risk from gut dysbiosis through lipid metabolism pathways, while specific bacterial taxa such as Prevotella and Alistipes putredinis increased risk, whereas Ruminococcus callidus reduced risk. The study identified kynurenine as a pathogenic metabolite that promotes cardiac hypertrophy and fibrosis, and demonstrated that probiotic modulation reduces kynurenine levels, suggesting a potential therapeutic intervention. Additionally, other disease-associated metabolites like imidazole propionate and phenylacetylglutamine correlated with reduced ejection fraction and increased mortality, while protective metabolites including tyramine O-sulfate and specific lipid species decreased heart failure risk. These findings establish a mechanistic framework whereby dysbiotic microbiota alter metabolic pathways to influence cardiac pathophysiology.

title: Dietary index for gut microbiota and its inverse association with female infertility: evidence from NHANES 2013–2018
summary:
# Summary

This NHANES-based study demonstrates that a higher Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota (DI-GM) score is inversely associated with female infertility, with participants achieving scores of 6 or higher showing a 40% lower infertility risk. The analysis reveals that gut dysbiosis—characterized by elevated pathogenic bacteria such as UBA1819, Eisenbergiella, Hungatella, and Erysipelatoclostridium—is linked to endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and obesity, while beneficial bacteria including Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Prevotella, and Bacteroides improve fertility through multiple mechanisms including estrogen and progesterone metabolism, gut barrier function, and oxidative stress reduction. Specific foods enhance fertility-related microbiota composition: fermented dairy products and green tea promote Lactobacillus growth, chickpeas and soy enrich Bifidobacterium populations, whole grains boost Prevotella, and cranberries plus broccoli increase Bacteroides. Short-chain fatty acids, particularly butyrate produced from dietary fiber fermentation, enhance the endometrial microenvironment and embryo implantation rates. The association between DI-GM and reduced infertility was notably stronger in women with lower education levels and those with coronary heart disease, suggesting that dietary microbiota modulation offers a particularly impactful intervention strategy for vulnerable populations.

title: Expert Opinion on the Use of Probiotics in Recurrent Pregnancy Loss
summary: Recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) is a multifactorial condition involving genetic, endocrine, immunological, and microbiological factors, with vaginal and endometrial microbiome dysbiosis emerging as significant contributors. Vaginal dysbiosis characterized by reduced Lactobacillus dominance is associated with early pregnancy loss, preterm birth, and RPL, while endometrial dysbiosis with elevated pathogenic bacteria such as Acinetobacter and Anaerobacillus correlates with reduced implantation and live birth rates. Probiotic supplementation, particularly Lactobacillus species like L. acidophilus and L. rhamnosus, shows promise in treating RPL by improving embryo implantation, reducing miscarriage risk, and suppressing inflammation through multiple mechanisms including production of short-chain fatty acids, bacteriocins, and immune modulation via gut-associated lymphoid tissue. These beneficial bacteria restore barrier function and maintain balanced microbiota composition, counteracting the dysbiotic state. Additional RPL risk factors include advanced maternal age, thyroid disease, polycystic ovarian syndrome with insulin resistance, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, uterine anomalies, and lifestyle factors such as alcohol and caffeine consumption. The expert opinion supports probiotics as a complementary therapeutic strategy for managing RPL through microbiota restoration and immune homeostasis.

title: Expert Opinion: Place in Therapy of Probiotics in Infertility and Recurrent Implantation Failure
summary: Microbial dysbiosis in the reproductive tract, characterized by loss of Lactobacillus dominance, is strongly associated with recurrent implantation failure, chronic endometritis, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage and preterm birth. The dysbiotic state triggers elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines and inappropriate immune responses that impair embryo implantation and endometrial receptivity. Lactobacillus-based probiotics restore microbial equilibrium and reduce inflammation by maintaining an acidic vaginal environment that inhibits pathogenic bacteria, supports sperm survival and motility, and promotes a balanced immune response. Studies demonstrate that women with higher proportions of Lactobacillus in their endometrial microbiota achieve significantly better implantation rates, pregnancy rates, and live birth outcomes compared to those with dysbiotic profiles. The expert consensus supports Lactobacillus-based oral probiotic supplementation as a promising therapeutic approach to correct endometrial and vaginal dysbiosis, enhance endometrial health, and improve fertility outcomes in patients with recurrent implantation failure.

title: Microbial and Immune Landscape of Malignant Ascites: Insights from Gut, Bladder, and Ascitic Fluid Analyses
summary: Malignant ascites, a hallmark of advanced cancer characterized by poor prognosis, creates a profoundly immunosuppressive microenvironment with reduced T-cell and NK cell populations. The development of malignant ascites involves multiple mechanisms including lymphatic obstruction, RAAS-mediated sodium retention, and vascular permeability changes driven by VEGF, IL-2, and TNF-alpha. Notably, although ascitic fluid itself remains largely sterile with minimal bacterial load, distinct microbial signatures in the gut and bladder microbiomes associate with peritoneal metastasis, with Clostridia and Gammaproteobacteria enriched in metastatic patients while beneficial Bacilli including Lactobacillus predominate in non-metastatic individuals. The gut microbiome significantly influences the tumor microenvironment by modulating epithelial-mesenchymal transition and the gut-vascular barrier, while Clostridia-derived short-chain fatty acids provide anti-inflammatory protection and Gammaproteobacteria reduce chemotherapy efficacy. Conversely, bladder microbiome diversity decreases with disease progression and influences local inflammation and host immunity. These findings underscore how dysbiotic microbial patterns, particularly in colorectal cancer patients with peritoneal metastasis, contribute to cancer progression and the immunosuppressive ascitic landscape despite the sterility of ascitic fluid itself.

title: Microbiome–Maternal Tract Interactions in Women with Recurrent Implantation Failure
summary: Women with recurrent implantation failure (RIF) exhibit a dysbiotic uterine and vaginal microbiome characterized by reduced Lactobacillus species and elevated pathogenic bacteria including Atopobium, Gardnerella, Prevotella, Pseudomonas, and Streptococcus. Lactobacillus species, particularly Lactobacillus crispatus, gasseri, jensenii, and iners, produce protective antimicrobial compounds—lactic acid, bacteriocins, and hydrogen peroxide—that maintain reproductive tract health; their depletion compromises this barrier function. A Lactobacillus-dominant vaginal microbiota (≥90%) is associated with significantly higher implantation and pregnancy rates, while pathogenic bacteria such as Gardnerella vaginalis induce inflammatory cytokines and trigger bacterial vaginosis, endometritis, and pelvic inflammatory disease that impair embryo quality and implantation success. Notably, seminal microbiota dynamically interact with the vagino-uterine microbiome to influence fertilization and embryo implantation, with specific bacterial signatures correlating with sperm parameters. Probiotic interventions using Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus show promise in restoring eubiotic conditions and improving RIF outcomes, suggesting that microbiota-targeted therapeutics may enhance assisted reproductive success.

title: Investigating Correlation Between Gut Microbiota and Rheumatoid Arthritis Subtypes by Mendelian Randomization
summary: This Mendelian randomization study reveals that gut microbiota composition causally influences rheumatoid arthritis susceptibility and disease phenotype through distinct bacterial taxa associated with seronegative and seropositive RA subtypes. Dysbiosis, particularly elevated Prevotella copri, contributes to RA pathogenesis by triggering pro-inflammatory TH17 responses and immune complex formation, while protective bacteria such as Lachnospira, Roseburia, Barnesiella, and Slackia reduce seropositive RA risk through immune tolerance mechanisms. Conversely, pathogenic taxa including Ruminococcus gauvreauii and Ruminococcaceae UCG-002 increase seropositive RA susceptibility, potentially through T-cell polarization and dysregulation of butyrate-mediated immune regulation, whereas Prevotella 9, Sutterella, and Christensenellaceae R.7 show protective associations against seronegative RA. The study demonstrates that Prevotella species exhibit divergent immunological effects depending on strain identity, with Prevotella histicola suppressing inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-2, IL-17) and promoting regulatory T cells, while Prevotella copri drives pathogenic TH1/TH17 responses. These findings establish specific microbiota-mediated mechanisms linking intestinal dysbiosis to RA pathogenesis and suggest that microbiota composition could serve as a biomarker for disease subtype classification and personalized therapeutic intervention.

title: Estrogen-Gut-Brain Axis: Examining the Role of Combined Oral Contraceptives on Mental Health Through Their Impact on the Gut Microbiome
summary:
# Summary

Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) treat endometriosis and other gynecological conditions but exert complex effects on multiple interconnected biological systems. COCs alter the gut microbiome composition, reducing bacterial diversity and richness while disrupting the estrobolome—the specific microbial population that regulates estrogen metabolism through beta-glucuronidase enzyme activity. These microbiota changes increase intestinal permeability and are associated with gastrointestinal complications, inflammatory bowel disease, and Crohn's disease. Beyond gastrointestinal effects, COCs affect the gut-brain axis through multiple mechanisms: progestins upregulate monoamine oxidase, which degrades serotonin and may contribute to depressive symptoms and anxiety, while also reducing estradiol receptor expression. The resulting dysbiosis has been linked to metabolic diseases, depression, and obesity, though probiotic genera such as Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium show promise in reversing these effects by suppressing inflammation and restoring neurotransmitter levels. These findings highlight important gaps in understanding how COCs' therapeutic benefits for endometriosis must be weighed against potential mental health and gastrointestinal risks mediated through the estrogen-gut-brain axis.

title: Characteristics and dynamic changes of gut microbiota in Mongolian horses and Guizhou horses
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes and compares the gut microbiota composition of two horse breeds—Guizhou horses and Mongolian horses—revealing significant differences in bacterial and fungal diversity. Guizhou horses exhibited higher bacterial diversity and fungal abundance, with dominant phyla including Firmicutes, Bacteroidota, and Verrucomicrobiota, while Mongolian horses showed enrichment of genera such as Bacillus and Ligilactobacillus. The gut microbiota plays critical roles in host digestion, immunity, metabolism, intestinal barrier function, and epithelial cell differentiation through production of short-chain fatty acids, digestive enzymes, vitamins, and antimicrobial peptides. Intestinal homeostasis depends on a stabilized microbiota, and dysbiosis caused by external factors such as antibiotics, heavy metals, pesticides, and microplastics is associated with gastrointestinal diseases, diabetes, obesity, and neurological conditions like Parkinson's disease. Host genetics significantly influence microbiota composition and structure, contributing to breed-specific differences observed between these horse populations, with key bacterial taxa showing positive interactions that likely support functional stability within each host's ecosystem.

title: Broadening horizons: microbiota as a novel biomarker and potential treatment for endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is closely associated with microbial dysbiosis across multiple body sites, including the gut, vagina, cervix, and endometrium, which plays a pivotal role in disease initiation and progression. Dysbiotic microbiota dysregulate immune function and elevate proinflammatory factors, establishing chronic inflammation that promotes adhesion formation and angiogenesis in the pelvic cavity. Specific dysbiotic signatures include reduced Lactobacillus and elevated pathogenic bacteria (Gardnerella, Atopobium, Actinobacteria) in the vagina, along with increased Firmicutes-to-Bacteroidetes ratios and intestinal dysbiosis that compromise epithelial barrier integrity. Intestinal dysbiosis damages the mucosal barrier and impairs the gut-brain axis, increasing inflammatory factor expression and allowing lipopolysaccharides to disrupt both immune and endocrine systems. Microbial metabolites, particularly lactic acid from Lactobacillus, directly induce functional alterations in endometrial cells; loss of this protective effect facilitates pathogenic bacterial proliferation and inflammatory responses. These microbiota alterations represent novel biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis and progression assessment, offering potential therapeutic targets through microbiome restoration strategies.

title: Association between dietary index for gut microbiota and female infertility: a cross-sectional NHANES 2013–2020
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study from NHANES 2013–2020 demonstrates that higher dietary index for gut microbiota (DI-GM) scores—characterized by consumption of fermented dairy, whole grains, avocados, broccoli, coffee, and green tea while limiting red meat, processed meat, and refined grains—are significantly associated with reduced female infertility risk. The protective mechanism operates through multiple interconnected pathways: a microbiota-friendly diet increases microbial diversity and β-glucuronidase production, which regulates estrogen enterohepatic circulation and maintains adequate circulating estrogen levels necessary for reproductive function. Additionally, beneficial gut bacteria produce short-chain fatty acids that regulate the hypothalamic–pituitary–ovarian axis, reduce systemic inflammation, and enhance metabolic health, while gut dysbiosis impairs these processes and disrupts estrogen secretion and follicular development. Body mass index partially mediates the DI-GM–infertility association, accounting for approximately 6% of the effect. These findings suggest that dietary optimization to support healthy gut microbiota composition represents a modifiable lifestyle intervention for improving female fertility outcomes.

title: Investigation of the female genital tract microbiome and its association with hydrosalpinx in women undergoing salpingectomy
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the female genital tract microbiome in women with hydrosalpinx undergoing salpingectomy, revealing distinct microbial dysbiosis associated with tubal pathology. Women with hydrosalpinx exhibited a dysbiotic vaginal microbiome characterized by reduced lactobacilli and increased levels of pathogenic bacteria including Prevotella, Atopobium, and Gardnerella—organisms typically associated with bacterial vaginosis. The vaginal microbiota serves as the primary source for bacterial colonization of the fallopian tubes through translocation, and dysbiotic compositions have been linked to decreased pregnancy and implantation rates in infertility contexts. Notably, tubal occlusion in hydrosalpinx appears to limit bacterial presence within the fallopian tubes, suggesting that pathogenic bacteria may contribute to the negative fertility effects of stagnant tubal fluid. Bacterial vaginosis itself is associated with multiple reproductive complications including pelvic inflammatory disease, preterm labor, and increased sexually transmitted infection risk, establishing a clear mechanistic link between microbiota dysbiosis and reproductive pathology in women with hydrosalpinx.

title: Metabolic disorders in polycystic ovary syndrome: from gut microbiota biodiversity to clinical intervention
summary:
# Summary

This review elucidates the critical role of gut microbiota dysbiosis in the pathogenesis of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a prevalent endocrine disorder characterized by elevated androgens, menstrual irregularities, and ovulatory dysfunction. PCOS patients exhibit reduced microbial alpha-diversity and altered bacterial composition, including elevated levels of pro-inflammatory phyla (Fusobacteria, Proteobacteria, and Bacteroidetes) and reduced beneficial bacteria (Firmicutes and Tenericutes), which collectively promote insulin resistance, hormonal imbalances, and bile acid metabolic disorders. Environmental factors including high-fat diets and sedentary lifestyles contribute to microbiota disturbance, while dietary interventions such as high-fiber and Mediterranean diets, combined with physical activity, can restore microbial diversity and reduce PCOS severity. The intestinal barrier dysfunction in PCOS patients, involving impaired tight junctions and reduced mucus secretion, allows pathogenic bacteria to proliferate while beneficial bacteria like lactobacilli and bifidobacteria become depleted. Beyond PCOS, dysbiotic microbiota are also associated with other gynecological conditions including endometriosis, bacterial vaginosis, and cervical cancer, suggesting that microbiota-targeted interventions may represent a novel therapeutic strategy for managing these interconnected metabolic and reproductive disorders.

title: Analysis of the Relationship Between Cervical Cancer Progression and the Microbiome
summary:
# Summary

Cervical cancer is strongly associated with profound microbiota dysbiosis characterized by loss of protective Lactobacillus and Firmicutes while pathogenic bacteria including Klebsiella, Rhodococcus, Enterobacter, and Proteobacteria become elevated. Lactobacillus normally suppresses pathogenic microorganism growth and upregulates E-cadherin expression, which inhibits cancer cell migration and appears to prevent high-risk HPV persistence. The dysbiotic microenvironment facilitates persistent HR-HPV infection and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia progression through multiple mechanisms, including production of microbial metabolites and toxins that damage epithelial tissue, modulation of host immune responses, and promotion of chronic inflammation. While overall microbial diversity indices remain similar between cancer patients and controls, beta diversity analysis reveals significantly distinct community structures. These findings establish the cervical microbiome as a key factor in cervical cancer pathogenesis and suggest that dysbiosis-induced disruption of microbial equilibrium contributes substantially to tumor development and progression.

title: Could a Mediterranean Diet Modulate Alzheimer’s Disease Progression? The Role of Gut Microbiota and Metabolite Signatures in Neurodegeneration
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how gut microbiota dysbiosis contributes to Alzheimer's disease progression through the gut-brain axis, with particular emphasis on microbial metabolite signatures. In healthy states, beneficial bacteria such as Faecalibacterium, Bifidobacterium, and Akkermansia produce short-chain fatty acids (particularly butyrate), which strengthen blood-brain barrier integrity, suppress pro-inflammatory pathways via NF-κB inhibition, and enhance microglial clearance of amyloid-β. Conversely, dysbiosis increases pathogenic bacteria like Proteobacteria and Bacteroides that produce lipopolysaccharides, triggering systemic and neuroinflammation while compromising barrier function. Additionally, dysbiosis shifts tryptophan metabolism away from neuroprotective serotonin and indole derivatives toward neurotoxic kynurenine pathway metabolites like quinolinic acid, which induces excitotoxicity. The article proposes that Mediterranean diet-derived bioactive compounds—prebiotics, omega-3 fatty acids, and polyphenols—along with microbiome-targeted interventions such as probiotics and fecal microbiota transplantation, may mitigate neurodegeneration by restoring beneficial microbiota and metabolite profiles that support cognitive health.

title: Identification and impact of microbiota-derived metabolites in ascites of ovarian and gastrointestinal cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dysbiotic changes in the gut microbiota contribute significantly to ascites formation in advanced ovarian and gastrointestinal cancers through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Bacterial translocation resulting from increased intestinal permeability promotes inflammation and ascites accumulation, while microbiota-derived metabolites show distinct signatures associated with disease progression and metastasis. Stage IV ovarian cancer patients exhibited elevated levels of eleven microbiota-derived metabolites including indole, 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid, and various others, while showing reduced levels of protective metabolites such as naringenin, phenol, and benzyl alcohol. Notably, butyrate produced by healthy gut bacteria can inhibit the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system to reduce fluid retention, suggesting that microbiota composition directly influences ascites pathogenesis. These metabolites correlate with inflammatory markers like IL-10 and MCP-1, indicating they actively shape the tumor microenvironment and promote metastasis. The findings reveal that microbiota-derived metabolites serve as both mechanistic drivers and potential biomarkers of cancer progression, highlighting the microbiota as a therapeutic target for managing malignant ascites.

title: Association between endometriosis and gut microbiota: systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates a significant association between gut microbiota dysbiosis and endometriosis, with women with endometriosis showing reduced microbial diversity compared to controls as measured by the Shannon Index. The study identifies specific compositional changes in endometriosis-associated microbiota, including elevated levels of Prevotella, Blautia, and Firmicutes, alongside reduced abundances of protective bacteria such as Bifidobacterium and Lachnospira. Dysbiosis contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms: disruption of immune function and triggering of chronic inflammation that promotes cellular adhesion, angiogenesis, and fibrosis; dysregulation of estrogen metabolism via altered bacterial glucuronidase activity; and increased intestinal permeability allowing bacterial endotoxin translocation. The findings highlight gut microbiota as a crucial modulator of key biological processes underlying endometriosis, including immunity, inflammation, and estrogen metabolism, with dysbiosis potentially serving as both a contributing factor and therapeutic target in this complex gynecological disorder.

title: Gut microbiota and ovarian diseases: a new therapeutic perspective
summary:
# Summary

Dysbiosis of the gut microbiota is strongly associated with multiple ovarian diseases, including premature ovarian insufficiency, polycystic ovary syndrome, and ovarian tumors, with evidence suggesting a potential causal relationship from animal studies. The healthy gut microbiota is essential for immune development, infection prevention, nutrient supply, and maintaining the gut-brain axis through vagal and immune signaling. Dysbiotic alterations cause systemic inflammation and increased gut permeability, characterized by reduced beneficial bacteria (Bifidobacteria, Lactobacillus, Actinobacteria) and elevated pathogenic taxa (Firmicutes, Clostridium, Mycobacterium), with specific bacterial compositions correlating with hormonal dysregulation in ovarian diseases. Several microbiota-targeting interventions show therapeutic promise: lycium barbarum polysaccharide and fisetin restore beneficial bacteria while reducing inflammatory markers and improving follicle development and estrous cycles; electroacupuncture increases microbiota diversity and beneficial Actinobacteria while suppressing pathogenic genera like Alistipes. These findings establish gut microbiota modulation as a novel therapeutic strategy for restoring reproductive function in ovarian diseases through manipulation of microbial composition and immune homeostasis.

title: The microbiome in endometrial cancer: vaginal milieu matters
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that uterine and vaginal microbiota composition differs significantly between endometrial cancer (EC), endometrial hyperplasia (EH), and healthy controls, with distinct bacterial signatures associated with disease progression. While EH is characterized by elevated Lactobacillus, Enterococcus, and Bifidobacterium species, EC patients show enrichment of pathogenic anaerobes including Porphyromonas, Prevotella, Streptococcus, Moryella, and Atopobium species. Several pathogenic bacteria, particularly Atopobium vaginae and Porphyromonas somerae, induce pro-inflammatory cytokine production that contributes to EC development, while Prevotella promotes fibrin degradation and biofilm formation, potentially compromising epithelial barrier integrity. Multiple bacterial genera enriched in EC patients, including Porphyromonas, Prevotella, and Streptococcus, possess genes involved in estrogen metabolism, suggesting the microbiota may interact with estrogen signaling—a critical risk factor for unopposed estrogen-driven Type 1 EC tumors. These findings support the emerging role of dysbiotic microbiota in EC tumorogenesis and suggest that vaginal microbial signatures could serve as non-invasive biomarkers for identifying EC risk and disease severity.

title: Navigating complexities of polymorphic microbiomes in endometrial cancer
summary: Endometrial cancer is characterized by marked dysbiosis of both vaginal and rectal microbiota, featuring depletion of protective Lactobacillus species and enrichment of pathogenic bacteria including Anaerococcus, Porphyromonas, and Peptoniphilus. Multiple specific bacterial taxa show significantly altered abundance in endometrial cancer patients compared to benign controls, with Limosilactobacillus sp. depletion and Lachnospiraceae sp. enrichment remaining significant even after adjustment for confounders. The dysbiotic microbiota pattern is linked to dysregulation of estrogen metabolism and microbial metabolic pathways involving amino acid and complex carbohydrate metabolism, processes known to influence cancer development. Postmenopausal status and elevated body mass index are associated with enrichment of pathogenic bacteria, suggesting these factors may contribute to cancer-associated dysbiosis. The loss of Lactobacillus dominance in endometrial cancer patients represents a fundamental shift in vaginal and rectal homeostasis, with the degree of dysbiosis potentially correlating with cancer severity, as Lactobacillus depletion appears more pronounced in aggressive cancer subtypes.

title: Exploring the impact of the liver-intestine-brain axis on brain function in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease
summary: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) disrupts the liver-intestine-brain axis through gut microbiota dysbiosis, characterized by elevated Bacteroides and reduced beneficial bacteria like Firmicutes and Clostridia, which increases intestinal permeability and allows endotoxin entry into the liver. High-fat diet-induced NAFLD leads to elevated deoxycholic acid (DCA) production by dysbiotic bacteria, which accumulates in NAFLD mice and triggers brain dysfunction by inhibiting the Hpgd gene in microglial cells, thereby activating the JAK2/STAT3 pathway. This pathway activation causes neuroinflammation, mitochondrial autophagy impairment, neuronal apoptosis, and reduced neuronal action potentials, manifesting as cognitive impairments, learning and memory deficits, depressive-like behavior, and motor dysfunction. Dysregulated metabolic pathways in NAFLD, particularly purine metabolism and glycine/serine/glutathione metabolism, further contribute to disease progression and systemic complications including metabolic syndrome and cardiovascular dysfunction. Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) effectively ameliorates both liver and brain dysfunction, suggesting that restoring healthy microbiota composition and reducing DCA-mediated neuroinflammation could serve as a therapeutic strategy for NAFLD-associated neurological complications.

title: Meta-Analysis of 16S rRNA Sequencing Reveals Altered Fecal but Not Vaginal Microbial Composition and Function in Women with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis of 16S rRNA sequencing data reveals that fecal microbiota composition and function are significantly altered in women with endometriosis, whereas vaginal microbiota show no meaningful differences between affected and healthy women. The fecal microbiome dysbiosis in endometriosis is characterized by increased abundance of genera including Anaerococcus, Fenollaria, Lactobacillus, and Peptoniphilus, alongside reduced populations of protective bacteria such as Alloprevotella, Succinivibrio, and Acinetobacter. These differentially abundant fecal bacteria can accurately predict disease status and are quantified by a microbial endometriosis index (MEI) that correlates with age. Functionally, the altered fecal microbiota in endometriosis display enrichment in proinflammatory pathways including proteasome, cellular adhesion, cellular communication, and metabolism, which connect dysbiosis to the disease's known pathogenic mechanisms involving inflammatory cytokines, angiogenesis, and ectopic implantation. The findings support a central role for oral-gut axis dysbiosis in endometriosis pathogenesis, providing mechanistic insight into how microbial alterations contribute to the condition's gastrointestinal symptoms and systemic inflammation.

title: The Role of the Gut Microbiota in Female Reproductive and Gynecological Health: Insights into Endometrial Signaling Pathways
summary:
# Summary

The gut microbiota functions as a critical systemic regulator of female reproductive health, exerting control over metabolism, immune function, and endocrine signaling that directly impacts endometrial function. Microbial-derived metabolites—including short-chain fatty acids, bile acids, and tryptophan catabolites—modulate immune tolerance, epithelial integrity, and inflammatory tone through regulation of T cell differentiation and cytokine expression. The estrobolome, a microbial gene repertoire controlling estrogen metabolism, mediates estrogen recycling via bacterial β-glucuronidase activity, and dysbiosis impairs this process leading to systemic estrogen dysregulation. Dysbiosis contributes to multiple gynecological disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, infertility, and adverse pregnancy outcomes through mechanisms involving chronic inflammation, hormonal dysregulation, and increased intestinal permeability.
