Environmental factors such as psychological stress, endocrine-disrupting chemicals, and delivery mode alter microbial composition and function, with specific genetic variants (FUT2, NOD2, CARD9, LCT) influencing beneficial bacterial abundance. These findings establish the gut–endometrial axis as a fundamental physiological interface where dysbiosis actively participates in endometriosis pathogenesis through disrupted estrogen metabolism and compromised mucosal immunity.

title: The Multifactorial Pathogenesis of Endometriosis: A Narrative Review Integrating Hormonal, Immune, and Microbiome Aspects
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder characterized by ectopic growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, causing dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility in up to 50% of affected women. Recent evidence demonstrates that gut microbiota dysbiosis plays a critical role in disease pathogenesis, with endometriosis patients exhibiting reduced beneficial bacteria (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Ruminococcaceae, Lachnospira) and elevated pro-inflammatory taxa (Escherichia/Shigella, Streptococcus, Bacteroides), along with diminished alpha and beta diversity. The dysbiotic microbiota creates a pro-inflammatory environment through multiple mechanisms: lipopolysaccharides from gram-negative bacteria activate TLR4/NF-κB signaling, dysbiotic alterations disrupt estrogen metabolism via the estrobolome leading to elevated systemic estrogen and prolonged inflammation, and reduced microbial diversity impairs immune homeostasis. These interconnected hormonal, microbial, and immune dysregulations collectively promote ectopic endometrial tissue proliferation and disease progression, with microbial diversity metrics showing potential as biomarkers for disease severity.

title: The Relationship Between Gut Microbiota and Recurrent Spontaneous Abortion
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbiota dysbiosis is significantly associated with recurrent spontaneous abortion through multiple interconnected immunological and metabolic mechanisms. Normal gut microbiota produces beneficial metabolites including short-chain fatty acids and B vitamins while maintaining immune homeostasis, but dysbiosis shifts the microbial composition—reducing protective bacteria like Lactobacillus, Bacteroidetes, and Roseburia while elevating pathogenic species such as Klebsiella and Eubacterium—leading to increased intestinal permeability and translocation of lipopolysaccharides. These alterations trigger a pathological Th1-dominated immune response through toll-like receptor 4 activation and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, disrupting the normal Th2-biased immune tolerance required for successful pregnancy and trophoblastic invasion. Specific bacterial taxa including Coprococcus3 and Odoribacter are inversely correlated with miscarriage risk, while beneficial bacteria like Bacillus shortis and Lactobacillus species promote protective immune regulation through SOCS3 induction and short-chain fatty acid-mediated IL-10 and TGF-β production. Additionally, endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome—conditions associated with subfertility—are characterized by similar gut microbiota alterations, suggesting dysbiosis represents a common pathogenic mechanism linking microbiota dysfunction to impaired reproductive outcomes.

title: Cold- and hot-classified botanical drugs differentially modulate gut microbiota: linking TCM emic classification to microbial ecology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) classification of botanical drugs correlates with distinct microbiota-modulating properties that reverse antibiotic-induced dysbiosis. Ceftriaxone sodium causes severe gut microbiota dysbiosis, reducing microbial diversity and stability while disrupting metabolic function. Cold-classified botanical drugs (Coptis chinensis, Rheum palmatum, Scutellaria baicalensis, and Senna alexandrina), enriched in alkaloids, selectively promote anti-inflammatory bacteria including Akkermansia and Bifidobacterium while reducing TNF-α levels. Conversely, hot-classified drugs (Panax ginseng, Astragalus membranaceus, Angelica sinensis, and Codonopsis pilosula), enriched in saponins, preferentially enhance metabolic-modulating bacteria such as Clostridia and Eubacterium coprostanoligenes while improving lipid profiles. By day twenty, all tested botanical drugs restored microbial diversity, Shannon index, and Chao index metrics while stabilizing dominant genera including Lactobacillus. These findings provide microbiota-driven validation of TCM's empirical classification system, linking plant constituents to specific bacterial communities and demonstrating differential therapeutic mechanisms for treating microbiota dysbiosis-related metabolic and inflammatory dysfunction.

title: Impact of gut microbiota in chronic kidney disease: natural polyphenols as beneficial regulators
summary:
# Summary

Chronic kidney disease is closely associated with dysbiosis of the gut microbiota, which promotes disease progression through multiple mechanisms including the production of uremic toxins such as indoxyl sulfate and p-cresyl sulfate, as well as pro-inflammatory metabolites including trimethylamine-N-oxide, tryptophan derivatives, and branched-chain amino acids. Natural polyphenols emerge as promising therapeutic agents that modulate the gut microbiota while simultaneously reducing inflammation and apoptosis through multiple signaling pathways including NF-κB, MAPK, mTOR, and NLRP3, and by enhancing the renal antioxidant defense system and protecting mitochondrial structure. Dysbiosis is characterized by reduced abundance of beneficial short-chain fatty acid-producing bacteria such as Lactobacilli and Prevotella, along with enrichment of pathogenic taxa including Escherichia-Shigella, while restoration of protective bacteria such as Bifidobacterium spp. and augmentation of short-chain fatty acid production alleviates kidney disease manifestations. Specific bacterial taxa are associated with different kidney disease subtypes, including Actinobacteria with increased risk of IgA nephropathy and Escherichia-Shigella with diabetic kidney disease progression. These findings support a mechanistic link between microbiota-derived metabolites and renal injury, suggesting that polyphenol-mediated microbiota modulation represents a novel therapeutic strategy for chronic kidney disease management.

title: Characteristics of vaginal microbiota in pregnant women with cervical insufficiency and its association with adverse pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Pregnant women with cervical insufficiency exhibit significant vaginal microbiota dysbiosis characterized by elevated levels of pathogenic bacteria including Ureaplasma urealyticum, Gardnerella vaginalis, Candida albicans, and Chlamydia, while protective lactobacilli and commensals like Escherichia coli are reduced. These pathogenic microorganisms collectively act as independent risk factors for adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage and premature birth, with Ureaplasma urealyticum showing particularly strong association with cervical insufficiency and reproductive tract infections such as endometritis and urethritis. The dysbiotic microbiota contributes to cervical insufficiency pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms involving altered immune responses, including dysregulated cytokine secretion, apoptosis, and autophagy in cervical tissues, while simultaneously facilitating bacterial access to the uterine cavity through cervical defects. Chlamydia trachomatis specifically triggers inflammatory responses in the fallopian tubes via upregulation of matrix metalloproteinases, whereas Gardnerella vaginalis provokes placental inflammation. Emergency cerclage surgery represents an effective therapeutic intervention that improves pregnancy outcomes by reducing miscarriage and premature birth rates in affected women, though microbiota dysbiosis may contribute to cerclage failure, suggesting that antimicrobial management may optimize surgical success.

title: Vitamin B5 and vitamin U review: justification of combined use for the treatment of mucosa-associated gastrointestinal pathologies
summary: Vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid) serves as a precursor to coenzyme A, a critical cofactor in intermediary metabolism, fatty acid and cholesterol synthesis, and energy production via the Krebs cycle and ATP synthesis. Beyond its metabolic roles, vitamin B5 exhibits potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities while promoting epithelial cell proliferation and differentiation, with specific evidence demonstrating its capacity to reduce TNF-α levels and oxidative stress in endometriosis patients. The vitamin enhances gut homeostasis by reducing harmful bacteria, increasing beneficial bacteria, and supporting CD8+ T-cell differentiation toward IL-22-producing cells, thereby strengthening mucosal immunity. Vitamin B5 supplementation alters intestinal microbiota composition favorably, supporting barrier integrity through epithelial regeneration and mucus production, processes essential for repairing gastrointestinal erosions and lesions. When combined with vitamin U at therapeutic doses, vitamin B5 demonstrates synergistic regenerative and protective effects on the gastrointestinal mucosa, with commensal bacteria such as Bacteroides and Escherichia coli contributing additional endogenous pantothenic acid production. These findings suggest vitamin B5 supplementation represents a promising adjunctive strategy for managing mucosa-associated gastrointestinal pathologies, particularly those involving microbiota dysbiosis and inflammatory conditions like endometriosis.

title: Exploring the role of intestinal pathogenic bacteria in metronidazole-induced bone loss: focus on Klebsiella variicola
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that metronidazole-induced bone loss operates through a pathogenic mechanism centered on gut dysbiosis and the expansion of *Klebsiella variicola*. Metronidazole treatment causes significant alterations in the gut microbiota, particularly promoting *Enterobacteriaceae* family expansion, which elevates *Klebsiella variicola* abundance. *K. variicola* secretes pro-inflammatory extracellular vesicles that accumulate in bone tissue and directly promote osteoclast differentiation while upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β and IL-6, thereby driving inflammatory bone loss. Notably, targeting *K. variicola* with ciprofloxacin effectively mitigates metronidazole-induced osteoporosis by suppressing the pathogenic bacterial blooms and reducing osteoclast activation. These findings establish *K. variicola* as a critical pathogenic mediator in antibiotic-induced bone loss and suggest that selective antimicrobial interventions targeting pathogenic bacteria while preserving beneficial microbiota, or probiotic supplementation with strains like *Lactobacillus* and *Akkermansia muciniphila*, may offer therapeutic strategies to preserve bone health during antibiotic therapy.

title: Supplementation of the probiotic Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG to pre-weaning calves decreases diarrhea incidence by modulating gut bacteria and associated metabolites
summary:
# Summary

Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG) supplementation significantly reduces diarrhea incidence in pre-weaning calves by modulating the gut microbiota composition and increasing beneficial metabolites. LGG administration increases abundances of beneficial bacteria including Lysinbacillus and Rikenellaceae_RC9_gut_group while simultaneously reducing pathogenic Escherichia-Shigella populations, thereby preventing dysbiosis-related intestinal colonization. The probiotic simultaneously elevates production of short-chain fatty acids—acetic acid, propionic acid, and butyrate—which enhance gut barrier function and mucosal immunity while inhibiting pathogenic bacteria. These microbiota and metabolite changes correlated with improved growth performance and feed intake in treated calves. The strong acid and bile acid resistance of LGG enables sustained colonization and competitive exclusion of pathogens, positioning this probiotic as a viable alternative to prophylactic antibiotic treatment in neonatal ruminant health management.

title: Endometrial microbiome in reproductive failure: The possibility of metagenomic analysis
summary:
# Summary

The endometrial microbiome plays a critical role in reproductive success, with Lactobacillus dominance promoting reproductive health while its loss associates with reproductive failure, repeated implantation failure, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage and preterm birth. Loss of Lactobacillus dominance triggers proinflammatory responses and allows pathogenic proliferation, creating an inflammatory environment that disrupts endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation. Dysbiosis characterized by increased microbial diversity and pathogenic bacteria such as Burkholderia spp. is frequently observed in women with repeated implantation failure and recurrent pregnancy loss. Recent clinical approaches targeting microbiota restoration through personalized antibiotic therapy combined with probiotic supplementation have demonstrated improved clinical pregnancy and ongoing pregnancy rates, with Lactobacillus-containing probiotics promoting immune tolerance through increased regulatory T cells and reduced proinflammatory cytokines. Metagenomic analysis using techniques like 16S rRNA sequencing and endometrial microbiota mapping (EMMA) enables identification of individual dysbiosis patterns and personalized therapeutic interventions to restore reproductive health.

title: Gut Microbiota Dysbiosis in Endometriosis: A Potential Link to Inflammation and Disease Progression
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, causes chronic inflammation, pain, infertility, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and gastrointestinal symptoms through dysbiosis-driven pathways. Dysbiosis—alterations in the normal gut microbiota composition—weakens the intestinal epithelial barrier, increasing permeability and enabling bacterial translocation that promotes low-grade systemic inflammation. This dysbiosis suppresses immune function and reduces pathogen defences while elevating inflammatory markers including β-glucuronidase and secretory IgA; dysbiotic microbiota also disrupts estrogen metabolism, with elevated β-glucuronidase linked to increased circulating estrogen levels through reduced fecal elimination. Pro-inflammatory cytokines released intraperitoneal reduce beneficial lactobacilli species while promoting gram-negative bacteria, further perpetuating the inflammatory microenvironment that accelerates endometriotic implant growth. Dysbiotic alterations involve specific bacterial taxa including elevated Bacteroides, Firmicutes, Bacteroidetes, Actinobacteria, and Proteobacteria, which collectively contribute to immune response modulation and disease aggravation in endometriosis patients.

title: Eosinophilic Esophagitis Pathogenesis: All Clear?
summary: Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) is a type 2 inflammatory disorder driven by complex interactions between genetic susceptibility, environmental triggers, and dysbiosis of the esophageal microbiota. Genome-wide association studies have identified multiple risk genes including CAPN14, TSLP, BACH2, GATA3, and TGFBR1/2, which govern epithelial barrier integrity and type 2 immune responses. The pathogenic mechanism centers on IL-13 and IL-33 signaling, which impairs epithelial tight junctions, induces epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis, and activates type 2 immunity through dendritic cells and basophils. Environmental exposures such as food emulsifiers, laundry detergents, and plasticizers (bisphenol A, phthalates) compromise barrier function, while early-life factors including cesarean delivery, neonatal intensive care admission, and early antibiotic use disrupt protective microbiota, favoring pathogenic organisms like Haemophilus over commensals including Streptococcus and Lactobacillus. Dupilumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting IL-4 and IL-13 receptors, effectively treats EoE by restoring epithelial integrity and suppressing type 2 inflammation, representing a significant therapeutic advance alongside dietary and pharmacological interventions.

title: Chronic Intestinal Inflammation and Microbial Dysbiosis Are Associated With Female Reproductive Outcomes in a Mouse Model of Inflammatory Bowel Disease
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dextran sodium sulfate-induced chronic intestinal inflammation in mice causes substantial disruptions to reproductive function through gut dysbiosis and systemic immune activation. DSS treatment induced classic colitis markers including weight loss, elevated disease activity scores, and shortened colons while simultaneously reducing estrus phase duration, litter sizes, and pup weights. The inflammation-associated dysbiosis featured pathogenic expansion of Bacteroides, Turicibacter, and Faecalibaculum alongside depletion of beneficial Lactobacillus, with Turicibacter abundance correlating positively with circulating neutrophils, proinflammatory cytokines, and serum estradiol. Conversely, Lactobacillus and Prevotellaceae positively correlated with reproductive outcomes including larger litter sizes, heavier pups, and longer estrus phases. DSS treatment also suppressed anti-müllerian hormone and luteinizing hormone while elevating estradiol, consistent with impaired ovarian reserve, and induced a proinflammatory immune phenotype marked by increased CD4+ T cells producing IFNγ, TNFα, and IL17a. These findings establish a mechanistic link between chronic bowel inflammation, microbial dysbiosis, and adverse reproductive outcomes in females, with direct relevance to the documented fertility and pregnancy complications observed in women with inflammatory bowel disease.

title: Probiotic Hungatella hathewayi increases host estrogen level via regulation of gut microbiota and host metabolism in sows
summary: Dietary supplementation with the probiotic bacterium *Hungatella hathewayi* significantly increases circulating estrogen levels in sows through coordinated alterations of gut microbial composition and host metabolic pathways. The probiotic enriches beneficial bacteria including *Roseburia*, *Alloprevotella*, *Lachnospira*, and *Anaerovibrio*—all of which produce short-chain fatty acids and influence host metabolism—while simultaneously modifying pyrimidine metabolism, tryptophan metabolism, and steroid hormone biosynthesis. *Hungatella hathewayi* achieves elevated estrogen through its production of β-glucuronidase, an enzyme that reactivates conjugated estrogen in the gut for reabsorption, a mechanism relevant to the estrobolome concept underlying reproductive disorders including endometriosis. The study confirms that gut microbiota dysbiosis reduces microbial glucuronidase activity, thereby suppressing estrogen circulation, while the administered probiotic restores this critical enzymatic capacity. Notably, *Hungatella hathewayi* supplementation did not alter follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, progesterone, or antioxidant markers, indicating estrogen elevation occurs through direct microbial-metabolic mechanisms independent of hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis stimulation. These findings suggest that dysbiosis-driven reductions in estrogen-reactivating microbiota may contribute to endometriosis pathogenesis, positioning targeted probiotic interventions as a potential therapeutic strategy.

title: Impact of the Female Genital Microbiota on Outcomes of Assisted Reproductive Techniques
summary:
# Summary

The female genital microbiota critically influences assisted reproductive technique outcomes through multiple biological mechanisms centered on Lactobacillus dominance and dysbiosis prevention. Lactobacillus species produce lactic acid, hydrogen peroxide, bacteriocins, and biosurfactants that collectively inhibit pathogenic microorganisms and maintain an acidic vaginal environment favorable for reproductive success. Dysbiotic states characterized by Community State Type IV (anaerobic bacteria including Gardnerella, Prevotella, and Mycoplasmataceae) are associated with impaired embryo implantation, chronic endometritis, recurrent implantation failure, and reduced clinical pregnancy rates, likely through local inflammatory responses mediated by altered cytokine profiles. Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota, particularly Lactobacillus crispatus, shows significant ethnic variation and is associated with successful embryo transfers, while high-diversity dysbiotic profiles and pathogenic bacteria including Enterococcus faecalis, Escherichia coli, Ureaplasma, and Halomonas compromise implantation success. The virome composition, including bacteriophages and human papillomavirus, further influences bacterial balance and reproductive outcomes. Lifestyle factors such as vaginal douching, high-sugar diets, and hormonal fluctuations affect Lactobacillus populations, with estrogen and progesterone stimulating glycogen release that lactobacilli metabolize into protective lactic acid.

title: Behind-the-Scenes Actors in Fertility: A Comprehensive Review of the Female Reproductive Tract Microbiome and Its Clinical Relevance
summary:
# Summary

The female reproductive tract microbiome plays a critical role in fertility outcomes, with composition directly influencing endometrial receptivity, embryo implantation, and immune tolerance. Microbiota dysbiosis—characterized by reduced Lactobacillus species and elevated pathogenic bacteria including Gardnerella vaginalis, Prevotella, and Atopobium—is strongly associated with poor reproductive outcomes including recurrent implantation failure, infertility, preterm birth, preeclampsia, and recurrent miscarriages. Lactobacillus crispatus dominance is protective, associated with successful embryo implantation and clinical pregnancy, while its depletion or dominance of pathogenic species correlates with IVF failure and idiopathic infertility. Therapeutic interventions combining Lactobacillus supplementation with antibiotics (clindamycin or metronidazole) demonstrate improved reproductive outcomes, though standalone probiotic treatments show limited efficacy in modifying microbiota composition and IVF success rates.

title: Microbiota of Cervical Canal in Nine Patients Diagnosed with Ectopic Pregnancy: Case Series
summary:
# Summary

This case series examines cervical microbiota composition in nine patients with ectopic pregnancy, revealing significant dysbiosis characterized by reduced Lactobacillus spp. (detected in 78% of patients at levels ≤5 × 10⁷ CFU/mL) and elevated pathogenic bacteria including Enterococcus spp. (55%), Staphylococcus spp. (22%), and Escherichia coli (11%). Lactobacillus species normally protect the reproductive tract through production of lactic acid, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins, which maintain an acidic environment and stimulate immune defenses; their depletion contributes to dysbiosis-induced chronic endometrial inflammation and recurrent implantation failure. The study identifies specific microbial alterations—particularly elevations of Gardnerella, Prevotella, Clostridia, and Leptotrichiaceae alongside Lactobacillus reduction—as potential biomarkers associated with ectopic pregnancy risk and abnormal embryo implantation. These findings underscore that microbiota dysbiosis links to early pregnancy complications, suggesting that cervical microbiota analysis may facilitate identification of women at elevated risk for ectopic pregnancy and informing future therapeutic interventions targeting microbiota restoration.

title: Gut Microbiome Dysbiosis and Its Impact on Reproductive Health: Mechanisms and Clinical Applications
summary:
# Summary

Gut dysbiosis disrupts multiple pathways critical to reproductive health, including the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis and estrogen metabolism. The microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (acetate, propionate, and butyrate) bind GPR41 and GPR43 receptors to inhibit the pro-inflammatory transcription factor NF-κB, thereby regulating gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion and downstream luteinizing and follicle-stimulating hormone release. Dysbiosis reduces production of these protective metabolites and disrupts β-glucuronidase-mediated estrogen deconjugation, leading to estrogen dominance and impaired endometrial receptivity. Additionally, dysbiosis-associated lipopolysaccharides trigger chronic low-grade inflammation via metabolic endotoxemia, disrupting folliculogenesis, implantation, and ovulatory function while impairing IL-22-dependent intestinal barrier integrity and glucose homeostasis. These mechanisms collectively link dysbiosis to endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and adverse pregnancy outcomes, positioning microbiota restoration as a potential therapeutic target for reproductive disorders.

title: Mechanistic insights into endometriosis: roles of Streptococcus agalactiae and L-carnitine in lesion development and angiogenesis
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that *Streptococcus agalactiae*, a bacterium elevated in the cervical microbiota of endometriosis patients, promotes lesion development and angiogenesis through an L-carnitine-dependent mechanism. In murine models, elevated *S. agalactiae* colonization significantly increased ectopic lesion number and severity, effects that were reversed by antibiotic treatment. The bacteria enhance endometrial cell proliferation, migration, and invasion both in vivo and in vitro, while also upregulating vascular endothelial growth factor to promote angiogenesis supporting lesion vascularization. Metabolomic analyses identified elevated L-carnitine levels in cervical secretions and serum of endometriosis patients, and exogenous L-carnitine administration alone increased lesion formation. Critically, inhibiting L-carnitine synthesis or transport with mildronate suppressed *S. agalactiae*-induced pathological cellular behaviors and lesion burden, indicating that L-carnitine is essential for bacterial-mediated endometriosis progression. These findings establish microbiota dysbiosis, specifically *S. agalactiae* overgrowth and associated L-carnitine elevation, as a mechanistic driver of endometriosis pathogenesis and identify L-carnitine metabolism as a potential therapeutic target.

title: Effects of gut microbiota in breast cancer
summary:
# Summary

Dysbiotic gut microbiota composition is strongly associated with breast cancer development and progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The intestinal microbiota regulates estrogen metabolism via the estrobolome, a consortium of microbial genes encoding β-glucuronidase enzymes that facilitate estrogen deconjugation and reabsorption; dysbiosis-driven imbalances in this axis elevate circulating estrogen levels, which drives breast cancer cell growth. Breast cancer patients exhibit characteristic microbiota alterations including reduced diversity, decreased Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes, and elevated pathogenic bacteria such as Propionicimonas, Veillonella, and Fusobacterium nucleatum. Pathogenic bacteria including enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis and Fusobacterium nucleatum actively promote tumor growth and metastasis through toxin-mediated signaling activation (β-catenin, Notch1, and TLR4/NF-κB pathways) and direct tumor cell interactions, while protective commensals producing bacteriocins like nisin demonstrate cytotoxic activity against cancer cells. Additionally, dysbiotic microbiota impair immune regulation by altering regulatory T cell differentiation, reducing secretory immunoglobulin A expression, and modulating neutrophil production, with reduced microbial diversity linked to poorer survival outcomes in breast cancer patients.

title: Probiotics may improve vaginal microbiota, metabolic disorders and ovarian function-related markers by modulating gut microbiota in POI mice
summary: Cyclophosphamide-induced premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) in mice is associated with dysbiosis of both gut and vaginal microbiota, characterized by enrichment of pathogenic bacteria including Allobaculum, Prevotella, and Bacteroides in the gut, alongside elevated Proteus, Streptococcus, and Rothia in the vagina. This dysbiosis corresponds with marked ovarian dysfunction including reduced anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, decreased growing follicles, increased atretic follicles, and metabolic abnormalities with elevated triglycerides and cholesterol. Probiotic treatment containing 12 beneficial bacterial strains reverses these pathogenic microbial shifts while improving key reproductive markers, including increased AMH and estradiol levels, expanded growing follicle populations, and reduced follicle-stimulating hormone and atretic follicles. Additionally, probiotics ameliorate the lipid metabolism disorders associated with POI by reducing triglyceride and total cholesterol levels. The therapeutic mechanisms involve multiple pathways including restoration of intestinal microecological balance, enhancement of intestinal barrier function, activation of immune cells, production of short-chain fatty acids that inhibit inflammatory cytokines, and modulation of the gut-brain axis. These findings demonstrate that microbiota dysbiosis contributes to POI pathogenesis and that probiotics can therapeutically target this underlying mechanism to restore both reproductive and metabolic health.

title: Advances in the interrelated nature of vaginal microecology, HPV infection, and cervical lesions
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

Vaginal microecology, dominated by protective Lactobacillus species, plays a critical role in preventing HPV infection and cervical lesion development through multiple antimicrobial mechanisms. Lactobacilli maintain vaginal acidity and produce lactic acid, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins that inhibit pathogenic bacteria overgrowth, strengthen mucosal barriers, and enhance local immune defenses. Disruptions in this microecological balance increase susceptibility to HPV infection, which further destabilizes the microbiota by disrupting the vaginal acidic environment and triggering inflammation, creating a vicious cycle that promotes cervical dysplasia and cancer progression. Persistent high-risk HPV infections suppress cervical immunity and contribute directly to cervical lesions, while the mucosal immune system and vaginal microbiota composition together influence HPV persistence or clearance. Notably, women with Lactobacillus iners-dominant microbiota experience significantly higher risks of HPV infection and cancer progression compared to those with Lactobacillus crispatus dominance, highlighting the importance of specific protective bacterial species in preventing cervical pathology.

title: Health disorders in menopausal women: microbiome alterations, associated problems, and possible treatments
summary:
# Summary

Menopause triggers widespread microbiota dysbiosis across multiple body sites through declining estrogen levels, which downregulates vaginal Lactobacillus and glycogen while reducing microbial diversity in the gut, urethra, and oral cavity. This dysbiosis contributes to numerous health disorders including obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, depression, anxiety, and genitourinary syndrome of menopause. Estrogen deficiency increases osteoclast activity and bone resorption while disrupting neurotransmitter balance, mechanisms underlying the heightened disease risk in postmenopausal women. Multiple therapeutic approaches effectively address these conditions: menopausal hormone therapy remains the gold standard, while dietary interventions (Mediterranean diet, cocoa polyphenols, soy isoflavones, dietary fiber), probiotic supplementation, and plant extracts (black cohosh, red clover, soybean) all demonstrate symptom reduction and microbiota restoration. Probiotics and hop extract specifically improve calcium absorption and bone density, whereas dietary and herbal interventions reduce cardiovascular risk factors including cholesterol, oxidative stress, and inflammation.

title: Efficacy of sclareol based on 16S rDNA sequencing in modulating gut microbiota composition in estradiol-treated mice
journal: Bioscience of Microbiota, Food and Health
summary:
# Summary

Dysmenorrhea is pathologically linked to estrogen accumulation and elevated prostaglandins, which drive inflammatory responses, uterine proliferation, and ischemia through estrogen-induced upregulation of endometrial prostaglandin production. Gut microbiota composition critically regulates circulating estrogen levels through the estrobolome, and dysbiosis is associated with gynecological diseases including endometriosis. Sclareol, a diterpene alcohol extracted from Salvia sclarea, effectively alleviates dysmenorrhea symptoms by suppressing COX-2 and iNOS while inhibiting inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, NO, MDA) and directly blocking uterine contractions through calcium signaling and oxytocin receptor modulation. Sclareol treatment reshapes the dysbiotic microbiota by expanding beneficial bacteria including Ruminococcus_1, Ruminococcaceae species, and Streptococcus—all of which reduce β-glucuronidase production and enhance short-chain fatty acid synthesis—thereby restoring estrogen homeostasis and alleviating dysmenorrhea-related pain and proliferation. These findings establish a microbiota-mediated mechanism linking sclareol's therapeutic action to both direct anti-inflammatory effects and indirect estrogen regulation through estrobolome restoration.

title: Will gut, oral, and vaginal microbiota influence the outcome of FET or be influenced by FET? A pilot study
summary: This pilot study examined whether gut, oral, and vaginal microbiota influence frozen embryo transfer (FET) outcomes and how FET affects microbial communities. The researchers identified distinct microbial signatures associated with FET success and failure, with specific bacterial genera such as Anaerococcus and Negativicoccus enriched in successful pregnancies, while taxa including Tissierellales, Peptoniphilus, and Finegoldia were more abundant in failed transfers. Notably, successful FET outcomes correlated with lower network complexity in oral and vaginal microbiota but higher complexity in gut microbiota, and specific bacterial taxa showed positive correlations with reproductive hormones and metabolic markers like anti-Müllerian hormone, human chorionic gonadotropin, and cholesterol levels. The study revealed that microbiota-associated metabolic pathways and serum metabolites, particularly cystamine, distinguished successful from failed FET groups before transfer. Additionally, elevated sex hormones during pregnancy altered oral microbiota composition and vascular permeability, with periodontal bacteria potentially translocating to the fetoplacental unit. These findings suggest that targeting microbiota composition and associated metabolic pathways could represent a promising therapeutic strategy to enhance FET success rates.

title: Vaginal bacteria-derived extracellular vesicles diffuse through human cervicovaginal mucus to enable bacterial signaling to upper female reproductive tract tissues
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that bacteria-derived extracellular vesicles (bEVs) from vaginal microbiota serve as crucial mediators of microbe-host communication that can penetrate cervicovaginal mucus and reach upper reproductive tract tissues. A healthy, Lactobacillus-dominated vaginal microbiome produces bEVs that are readily internalized by endometrial and placental cells while triggering only modest inflammatory responses, whereas dysbiosis-associated pathogens like Gardnerella vaginalis and Mobiluncus mulieris produce bEVs that diffuse more efficiently through the mucus barrier and potently upregulate pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNFα) in vaginal epithelial cells. The smaller size and more neutral charge of pathogenic bEVs enable superior mobility and cellular uptake compared to commensal Lactobacillus bEVs, facilitating virulence factor delivery and horizontal gene transfer. These findings establish bEVs as a critical mechanism by which vaginal dysbiosis—characterized by pathogenic species including G. vaginalis and M. mulieris—propagates inflammatory signaling to reproductive tissues and contributes to gynecologic and obstetric disease, while loss of protective commensal bEV production during dysbiosis further compromises reproductive health outcomes.

title: The association between dietary fiber intake and pelvic inflammatory disease in women: findings from the NHANES 2015–2018
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that higher dietary fiber intake is inversely associated with pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) risk in U.S. women, with each additional gram per day reducing PID odds by 5% and a protective threshold identified at 19.45 g/day. The protective mechanism operates through the gut microbiota pathway: dietary fiber promotes growth of beneficial bacteria (Lachnospiraceae, Akkermansia, Lactobacillus, and Bifidobacterium) that produce short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate, and acetate), which suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α while promoting anti-inflammatory mediators IL-10 and TGF-β through inhibition of NF-κB signaling and histone deacetylase activity. Fiber additionally supports gut barrier integrity and modulates the reproductive tract microenvironment through the gut–reproductive tract axis. Additional dietary minerals such as magnesium and copper further reduce PID risk by contributing anti-inflammatory responses, while high-fat diets and risk factors including smoking and obesity increase PID susceptibility. Women with PID typically demonstrate significantly lower fiber intake and are at risk of serious sequelae including tubal factor infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain if left untreated.

title: Untargeted metabolomics as a tool to assess the impact of dietary approaches on pig gut health: a review
journal: Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology
summary:
# Summary

Untargeted metabolomics serves as a powerful discovery-based approach for comprehensively assessing how dietary interventions influence pig gut health by measuring the global metabolome—the end products of biochemical pathways that reflect phenotypic changes and disease states. The metabolome is shaped by complex interactions between dietary nutrients, the gut microbiota, and the intestinal barrier, with dietary fiber emerging as a key modulator that undergoes microbial fermentation to produce beneficial metabolites including short-chain fatty acids, secondary bile acids, and L-methionine, which collectively strengthen gut barrier function, modulate immune responses, and enhance epithelial integrity. Conversely, toxic fermentation byproducts such as ammonia, histamine, and phenol impair intestinal epithelial integrity and promote inflammatory reactions, highlighting the importance of dietary composition in controlling pathogenic metabolite production. Specific dietary fatty acids and their oxidation products demonstrate remarkable functional diversity: capric acid and stearic acid improve barrier function and gut morphology, while oxidation products of linoleic acids exhibit contrasting immunological properties, with 9-HODE promoting inflammation and 13-HODE exhibiting anti-inflammatory effects. This metabolomics-based approach enables the discovery of biomarkers linking nutritional status, dietary exposure, and disease outcomes, making it invaluable for optimizing dietary strategies to promote gut health and prevent dysbiosis-related conditions through precise measurement of metabolic signatures.

title: Increased gut microbiota diversity in women with uterine fibroids: Insights from a pilot study on gut and reproductive tract microbiota
summary: This pilot study reveals dysbiotic alterations in both gut and reproductive tract microbiota in women with uterine fibroids, with Lactobacillus iners emerging as a key pathogenic agent. Elevated L. iners in cervical samples is associated with vaginal dysbiosis and produces less lactic acid than protective species like L. crispatus, potentially facilitating the transition to dysbiosis and contributing to chronic low-grade inflammation. The dysbiotic state impairs estrogen detoxification through reduced β-glucuronidase activity, leading to excessive estrogen accumulation that promotes UF development—a process compounded by altered hormone-dependent signaling and immune responses. Notably, gut microbiota diversity is paradoxically elevated in UF patients, though specific dysbiotic patterns emerge, including increased Prevotellaceae and reduced Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus curvatus. The study highlights a bidirectional relationship between estrogen metabolism and microbiota composition, where disrupted microbiome composition weakens estrogen detoxification and excretion, creating a favorable hormonal environment for UF pathogenesis. These findings underscore the complex interplay between microbiota dysbiosis, hormonal imbalance, and inflammation in uterine fibroid development.

title: Unraveling the Contribution of Estrobolome Alterations to Endometriosis Pathogenesis
summary:
# Estrobolome Dysbiosis and Endometriosis: A Mechanistic Overview

This article describes how alterations in the estrobolome—the collection of bacterial genes regulating enterohepatic estrogen circulation—contribute to endometriosis pathogenesis through dysregulation of systemic estrogen levels. Gut microbiota dysbiosis impairs the balance of estrogen-metabolizing enzymes, particularly β-glucuronidase produced by bacteria such as *Bacteroides fragilis* and *Clostridium perfringens*, leading to elevated circulating free estrogens that amplify ectopic endometrial tissue growth and inflammation. Concurrently, disrupted microbiota composition reduces the production of protective metabolites like short-chain fatty acids and allows pathogenic bacteria to breach intestinal barriers, triggering systemic and local inflammation while simultaneously altering vaginal microbiota composition through estrogen-dependent mechanisms. The estrogen-microbiome axis thus operates bidirectionally: dysbiosis increases bioavailable estrogen, promoting endometriosis development, while endometriosis-associated inflammation further disrupts microbiota homeostasis. These findings suggest that targeting estrobolome dysbiosis through dietary interventions, probiotics, or estrogen-modulating therapies may offer novel therapeutic strategies for managing this chronic inflammatory condition.

title: Exploring the Interplay Between Gut Microbiota and the Melatonergic Pathway in Hormone Receptor-Positive Breast Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a critical interplay between gut dysbiosis and dysregulation of the melatonergic pathway in hormone receptor-positive breast cancer. Patients with HR+ BC exhibit characteristic dysbiosis featuring reduced *Bifidobacterium longum* and elevated *Bacteroides eggerthii*, accompanied by disrupted melatonin metabolism along the tryptophan-serotonin-N-acetylserotonin-melatonin pathway. Specifically, elevated aromatase (AANAT) activity shunts serotonin toward N-acetylserotonin accumulation while reduced ASMT enzyme activity decreases melatonin production, resulting in reduced serum melatonin levels alongside elevated estrogen levels. The dysbiotic microbiota produces excessive beta-glucuronidase, promoting estrogen deconjugation and reabsorption, while reduced short-chain fatty acid production compromises intestinal barrier integrity and melatonin synthesis support. Notably, melatonin exerts protective effects by promoting beneficial bacteria and inhibiting pathogenic species, suggesting that restoring melatonin production through microbiota modulation could represent a therapeutic approach to mitigate estrogen-driven HR+ breast cancer progression.

title: Gut–Vaginal Microbiome Crosstalk in Ovarian Cancer: Implications for Early Diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

The gut–vaginal microbiota axis plays a critical role in ovarian cancer development through dysbiosis-driven alterations in microbial composition and function. Microbial dysbiosis promotes oncogenesis via three principal mechanisms: estrogen-mediated metabolic reprogramming through microbial β-glucuronidase activity, activation of NF-κB and STAT3 signaling pathways, and epigenetic silencing of tumor suppressor genes via DNA methyltransferase. Characteristic microbiota shifts in ovarian cancer patients include depletion of protective bacteria (Lachnospiraceae, Bifidobacteriaceae, Clostridiaceae, and Lactobacillus) and enrichment of pathogenic taxa (Coriobacteriaceae, Adlercreutzia, Collinsella, Gardnerella, Prevotella, and Escherichia). The estrobolome—a microbial network of Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes—governs estrogen metabolism by converting conjugated estrogens to bioavailable forms, driving excessive estrogen exposure that stimulates ovarian epithelial cell proliferation through estrogen receptor signaling (ER-α and GPER pathways) and increases cancer risk, particularly with long-term menopausal hormone therapy. Current therapeutic options include PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib), anti-angiogenic agents (bevacizumab), and platinum–paclitaxel chemotherapy, though microbiota-targeted interventions such as oral probiotics to restore protective Lactobacillus species may offer complementary strategies for early diagnosis and prevention.

title: Gut Microbiota in Women with Eating Disorders: A New Frontier in Pathophysiology and Treatment
summary:
# Summary: Gut Microbiota in Women with Eating Disorders

Women with eating disorders—including anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and binge eating disorder—exhibit significant gut dysbiosis characterized by reduced microbial diversity and altered bacterial populations. The dysbiosis arises from restrictive eating patterns, purging behaviors, and altered dietary intake, leading to increased intestinal permeability and low-grade inflammation that further perpetuate disease pathology. The gut microbiota plays a fundamental role in regulating appetite, mood, stress responses, metabolism, immune function, and neurotransmitter synthesis, with specific bacterial taxa such as Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus producing neurotransmitters like GABA and serotonin that influence brain function through the gut-brain axis via vagal signaling. Dysbiosis is linked to anxiety and depression, conditions commonly comorbid with eating disorders. Microbiota-targeted therapeutic interventions—including probiotics, prebiotics, dietary modulation rich in fiber, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are being investigated to restore microbial balance and ameliorate eating disorder symptoms. Additionally, hormonal fluctuations in women, particularly estrogen and testosterone, modulate specific bacterial taxa composition, suggesting that sex-specific microbiota profiles may contribute to the higher prevalence of eating disorders in women.

title: Gut microbiota: emerging biomarkers and potential therapeutics for premature ovarian failure
summary: Gut microbiota dysbiosis contributes to premature ovarian failure (POF) through multiple interconnected mechanisms affecting reproductive endocrine function and ovarian health. Dysregulation of key bacterial taxa such as reduced Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes, along with decreased production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) by beneficial bacteria like Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, impairs the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and disrupts sex hormone regulation, leading to elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and reduced estrogen levels characteristic of POF. SCFAs normally preserve intestinal barrier integrity by regulating tight junction proteins and inhibiting inflammatory pathways (TLR-4/NF-κB), but their depletion during dysbiosis permits increased inflammatory cytokine production and systemic immune activation. Dysbiotic microbiota also promotes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in ovarian granulosa cells, triggering apoptosis and follicular atresia that directly precipitate ovarian failure. Beyond reproductive complications, POF dysbiosis-associated dysregulation elevates risks for cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, and depression through altered neuroendocrine signaling and metabolic homeostasis. These findings suggest that microbiota-targeted therapeutic interventions—including probiotics, dietary fiber supplementation, and lifestyle modifications to restore beneficial bacterial populations—represent emerging strategies for preventing or treating POF.

title: Gut Microbiota‐Derived Acetate Ameliorates Endometriosis via JAK1/STAT3‐Mediated M1 Macrophage Polarisation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that gut microbiota-derived acetate ameliorates endometriosis through activation of the JAK1/STAT3 signaling pathway and subsequent M1 macrophage polarization. Endometriosis is associated with gut microbiota dysbiosis, and fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) from healthy donors significantly reduced ectopic lesion volume and proliferation while exacerbating disease in recipients of endometriosis patient microbiota. Healthy donor FMT enhanced intestinal barrier integrity by upregulating tight junction proteins (ZO-1, Occludin, Claudin-1/5) and reducing permeability markers, while simultaneously elevating fecal acetate levels and promoting microbial shifts toward increased Lactobacillus and Firmicutes abundance with reduced Bacteroidetes. The beneficial acetate-JAK1/STAT3 axis drives macrophage polarization toward the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype, which inhibits endometriotic lesion growth through release of pro-inflammatory cytokines, contrasting with immunosuppressive M2 macrophages that promote disease progression. These findings establish a mechanistic link between commensal bacteria, short-chain fatty acid metabolism, immune polarization, and endometriosis pathophysiology, suggesting microbiota-targeted interventions as potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The role of cervical microbiome in cervical incompetence: insights from 16 S rRNA metagenomic sequencing
summary:
# Summary

Cervical incompetence contributes to adverse pregnancy outcomes including mid-pregnancy miscarriage and preterm delivery, with dysbiosis of the cervicovaginal microbiota playing a critical mechanistic role. Women with cervical incompetence exhibit reduced abundance of protective Lactobacillus species and increased colonization with pathogenic bacteria like Gardnerella and Group B Streptococcus, which trigger excessive inflammatory responses through the TLR/NF-κB signaling pathway, leading to collagen degradation and cervical tissue remodeling. Lactobacillus crispatus counteracts this pathology by suppressing TLR2/TLR4 and NF-κB signaling, reducing inflammatory cytokine production (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and enhancing cervical epithelial cell viability. Cervical cerclage surgery modifies the cervical microbiome by increasing Lactobacillus abundance and decreasing pathogenic species, while simultaneously extending gestational duration and improving neonatal survival rates. These findings demonstrate that restoring a Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota may represent a therapeutic strategy to mitigate the inflammatory cascade and poor reproductive outcomes associated with cervical incompetence.

title: Integrated oral-gut microbiota therapy: a novel perspective on preventing bacterial translocation for systemic disease management
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how oral-gut microbiota dysbiosis drives systemic disease through bacterial translocation and immune dysregulation. Pathogenic oral bacteria, particularly Porphyromonas gingivalis and Fusobacterium nucleatum, translocate to distal sites where their virulence factors (fimbrial proteins, gingipains, adhesins) bind pattern recognition receptors including TLR2, complement receptor 3, and CXC-chemokine receptor 4, triggering pro-inflammatory responses. Oral pathogens compromise epithelial barriers by degrading tight junction proteins like occludin and E-cadherin, while simultaneously producing quorum-sensing molecules such as autoinducer-2 that reshape gut microbiota composition and promote dysbiosis. The resulting bacterial translocation and metabolite disruption establish a bidirectional oral-gut axis where dysbiosis perpetuates systemic inflammation and contributes to hepatic disease, colorectal cancer, insulin resistance, and inflammatory conditions. Risk factors including smoking, poor oral hygiene, and stress hormones like cortisol exacerbate this pathogenic cascade. The article proposes integrated oral-gut microbiota therapeutic interventions to restore homeostasis and prevent disease progression through restoration of protective barriers and antimicrobial defenses mediated by secretory IgA and commensal bacteria.

title: The relationship between vaginal and endometrial microbiota and the outcome of Frozen-Thawed embryo transfer during hormone replacement cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how vaginal and endometrial microbiota composition affects outcomes in frozen-thawed embryo transfer (FET) cycles with hormone replacement therapy. Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota in the uterine cavity were strongly associated with improved clinical pregnancy rates (75% versus 45.16%) and live birth rates (65% versus 29.03%) compared to non-Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota. The Lactobacillus-dominant uterine group (B1) displayed reduced microbial diversity with lower Shannon and higher Simpson indices, alongside enrichment of Firmicutes, whereas the dysbiotic group (B2) showed greater diversity with increased Proteobacteria, Acinetobacter, and other potential pathogens. Notably, while 88.24% of patients maintained Lactobacillus dominance in the vagina, only 39.22% exhibited this dominance in the uterine cavity, highlighting that vaginal microbiota composition does not fully predict uterine microbiota status. These findings underscore the critical importance of uterine microbiota composition, specifically Lactobacillus dominance, in promoting successful embryo implantation and reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Vaginal microbiota alterations under supraphysiological estradiol state during in vitro fertilization-embryo transfer (IVF-ET) and the association with reproductive outcomes
summary:
# Vaginal Microbiota Alterations During IVF-ET and Reproductive Outcomes

The IVF-ET process induces supraphysiological estradiol levels that significantly alter vaginal microbiota composition and reduce both alpha and beta diversity, shifting community state types toward Lactobacillus-dominant profiles. Elevated estradiol activates Lactobacillus colonization while reducing abundances of Escherichia-Shigella, yet paradoxically, Lactobacillus dominance under these supraphysiological conditions does not improve assisted reproductive outcomes. Specific bacterial taxa emerge as biomarkers associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes: Streptococcus correlates positively with peak estradiol and predicts non-pregnancy (AUC=0.764), Atopobium predicts abortion (AUC=0.899), and Bifidobacterium predicts biochemical pregnancy (AUC=0.876). The estrogen-driven shift toward Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota occurs through multiple mechanisms including epithelial glycogen accumulation and subsequent lactic acid production that acidifies the vaginal environment, yet this favorable microbial environment created by supraphysiological estradiol may actually impair reproductive success rather than enhance it. These findings suggest that while elevated estrogen promotes protective Lactobacillus growth through well-characterized metabolic pathways, the overall dysbiotic shift caused by supraphysiological hormone levels during IVF-ET negatively impacts implantation and pregnancy maintenance.

title: International consensus statement on microbiome testing in clinical practice
summary:
# Microbiome Testing in Clinical Practice: Key Findings

The gut microbiota serves as a central regulator of multiple essential physiological functions, including metabolism, immune regulation, colonization resistance, and drug response. Dysbiosis—an imbalance in the gut microbiome composition—is increasingly recognized as associated with both intestinal and extraintestinal disorders, as well as altered responses to therapeutic interventions. The expert consensus identifies microbiome testing as a valuable clinical tool for diagnosis, prognosis, risk stratification, and prediction of individual therapy responses, with faecal microbiota transplantation already established as the standard treatment for recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection. Comprehensive microbiome assessment requires standardized protocols including amplicon and whole genome sequencing for taxonomic profiling, detection of pathogenic organisms, documentation of health-relevant taxa (such as Akkermansia and Bifidobacteria), and measurement of community diversity metrics. The panel emphasizes that results must account for multiple host factors—including age, gender, body mass index, diet, medications, and smoking status—that significantly influence microbiome composition and interpretation. Implementation of microbiome testing in clinical practice demands rigorous quality control measures, appropriate sample collection and preservation techniques, and longitudinal assessments to reliably monitor therapeutic effects and dietary interventions.

title: Effects of Baobab fruit powder on gut and cardiometabolic health in obesity—Protocol for a randomised placebo-controlled trial
summary: This study proposes a randomized controlled trial investigating baobab fruit powder (BFP) as a dietary intervention for cardiometabolic health in obesity. BFP, rich in soluble fiber, polyphenols, and vitamin C, is hypothesized to improve intestinal barrier function and increase gut microbiota diversity, which are typically reduced in obesity and type 2 diabetes. The mechanism underlying cardiometabolic dysfunction involves increased intestinal permeability, which allows bacterial lipopolysaccharides to translocate and trigger low-grade inflammation characterized by elevated C-reactive protein, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and interleukin-6, ultimately contributing to metabolic disease. By restoring microbial composition and reducing intestinal permeability through its prebiotic effects, BFP may modulate dysglycemia, postprandial glycemia, and insulin responses, thereby reducing cardiometabolic risk. This represents the first comprehensive investigation of BFP's effects on the gut-inflammation-metabolic disease axis in humans. The trial addresses a critical gap in understanding how dietary fiber interventions can reverse microbiota dysbiosis and restore intestinal barrier function in obesity.

title: Butyrate enhances gut dysbiosis by activating the cAMP/PKA/CREB signaling pathway to inhibit the progression of endometrial carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial carcinoma (EC) is associated with characteristic gut dysbiosis featuring elevated Proteobacteria and reduced Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes, alongside decreased short-chain fatty acids including butyrate and acetate. Fecal supernatants from EC patients promote tumor cell proliferation, invasion, and migration while inhibiting apoptosis; however, sodium butyrate reverses these protumoral effects both in vitro and in vivo by activating the cAMP/PKA/CREB signaling pathway. Mechanistically, butyrate functions as a histone deacetylase inhibitor to suppress cell proliferation through p21/p27 regulation, induce apoptosis via reactive oxygen species generation, and regulate the PI3K/AKT pathway, while also strengthening the intestinal barrier through increased Occludin and Claudin-1 expression and shifting the immune microenvironment toward anti-inflammatory conditions by decreasing interleukin-1β and tumor necrosis factor-α while increasing interleukin-10. These findings establish gut dysbiosis and reduced short-chain fatty acid production as contributors to EC progression and identify butyrate supplementation as a potential therapeutic strategy to restore the antitumoral microbiota-immune axis.

title: The gut microbiome and ovarian cysts: a mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identified causal relationships between gut microbiota composition and ovarian cyst development. Seventeen gut microbial genera demonstrated suggestive causal associations with ovarian cysts, with protective taxa including Bifidobacterium, Ruminococcus, and several Bacteroides and Lachnospiraceae species, while pathogenic taxa such as Oscillibacter, Pseudoflavonifractor, and Clostridiales species increased cyst risk. The mechanisms linking microbiota dysbiosis to ovarian pathology involve microbiota-mediated modulation of sex hormones through multiple pathways: microbial production of β-glucuronidase prevents estrogen conjugation thereby elevating estradiol levels, while microbiota regulate testosterone and dihydrotestosterone through bile acid metabolism and farnesoid X receptor signaling. Excess androgens promote apoptosis in granulosa cells, driving cyst formation, and dysbiosis-associated systemic inflammation further contributes to disease pathogenesis. These findings suggest that restoring protective bacterial taxa and reducing pathogenic genera may represent novel therapeutic strategies for ovarian cyst prevention and treatment, with potential implications for related endometrial conditions.

title: Global research trends in gut microbiota and cellular senescence: a bibliometric and visual analysis from 2015 to 2025
summary:
# Summary

The intestinal microbiota functions as a vital organ intimately connected to human health, playing crucial roles in metabolism control, immune system development, cancer prevention, and regulation of inflammatory responses. Dysregulation of gut microbiota composition—microbiota dysbiosis—accelerates cellular senescence through multiple interconnected mechanisms including chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction, processes central to aging and tissue degeneration. The dysregulated microbiota is associated with numerous disease states including obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disorders, autoimmune conditions, inflammatory bowel disease, Alzheimer's disease, and endometriosis. Recent research hotspots (2022–2025) highlight emerging mechanistic insights into the gut-brain axis, short-chain fatty acids, and bacterial metabolites such as butyric acid and methylglyoxal as potential modulators of cellular senescence and age-related disease prevention. The aging gut microbiota specifically correlates with intestinal inflammation and metabolic disturbances, creating a bidirectional relationship between microbiota dysbiosis and senescent cellular phenotypes. These findings establish the gut microbiota as a key therapeutic target for managing aging, metabolic syndrome, neurodegenerative diseases, and related conditions.

title: In vivo metabolism, pharmacokinetics, and pharmacological activities of ginsenosides from ginseng
summary:
# Summary

Ginsenosides, the primary active constituents of ginseng, undergo extensive biotransformation by the gut microbiota to generate bioactive metabolites responsible for ginseng's diverse pharmacological effects, including anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, anti-tumor, and neuroprotective activities. The intestinal microbiota, particularly bacterial species such as Bacteroides, Bifidobacterium, Eubacterium, and Clostridium, express specific glycoside hydrolases (β-glucosidase, α-rhamnosidase, xylosidase) that sequentially deglycosylate high-molecular-weight ginsenosides into lower-molecular-weight metabolites that can be readily absorbed. Key metabolic pathways involve the conversion of parent ginsenosides like Rb1 through intermediate forms (Rd, F2) to compound K, which circulates in blood and exhibits organ-protective, neuroprotective, antioxidant, and anti-cancer properties. Additionally, hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, particularly CYP3A4, catalyze further phase I metabolism of absorbed ginsenosides such as PPT and Rh2, while other enzymatic systems facilitate oxidation and glycosylation reactions. This integrated system of microbial and hepatic metabolism fundamentally determines ginseng's bioavailability and therapeutic efficacy, highlighting the critical role of a functional microbiota-host axis in converting ingested ginsenosides into pharmacologically active compounds.

title: Changes in the Subdoligranulum genus in patients with autoimmune disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that Subdoligranulum, a butyrate-producing bacterium of the Ruminococcaceae family, is consistently reduced in patients with multiple autoimmune diseases compared to healthy controls. Subdoligranulum maintains immune homeostasis through butyrate production, which strengthens intestinal barrier integrity and induces regulatory T cells (Treg) while suppressing inflammatory responses and pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-8. The depletion of Subdoligranulum observed across organ-specific autoimmune conditions including rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, thyroiditis, and urticaria disrupts this protective mechanism, leading to decreased Treg cells, enhanced Th2 differentiation, and elevated inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein and lipopolysaccharide-binding protein). Notably, certain Subdoligranulum strains, particularly *Subdoligranulum didolesgii*, exhibit pro-inflammatory effects and trigger joint inflammation, suggesting strain-specific pathogenic roles in autoimmunity. These findings establish microbiota dysbiosis targeting short-chain fatty acid-producing bacteria as a significant contributor to autoimmune pathogenesis and identify potential therapeutic targets for restoring microbial balance.

title: The untapped potential of vaginal microbiome diagnostics for improving women’s health
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary: The vaginal microbiome is a dynamic ecosystem regulated by hormonal fluctuations across the menstrual cycle that maintains vaginal health primarily through Lactobacillus species producing lactic acid, which inhibits pathogenic organisms and maintains an acidic pH. Dysbiosis—characterized by Lactobacillus depletion, elevated pH, and overgrowth of organisms such as Gardnerella vaginalis and Atopobium vaginae—contributes to multiple adverse reproductive and gynecological outcomes including infertility, miscarriage, and susceptibility to sexually transmitted infections and cervical cancers. Low Lactobacillus abundance increases susceptibility to persistent high-risk HPV infection and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia, while Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota are associated with natural regression of cervical lesions, suggesting a protective mechanism. Beyond cervical disease, dysbiosis-associated bacteria like Gardnerella promote chronic inflammation that activates inflammatory cytokines linked to endometrial cancer, while elevated Firmicutes and reduced Gardnerella characterize adenomyosis with distinct microbial signatures. Microbiota composition also associates with polycystic ovary syndrome (increased Mycoplasma, Prevotella, Actinomyces, Gardnerella, and Streptococcus), endometriosis, and fertility outcomes, with diverse microbiota and Lactobacillus depletion correlating with reduced baseline fertility and lower in vitro fertilization success rates. These findings underscore the potential of vaginal microbiome diagnostics to identify women at risk for reproductive complications and guide therapeutic interventions targeting microbial balance restoration.

title: Exposure to antibiotics during pregnancy or early infancy and the risk of autoimmune disease in children: A nationwide cohort study in Korea
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide Korean cohort study examined whether antibiotic exposure during pregnancy or early infancy increases the risk of autoimmune diseases in children, finding no overall association between early-life antibiotic exposure and autoimmune disease development. However, the analysis revealed specific associations with certain conditions: prenatal exposure to broad-spectrum antibiotics and cephalosporins, particularly during the first and second trimesters, showed modestly elevated risks for Crohn's disease, while antibiotic exposure within two months after birth was associated with increased autoimmune thyroiditis risk in males. The study proposes that antibiotics disrupt the developing microbiota and immune system through multiple mechanisms, including dysbiosis-induced alterations in Th17 cell activity and B cell maturation, maternal microbiome disruption affecting offspring immune tolerance, and impaired regulatory T cell development—effects that may be particularly pronounced in males who show delayed Treg development. These findings highlight the complex relationship between antibiotic-induced microbiota dysbiosis and immune system development during critical developmental windows, suggesting that while infection itself contributes to autoimmune disease risk, the protective effects of treating infections must be weighed against potential microbiome-mediated immune consequences.

title: Deconjugating taurocholic acid with Bifidobacterium to mitigate obesity-driven cancer progression by restoring CD8+ T-cell infiltration
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that obesity-driven hepatocellular carcinoma progression is mediated by elevated taurocholic acid (TCA), a bile acid that suppresses anti-tumor CD8+ T-cell infiltration and ERK phosphorylation. High-fat diet feeding reduces Bifidobacterium abundance while increasing circulating TCA levels, creating an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment characterized by reduced apoptosis and altered STAT3 signaling. The probiotic Bifidobacterium breve reverses this pathology by deconjugating TCA via its type 4 bile salt hydrolase enzyme, thereby restoring CD8+ T-cell infiltration, promoting tumor apoptosis, and inhibiting tumor growth in obese mice. Mechanistically, B. breve treatment reshapes the gut microbiota composition, reduces serum TCA concentrations, and restores anti-tumor immune responses through enhanced T-cell and macrophage infiltration. These findings establish a microbiota-derived metabolite axis linking obesity-associated dysbiosis to cancer progression and identify TCA as a therapeutic target for obesity-related malignancies.

title: Main differential endometrial microbiota associated with recurrent implantation failure: a case control study
summary:
# Summary

Alterations in endometrial microbiota composition are significantly associated with recurrent implantation failure (RIF), explaining 20% of the variation in microbiota composition between affected and control groups. Women with RIF demonstrate reduced microbiota richness but paradoxically increased diversity, along with markedly lower Lactobacillus abundance and elevated levels of pathogenic taxa including Ralstonia, Methyloversatilis, Vibrio, Bacteroides, Pseudomonas, and Escherichia-Shigella. Chronic endometritis was present in 100% of RIF patients studied, suggesting a link between dysbiotic microbiota and uterine inflammation. Key bacterial taxa exhibited distinct network correlations within RIF groups, with Pseudomonas showing strong positive associations with multiple pathogenic species, while Lactobacillus displayed negative correlations with opportunistic pathogens in other patient groups. Clinical factors including pregnancy history, induced abortions, and sexual activity frequency were found to associate with endometrial microbiota composition. These findings establish microbiota dysbiosis as a potential mechanistic factor in RIF pathogenesis, with implications for diagnostic assessment and targeted microbiota-modulating interventions.

title: Lactobacilli Deficiency in Infertile Women Seeking IVF in Arash Hospital: An Imbalance in the Genital Microbiome
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals significant microbiota dysbiosis in infertile women undergoing IVF, characterized by reduced Lactobacillus colonization across the vagina, cervix, and endometrium compared to healthy controls. Lactobacillus normally dominates the healthy female genital tract and suppresses pathogenic bacteria such as Gardnerella, Streptococcus, and Enterobacteriaceae by producing protective compounds including lactic acid, bacteriocins, and hydrogen peroxide. In infertile women, the depletion of Lactobacillus permits pathogenic bacteria to proliferate, triggering multiple mechanisms of reproductive failure: these dysbiotic bacteria activate pro-inflammatory immune responses, directly damage spermatozoa through toxins and metabolites, cause structural damage to reproductive tissues, and impair embryo implantation. The presence of bacterial DNA in 94% of uterine samples confirms that dysbiosis extends to the endometrial cavity itself, and low Lactobacillus abundance correlates with unsuccessful embryo implantation and poor assisted reproductive technology outcomes. These findings underscore the critical role of a Lactobacillus-dominated endometrial microbiota in achieving pregnancy success and suggest that microbiota composition may serve as a predictive biomarker for IVF outcomes.

title: Hormonal Contraceptives and the Gut Microbiome in Female Athletes: Implications for Health, Performance, and Exercise-Related Physiological Adjustments
summary:
# Summary

Hormonal contraceptives substantially alter the gut microbiome composition and function in ways that may have clinical implications, particularly through disruption of the estrobolome—the consortium of microbes that bidirectionally regulate estrogen metabolism. Combined oral contraceptives reduce microbial diversity and beneficial short-chain fatty acid (SCFA)-producing bacteria such as Clostridium species and Eubacterium eligens, while simultaneously enriching Akkermansia and other taxa, resulting in reduced SCFA production that compromises colonocyte energy supply and immune function. The mechanisms underlying these shifts include disruption of estrobolome function, alterations in bile acid metabolism and gastrointestinal motility, and immunomodulation of gut-associated lymphoid tissue. SCFAs, produced primarily by Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes, function as critical signaling molecules through G-protein coupled receptors and histone deacetylase inhibition, maintaining intestinal barrier integrity and glucose homeostasis. In female athletes specifically, the microbiota-disrupting effects of hormonal contraceptives may conflict with the microbiota-enhancing benefits of physical activity, which naturally promotes microbial diversity and enriches amino acid and carbohydrate metabolism—functions essential for athletic performance, nutrient absorption, and immune regulation.

title: Endometrial microbiome during early pregnancy among women with and without chronic endometritis: a pilot study
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis is strongly associated with multiple adverse reproductive outcomes including infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, recurrent implantation failure, and premature birth, characterized by plasma cell infiltration into endometrial tissue. This pilot study identified significant dysbiosis in the endometrial microbiome of women with chronic endometritis during early pregnancy, with reduced Lactobacillus abundance (10% vs 14%) and elevated levels of pathogenic bacteria including Faecalibacterium, Escherichia-Shigella, and Akkermansia compared to healthy controls. Lactobacillus, Gardnerella, and Faecalibacterium were identified as key biomarkers distinguishing chronic endometritis cases, with dysbiotic shifts in these bacterial populations linked to adverse pregnancy outcomes. The microbiome composition was further shaped by reproductive history, with delivery number correlating positively with Akkermansia and negatively with Sphingomonas abundance, suggesting that maternal microbial dynamics influence conception ability in chronic endometritis. These findings suggest that dysbiosis-driven endometrial inflammation may represent a tractable mechanism underlying endometritis-associated reproductive failure, potentially opening avenues for microbiota-targeted interventions to improve pregnancy outcomes.

title: The Gut–Endometriosis Axis: Genetic Mechanisms and Public Health Implications
summary:
# Summary

Recent research has identified a critical gut–endometriosis axis whereby dysbiosis—characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory bacteria such as *Escherichia coli* and *Clostridium* species alongside reduced beneficial bacteria like *Lactobacillus* and *Bifidobacterium*—contributes to endometriosis development and its hallmark symptoms of chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. The dysbiotic microbiota disrupts immune homeostasis and increases intestinal permeability, allowing lipopolysaccharides to trigger systemic inflammation while simultaneously dysregulating estrogen metabolism through bacterial β-glucuronidase activity, which elevates systemic estrogen levels that drive ectopic endometrial lesion growth and persistence. Host genetics influence both endometriosis susceptibility and microbiota composition through variants in genes like WNT4, GREB1, and FSHB, while anti-inflammatory bacterial taxa such as *Clostridiales vadin BB60* and *Oxalobacteraceae* appear protective. Therapeutic interventions including probiotics and dietary modifications—particularly high-fiber diets rich in fruits, vegetables, and fermented foods—show promise in reducing inflammation and endometriotic lesions by restoring microbial diversity and promoting short-chain fatty acid production, which modulates gene expression through histone deacetylase inhibition.

title: Carbohydrate Intake and Bacterial Vaginosis: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review demonstrates that dietary carbohydrate composition significantly influences bacterial vaginosis (BV) risk through microbiota-mediated mechanisms. High glycemic load intake is positively associated with BV development, progression, and persistence, while higher dietary fiber intake inversely protects against BV. The mechanism involves a gut-vaginal axis whereby dietary carbohydrates alter gut microbiota composition—with simple sugars promoting pathogenic flora and fiber supporting beneficial bacteria—ultimately affecting vaginal microbial balance and immune function. High-glycemic foods elevate blood glucose, triggering insulin release and oxidative damage that increases systemic inflammation, which further dysregulates both gut and vaginal microbiota. Specific micronutrients including vitamins A, C, and E, beta-carotene, zinc, and calcium reduce BV risk, suggesting that comprehensive dietary modification toward low glycemic load and high fiber intake may be protective.
