Notably, BV is strongly linked to endometriosis along with infertility, pelvic inflammatory disease, and sexually transmitted infections, highlighting the clinical importance of dietary intervention in managing these interconnected reproductive health conditions.

title: Role of curcumin in altering gut microbiota for anti-obesity and anti-hyperlipidemic effects
summary:
# Summary

This review examines curcumin's anti-obesity and anti-hyperlipidemic mechanisms through gut microbiota modulation. Curcumin, a natural phenolic antioxidant from *Curcuma longa*, reduces obesity and hyperlipidemia by suppressing pathogenic bacteria that elevate serum lipopolysaccharide levels and activate the pro-inflammatory TLR-4/NF-κB signaling pathway. The compound simultaneously promotes beneficial bifidobacteria, which increase CD4+ CD25+ regulatory T cells that inhibit NF-κB-mediated inflammation. Curcumin additionally improves metabolic dysfunction by reducing insulin resistance, glucose levels, and oxidative stress markers while increasing GLUT4 gene expression. The therapeutic mechanism also involves promoting short-chain fatty acid production by healthy microbiota, which stimulates satiety hormones (GLP-1 and PYY) and activates the AMPK pathway to enhance fatty acid oxidation while suppressing lipid synthesis. These multi-targeted effects on microbiota composition, inflammatory signaling, and metabolic pathways position curcumin as a promising natural therapeutic agent for obesity and related metabolic disorders.

title: Identifying a Vaginal Microbiome-Derived Selective Antibiotic Metabolite via Microbiome Pharmacology Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies hydroxyisocaproate (HICA), a metabolite produced by vaginal Lactobacillus species, as a selective antibiotic with therapeutic potential for bacterial vaginosis (BV). Healthy vaginal microbiota dominated by Lactobacillus maintains immune and epithelial homeostasis through lactic acid production and protective biofilm formation, while BV—characterized by loss of Lactobacillus and overgrowth of anaerobic pathogens like Gardnerella vaginalis—disrupts epithelial barriers, activates inflammation, and associates with endometriosis, infertility, and preterm birth. Through microbiome pharmacology analysis, the authors demonstrate that HICA, derived from leucine metabolism, selectively inhibits G. vaginalis growth by reducing pH while causing minimal inhibition of L. crispatus, and enhances epithelial barrier integrity and cellular density in a vaginal-on-chip model. As a naturally occurring metabolite found in fermented foods with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, HICA represents a potentially translatable alternative to conventional antibiotics like metronidazole and clindamycin for restoring healthy vaginal microbiota.

title: Distinct Microbial Signatures along the Female Reproductive Tract in Endometrial Cancer Patients
summary:
# Summary

Microbiota dysbiosis along the female reproductive tract contributes significantly to endometrial cancer pathogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Endometrial cancer patients exhibit altered microbial signatures characterized by enrichment of pathogenic taxa including Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Akkermansia muciniphila, Bacillus, Streptomyces, and Burkholderia species across vaginal and endometrial sites, coupled with depletion of protective Actinobacteriota, particularly Gardnerella. These dysbiotic shifts promote proinflammatory responses and metabolic dysregulation—specifically, enriched Acinetobacter correlates with elevated fasting plasma glucose and hyperglycemia, while Pseudomonas associates with estrogen and progesterone receptor expression and may be stimulated by elevated estrogen levels to enhance steroid sulfatase activity. The dysbiotic microbiota-driven inflammation elevates pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and fibrinogen, establishing a pathogenic milieu that, combined with hyperglycemia-induced increases in estrogen and insulin-like growth factor bioavailability, promotes endometrial proliferation and cancer development. Notably, protective bacteria like Lactobacillus crispatus exert anti-proliferative effects, suggesting that microbiota restoration may represent a therapeutic target in endometrial cancer prevention and management.

title: Microbial metabolites short chain fatty acids, tight junction, gap junction, and reproduction: a review
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbiota dysbiosis compromises reproductive health through altered production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), particularly butyrate, which regulate critical barrier functions and reproductive signaling. Butyrate maintains intestinal barrier integrity by upregulating tight junction proteins like CLDN1 and MUC2 while downregulating permeable CLDN2, and by activating gap junction protein CX43 to preserve gut motility and epithelial integrity. At the reproductive level, butyrate and propionic acid upregulate gap junction proteins (CX43, CX37) essential for follicular growth and oocyte maturation, while also promoting progesterone and estradiol synthesis through cAMP signaling pathways. In the endometrium, butyrate improves receptivity and reduces inflammation by enhancing tight junction proteins (CLDN3, occludin) and inhibiting inflammatory responses, while progesterone counterregulates connexin expression to prevent premature uterine contractions. The review establishes a mechanistic link whereby microbiota-derived butyrate orchestrates both intestinal barrier function and reproductive tract homeostasis through coordinated regulation of claudins and connexins, suggesting that dysbiosis-associated SCFA deficiency may underlie endometrial dysfunction and impaired fertility.

title: Investigation of the Endometrial Microbiome in Recurrent Pregnancy Loss Individuals: Microbial Imbalance and Network Fragility
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a critical association between endometrial microbial dysbiosis and recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), characterized by a significant reduction in protective Lactobacillus species and elevated pathogenic bacteria including Gardnerella, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Fusobacterium. Machine learning analysis identified Streptococcus, Chryseobacterium, and Fusobacterium as key genera driving RPL risk, while pathogenic microorganisms and their endotoxins trigger abnormal inflammatory responses in the endometrium that impair pregnancy establishment. Network analysis demonstrated that healthy control subjects maintain a complex, stable microbial interaction network with strong cooperative interactions and high Lactobacillus connectivity, whereas RPL patients exhibit severely fragmented networks with low connectivity and minimal Lactobacillus involvement. The findings underscore that Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota (>90%) are associated with significantly higher implantation, pregnancy, and live birth rates, whereas dysbiosis-related pathogens like Gardnerella and Atopobium promote chronic endometritis and implantation failure. These results establish endometrial microbiota composition and network integrity as critical determinants of reproductive outcomes and potential therapeutic targets for RPL management.

title: The Impact of Lifestyle on Reproductive Health: Microbial Complexity, Hormonal Dysfunction, and Pregnancy Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Lifestyle factors including diet, sleep, stress, and physical activity fundamentally shape microbiome composition, which in turn regulates hormonal balance and reproductive health through multiple interconnected pathways. The gut microbiome directly influences the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and estrogen metabolism, while microbial metabolites like short-chain fatty acids (particularly butyrate and propionate) modulate immune function, DNA methylation, and gut barrier integrity. Dysbiosis—characterized by reduced beneficial bacteria (Akkermansia muciniphila, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii) and altered Firmicutes-to-Bacteroidetes ratios—triggers chronic low-grade inflammation, insulin resistance, thyroid disturbances, and disruptions in the reproductive-hormonal axis, increasing the risk of infertility, polycystic ovary syndrome, gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, and spontaneous abortion. Stress, poor diet, and physical inactivity exacerbate these hormonal dysfunctions, while opportunistic fungi like Candida species and pathogenic archaea and viruses further compromise reproductive outcomes through inflammatory mechanisms. Conversely, optimization of lifestyle factors and restoration of a healthy microbiota—particularly maintaining vaginal Lactobacillus dominance and intestinal microbial diversity—supports endometrial receptivity, hormonal balance, and improved pregnancy outcomes.

title: Gut Microbiome and Estrogen
summary:
# Summary

Estrogens regulate multiple physiological systems including the reproductive, neuroendocrine, skeletal, and cardiovascular systems, while the gut microbiome maintains internal balance and modulates estrogen levels through a bidirectional relationship. Dysbiosis—an imbalance in microbial composition—disrupts this equilibrium and contributes to multiple disease states including cardiovascular disorders, cancers, osteoporosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease through elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines. Estrogen depletion, often resulting from microbiome imbalance, impairs cognitive function, bone density, and skin health while increasing pain sensitivity and gut permeability during hormonal fluctuations. The bacterial enzyme β-glucuronidase plays a critical role in estrogen metabolism, with reduced activity correlating to decreased circulating estrogen levels. Sex hormone fluctuations directly impact the brain-gut axis and visceral sensitivity, while estrogens enhance microbial diversity and diversity-dependent estrogen metabolism, illustrating the complex interplay between hormonal status and microbial health in maintaining systemic homeostasis.

title: Gut Microbiota Shifts with Lifetime Duration of Oral Contraception Use
summary: This study reveals that lifetime duration of oral contraceptive use is associated with significant shifts in gut microbiota composition, particularly affecting bacterial taxa involved in estrogen metabolism. Extended oral contraceptive use decreases the abundance of Clostridia species, which normally produce beta-glucuronidase to facilitate estrogen recycling through enterohepatic recirculation, while simultaneously increasing Bacteroides plebeius and decreasing Prevotella and other Firmicutes members. The progressive reduction in Clostridia with prolonged oral contraceptive exposure suggests that long-term hormonal contraception gradually reshapes the microbiome in ways that could alter systemic estrogen bioavailability and hormone regulation. Since dysbiosis—characterized by an elevated Firmicutes-to-Bacteroidota ratio and reduced bacterial diversity—is associated with multiple conditions including polycystic ovarian syndrome, endometriosis, and inflammation, the microbiota alterations induced by oral contraceptives may have broader implications for reproductive and metabolic health. These findings establish a mechanistic link between oral contraceptive use and microbiota-mediated changes in estrogen metabolism that warrant investigation of long-term clinical consequences for women using hormonal contraception.

title: Advancing Digital Precision Medicine for Chronic Fatigue Syndrome through Longitudinal Large-Scale Multi-Modal Biological Omics Modeling with Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence
summary:
# Summary

Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS) is a complex debilitating disorder characterized by severe fatigue, post-exertional malaise, and multiple systemic symptoms including gastrointestinal disturbances, sleep problems, and muscle pain. Using multi-omics analysis, this study reveals that ME/CFS involves disrupted microbiome-immune-metabolome crosstalk, with short-term patients showing pronounced microbial dysbiosis characterized by decreased bacterial diversity and reduced abundance of key metabolite-producing microbes, while long-term patients develop a stable but metabolically aberrant microbiota. Critical findings include reduced production of short-chain fatty acids (particularly butyrate), bile acids, tryptophan, and other microbial metabolites that normally regulate immune function, alongside activation of pro-inflammatory MAIT and γδ T cells that secrete interferon-γ and granzyme A. Machine learning models incorporating multi-omics data identified reduced butyrate-, tryptophan-, and propionic acid-producing microbes, decreased betaine biosynthesis genes, and altered serotonin and cholesterol metabolism as key discriminatory biomarkers for ME/CFS classification. These findings suggest that restoration of microbial metabolic functions and immune regulation through targeted microbiota modulation may represent a therapeutic avenue for this debilitating condition.

title: Gut microbiome species Levilactobacillus brevis regulates reproductive fitness in C. elegans
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the gut bacterium *Levilactobacillus brevis* impairs reproductive function in *C. elegans* through disruption of serotonin signaling in hermaphrodite-specific neurons (HSNs) that control egg-laying behavior. Exposure to *L. brevis* causes severe reproductive dysfunction including egg retention, reduced brood size, abnormal egg morphologies, and significantly decreased embryonic viability and progeny development in offspring. The mechanism involves *L. brevis*-induced structural defects in HSNs, including reduced cell body size and decreased fluorescence intensity, which impair the neurons' capacity to produce and release serotonin—a critical signaling molecule that activates vulval muscles and ventral cord neurons necessary for egg laying. Notably, exogenous serotonin administration rescued egg-laying in *L. brevis*-fed worms, confirming serotonin insufficiency as the primary pathophysiological mechanism. These findings are clinically relevant as gut dysbiosis is increasingly recognized as a contributing factor to reproductive health disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and gynecologic cancers, suggesting that dysbiotic microbiota composition may underlie reproductive dysfunction through similar microbial-neural-endocrine pathways.

title: From gut to gamete: how the microbiome influences fertility and preconception health
summary:
# Summary

The gut microbiota plays a critical role in women's reproductive health by producing metabolites that regulate metabolic, immune, and hormonal functions essential for fertility. Women with reproductive disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, primary ovarian insufficiency, and decreased ovarian reserve exhibit distinct microbial signatures, and dysbiosis is associated with infertility, recurrent implantation failure, and poor responses to assisted reproductive technologies. Western dietary patterns rich in fat and ultra-processed foods disrupt the microbiota, reducing short-chain fatty acid production and triggering intestinal permeability and inflammation that cascade into oocyte dysfunction, including lipid accumulation, mitochondrial impairment, oxidative stress, and decreased fertilization rates. Animal studies demonstrate that commensal bacteria are required for sexual maturity onset and normal ovarian development, while microbiota disruption accelerates ovarian aging and depletes primordial follicle reserves. Preconception antibiotic exposure and broad-spectrum antibiotic use reduce microbial diversity and short-chain fatty acid availability, linking to increased infertility and adverse pregnancy outcomes. These findings highlight that dietary interventions to restore microbiota composition and function represent an underexplored therapeutic avenue for improving preconception health and fertility outcomes.

title: The Dose-Dependent Influence of Type 2 Resistant Starch on Gut Microbial Communities and Metabolic Outputs: An In Vitro Simulation
summary: Type 2 resistant starch (RS2) modulates the gut microbiota in a dose-dependent manner, shifting microbial metabolism from proteolytic to saccharolytic pathways by enriching beneficial bacteria including Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus, and Roseburia while suppressing pathogenic taxa such as Escherichia-Shigella and Fusobacterium. RS2 fermentation by these beneficial bacteria produces elevated levels of short-chain fatty acids (acetate, propionate, and butyrate) that maintain gastrointestinal epithelial integrity and promote colonic health. The highest RS2 concentration (15 g/L) demonstrates optimal metabolic outcomes by redirecting tryptophan metabolism away from potentially harmful indole derivatives toward neuroprotective metabolites such as indole-3-propionic acid while simultaneously decreasing toxic metabolites like hydrogen sulfide and kynurenine. These metabolic shifts confer multiple physiological benefits including prevention of colitis, improved glucose homeostasis, enhanced insulin sensitivity, and glycemic control through butyrate-mediated systemic metabolic regulation. The strong positive correlations between beneficial microbes and both short-chain fatty acids and neuroprotective metabolites suggest that RS2 supplementation may provide a mechanistic basis for improving conditions associated with microbiota dysbiosis, including endometriosis through restoration of intestinal barrier function and immune tolerance.

title: Vaginal Microbiome and Its Relationship with Assisted Reproduction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that vaginal microbiome composition significantly impacts fertility outcomes in assisted reproduction. Women with a favorable microbiome dominated by Lactobacillus species, particularly *Lactobacillus crispatus*, achieved substantially higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates compared to those with dysbiotic microbiota, while unfavorable microbiome status was associated with increased miscarriage risk. Dysbiosis—characterized by loss of Lactobacillus dominance and increased abundance of pathogenic bacteria such as *Gardnerella vaginalis*, *Prevotella* species, and *Atopobium*—compromises the vaginal epithelial barrier, promotes endometrial infection, and increases susceptibility to sexually transmitted infections and chronic endometritis, all of which contribute to implantation failure and infertility. The review identifies lifestyle factors (smoking, alcohol consumption, intimate hygiene practices) and assisted reproduction interventions themselves as key disruptors: controlled ovarian stimulation and progesterone supplementation reduce Lactobacillus abundance while promoting pathogenic species overgrowth, thereby diminishing ongoing pregnancy rates. These findings underscore the critical role of maintaining Lactobacillus-dominated eubiosis for reproductive success and suggest that microbiome assessment and modification strategies may improve assisted reproduction outcomes.

title: Influence of Tempeh, Daidzein, Probiotics, and Their Combination on Magnesium Status and Hematological Ratios in a Postmenopausal Osteoporotic Animal Model
summary:
# Summary

Postmenopausal osteoporosis results from estrogen deficiency, which impairs bone remodeling and disrupts magnesium homeostasis, leading to elevated inflammatory markers (neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio) and metabolic dysfunction (elevated triglyceride-to-glucose index indicating insulin resistance). Magnesium deficiency exacerbates this pro-inflammatory state and oxidative stress, while the combination of tempeh and probiotics effectively restored magnesium levels and reduced metabolic indices compared to standard bisphosphonate treatment. Tempeh provides isoflavones with estrogenic activity that reduce systemic inflammation and modulate magnesium metabolism, while Lactobacillus acidophilus probiotics enhance mineral bioavailability and improve intestinal absorption by modulating the microbiota to hydrolyze isoflavone conjugates into bioactive aglycone forms. Notably, spleen magnesium correlated inversely with platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio and liver magnesium correlated positively with the triglyceride-to-glucose index, suggesting magnesium status directly influences hematological and metabolic health in osteoporotic conditions. These findings highlight that targeted dietary interventions combining phytoestrogen-rich foods with specific probiotics may address the interconnected bone, mineral, and inflammatory dysfunction in postmenopausal osteoporosis more effectively than conventional pharmacological approaches alone.

title: Unraveling the links between estrogen and gut microbiota in sex-hormone driven cancers
summary:
# Summary

This article elucidates the bidirectional relationship between estrogen metabolism and gut microbiota composition in sex-hormone-driven cancers, particularly endometrial and ovarian cancers. The gut microbiome regulates circulating estrogen levels through bacterial production of β-glucuronidase, an enzyme that deconjugates estrogens into their bioactive forms; dysbiosis impairs this process, reducing glucuronidase-producing bacteria like Bacteroidetes and Firmicutes, which subsequently decreases circulating estrogens. Conversely, elevated estrogen levels stimulate proliferation of epithelial cells in the reproductive tract and promote the expansion of pathogenic bacteria such as Proteobacteria, Porphyromonas, and Atopobium vaginae, creating a pathogenic cycle that facilitates ectopic endometrial invasion and carcinogenesis. Dysbiosis-associated dysregulation of the estrobolome (the estrogen-metabolizing bacterial consortium) increases circulating estrogen and cancer risk, while beneficial bacteria like Faecalibacterium and Ruminococcaceae enhance anti-tumoral immune responses. Dietary factors such as high-fat diets further exacerbate dysbiosis by promoting pathobiont expansion, and reduced estrogen signaling impairs intestinal homeostasis and increases metabolic syndrome risk, collectively contributing to sex-hormone-driven malignancies.

title: Discovering a new paradigm: Gut microbiota as a central modulator of sexual health
summary:
# Summary

This article establishes the gut microbiota as a central regulator of sexual health through multiple interconnected biological pathways. A balanced microbiota maintains optimal levels of sex hormones like estrogen and testosterone while producing short-chain fatty acids that support mucosal barrier integrity and regulate immune responses. Dysbiosis—an imbalance in microbial composition—is associated with sexual dysfunctions including erectile dysfunction, polycystic ovary syndrome, and reduced libido, acting through increased systemic inflammation, impaired nitric oxide production, hormonal imbalances, and altered gut-brain axis signaling that affects mood and neurotransmitter levels. Key commensal bacteria including Bifidobacterium, Akkermansia muciniphila, and Lactobacillus species contribute to immune regulation and protective mechanisms, while the vaginal microbiota similarly influences female reproductive health through lactic acid production, which maintains acidic conditions and inhibits pathogenic infections. Lifestyle factors including diet, stress, and antibiotic use modulate microbial composition, and emerging therapeutic strategies such as probiotics, prebiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation offer potential interventions to restore microbial balance and improve sexual function.

title: Aerobic exercise modalities on gut microbiome and skeletal muscle quality in ovariectomized mice
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that eight weeks of aerobic exercise in estrogen-deficient ovariectomized mice substantially improves skeletal muscle mass, grip strength, and fatigue resistance while simultaneously increasing gut microbial diversity and restoring beneficial bacterial populations. The exercise-induced benefits operate through multiple interconnected mechanisms: aerobic exercise activates the Akt/mTOR/p-S6 protein synthesis pathway while inhibiting FOXO3-mediated protein degradation, and it upregulates aromatase expression and intramuscular estradiol levels to compensate for systemic estrogen deficiency. Exercise-driven improvements in gut microbiota diversity—particularly increased abundance of beneficial Bacteroidota—correlate strongly with enhanced lean mass and fatigue resistance, likely through microbial production of short-chain fatty acids that activate AMPK signaling to promote protein synthesis and mitochondrial biogenesis in skeletal muscle. These findings reveal that aerobic exercise mitigates estrogen deficiency-induced sarcopenia by coordinating local muscle estrogen synthesis with restoration of microbial diversity, establishing the gut-muscle axis as a critical therapeutic target for exercise interventions in postmenopausal or hormone-deficient states.

title: Leveraging the role of the microbiome in endometriosis: novel non-invasive and therapeutic approaches
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is strongly associated with gut microbiome dysbiosis characterized by depletion of beneficial Lactobacillus species, elevation of pathogenic bacteria like Escherichia coli, and altered Bacillota/Bacteroidota ratios, which collectively drive chronic inflammation and immune dysfunction. Dysbiotic microbiota compromise intestinal barrier integrity and impair production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), particularly butyrate, which normally suppress endometriotic cell growth and maintain mucosal immunity through activation of anti-inflammatory pathways like JAK1/STAT3 signaling. Pathogenic bacterial metabolites and lipopolysaccharides breach the damaged intestinal barrier, activating TLR4-mediated NF-κB signaling cascades that amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine production and prostaglandin E2 synthesis, contributing to the multi-symptom presentation of endometriosis including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. Novel microbiome-based therapeutic interventions including Lactobacillus-based probiotics and fecal microbiota transplantation show promise in restoring microbial balance and SCFA production, thereby attenuating the dysbiosis-driven inflammatory pathology underlying endometriosis. These findings establish the microbiota-immune axis as a critical mechanistic target for developing non-invasive diagnostic biomarkers and microbiota-modulating treatments for endometriosis.

title: Gut microbiome in adult Asians with obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and a combination of obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus
journal: Bioscience of Microbiota, Food and Health
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbiome dysbiosis is significantly associated with obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus in adult Asian populations, characterized by reduced microbial diversity, increased gut permeability, and loss of the protective gut mucosal barrier. Beneficial bacterial taxa including Akkermansia muciniphila, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, and Bifidobacterium species are substantially decreased in obese and diabetic individuals, while pathogenic bacteria such as Escherichia, Lactobacillus acidophilus, and Fusobacterium species are correspondingly elevated. These protective bacteria exert metabolic benefits through multiple mechanisms: A. muciniphila and Faecalibacterium produce short-chain fatty acids (propionate and butyrate) that enhance tight junction protein expression, regulate fatty acid metabolism, and promote glucagon-like peptide-1 secretion to improve insulin sensitivity, while Bifidobacterium species reduce inflammation and insulin resistance. The dysbiotic profile observed in obesity and type 2 diabetes—characterized by elevated Firmicutes abundance, reduced Akkermansia and Blautia, and increased pathogenic taxa—directly contributes to metabolic dysfunction through compromised intestinal barrier integrity and impaired production of beneficial metabolites.

title: The role of dysbiosis in shaping host immunity in endometrial cancer development
summary:
# Summary

Dysbiosis of the endometrial microbiome plays a critical role in endometrial cancer development through disruption of host immunity and chronic inflammation. The endometrium harbors a complex microbial community dominated by species such as Lactobacillus, Gardnerella, and Prevotella, which under healthy conditions maintain epithelial barrier integrity, restrict pathogenic invasion, and promote immune tolerance necessary for embryo implantation. When dysbiosis occurs, pathogenic bacterial strains including Klebsiella pneumoniae, Clostridium botulinum, and Haemophilus influenzae become overrepresented and trigger inflammation through pattern recognition by endometrial epithelial and stromal cells, while simultaneously destabilizing the host genome through bacterial toxin release and altered gene expression. This dysbiosis-induced chronic inflammation creates immunopathological conditions that promote tumorigenesis, with similar microbiota composition changes also associated with other reproductive disorders including endometriosis, infertility, and polyps. The article emphasizes that the endometrial microbiota functions as a critical modulator of local immunity, and restoration of eubiotic conditions may represent a preventive or therapeutic strategy for cancer development in the reproductive tract.

title: Exploring Insights Into Vaginal Microbiome Profiles in Relation to Intrauterine Insemination Success Rates
summary:
# Vaginal Microbiome Composition and Intrauterine Insemination Success

Vaginal microbiome composition significantly predicts intrauterine insemination (IUI) success, with Lactobacillus-dominant profiles independently associated with improved clinical pregnancy and implantation rates compared to non-dominant profiles. L. crispatus, the primary beneficial Lactobacillus species, maintains acidic vaginal pH, suppresses pathogenic organisms, and directly promotes sperm survival and embryo implantation while improving live birth rates with assisted reproductive technologies. In contrast, low Lactobacillus abundance combined with high anaerobic bacterial diversity—particularly enrichment in Gardnerella and Atopobium species—promotes chronic inflammation and dramatically reduces implantation success by increasing local cytokines that impair embryo receptivity. L. iners, though a Lactobacillus species, is associated with adverse reproductive outcomes due to reduced microbiota stability. Probiotic supplementation can restore eubiotic conditions and improve conception outcomes, while pretreatment microbiome profiling may identify women at risk for poor treatment responses, suggesting therapeutic potential for microbiome-targeted interventions in fertility management.

title: Parkin overexpression modulates gut-microbiota composition during aging in Drosophila melanogaster
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Parkin overexpression extends lifespan in *Drosophila melanogaster* by modulating gut microbiota composition toward a eubiotic, less inflammatory state. The mechanism involves Parkin's role in mitochondrial quality control through the PINK1/Parkin circuit, which gut-derived metabolites (short-chain fatty acids, bile-acid derivatives, and indole metabolites) enhance by promoting high ATP output, low reactive oxygen species, and robust IgA secretion in colonocytes. Age-related dysbiosis in control flies—characterized by loss of beneficial bacteria like *Gluconacetobacter* and *Pasteurella* and expansion of pathobionts—leads to mitochondrial dysfunction, local hypoxia, and pathobiont-derived toxins (lipopolysaccharides, p-cresol, hydrogen sulfide) that further impair respiration and trigger inflammation. Critically, microbiota transplant experiments reveal that the balanced microbial profile from Parkin-overexpressing flies maintains lifespan and reduces immune activation in germ-free recipients, whereas aged control microbiota reduces lifespan and triggers proinflammatory responses. These findings establish a bidirectional gut-mitochondria axis linking mitochondrial quality control, microbiota composition, and healthy aging, with potential implications for understanding dysbiosis-associated pathologies.

title: Evaluating the effects of hospital wastewater treatment on bacterial composition and antimicrobial resistome
summary:
# Summary

Hospital wastewater treatment systems significantly reduce antimicrobial resistance genes (ARGs) and alter bacterial composition, decreasing Gammaproteobacteria while enriching Betaproteobacteria and Actinobacteria in treated effluent. The study identified 140 non-redundant ARGs in hospital wastewater, including clinically relevant beta-lactam resistance genes (blaCTX-M, blaKPC, blaNDM) and last-resort antibiotic resistance genes (vancomycin, linezolid, and colistin resistance), with blaKPC-2 showing the highest relative abundance among beta-lactam genes. Although treatment reduced overall ARG abundance by 70%, certain resistance genes including quaternary ammonium compound resistance (qacF, qacG2) paradoxically increased in effluent, while ARGs located on plasmids or adjacent to mobile genetic elements showed smaller reductions, highlighting the role of these mobile elements in preserving resistance determinants during treatment. These findings underscore the importance of optimizing hospital wastewater treatment to mitigate the environmental dissemination of clinically important antimicrobial resistance mechanisms.

title: Microbiome impacts quality of life in patients with endometrial cancer and benign gynecological conditions
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that vaginal and rectal microbiome composition significantly impacts quality of life in patients with endometrial cancer and benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis and fibroids, all of which are associated with poor sexual and overall quality of life outcomes. Specific bacterial taxa demonstrate distinct associations with sexual function and vaginal symptoms: pathogenic bacteria such as Porphyromonas and Campylobacter negatively correlate with sexual interest, while beneficial organisms like Dialister B and Lagierella are associated with improved sexual interest and lower vaginal symptoms. Endometrial cancer patients exhibit characteristic microbiome alterations including elevated vaginal pH, increased species richness, and enrichment of pathogenic taxa including Porphyromonas and Fannyhessea implicated in cancer pathogenesis, in contrast to benign condition patients who show higher sexual interest and better vaginal assessment scores. Perceived stress emerges as a critical modulator, strongly correlating with worse vaginal and vulvar symptoms, reduced sexual interest, and poor mental and physical health across all patient groups. The findings suggest that microbiome-mediated mechanisms, including altered estrogen metabolism, central nervous system modulation, and dysbiosis-induced symptoms, contribute to quality of life impairment in gynecologic disease, offering potential targets for microbiome-based interventions to improve patient outcomes.

title: Exploring cervicovaginal microbiome differences between single and multiple endometrial polyps: implications for non-invasive classification
summary: This study reveals distinct cervicovaginal microbiota signatures that differentiate single from multiple endometrial polyps, with important implications for non-invasive clinical classification. Endometrial polyps are associated with chronic endometritis, abnormal uterine bleeding, and dysbiotic microbiota characterized by reduced Lactobacillus crispatus and elevated pathogenic species including Atopobium vaginae, Sneathia species, and Lactobacillus iners. Multiple endometrial polyps show higher recurrence rates and elevated expression of estrogen receptor, vascular endothelial growth factor, and transforming growth factor β1, while displaying a distinct microbiota profile enriched in Lactobacillus acidophilus and Corynebacterium amycolatum. Single endometrial polyps are characterized by specific bacterial taxa (Propionibacterium sp., Acinetobacter baumannii, Enterococcus faecium) and enriched metabolic pathways including aminoacyl-tRNA biosynthesis and pyrimidine metabolism. The analysis identified Lactobacillus iners abundance and fibrinogen as key drivers for endometrial polyp classification in multivariate models, suggesting that cervicovaginal microbiota composition offers a promising non-invasive biomarker for distinguishing polyp subtypes and predicting clinical outcomes.

title: Research Progress
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the multifaceted mechanisms underlying poststroke depression, a complex neuropsychiatric complication following cerebrovascular events. Stroke-induced brain tissue damage—particularly in the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, basal ganglia, and hippocampus—triggers neuroplasticity impairment, dendritic atrophy, and suppressed neurogenesis, all of which compromise emotional regulation and contribute to anhedonia and depressive symptoms. The pathophysiology involves disruption of monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems, with stroke-damaged axons reducing serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine signaling, while simultaneously elevating glutamate and reactive oxygen species. Neuroinflammation plays a central role, with activated microglia and astrocytes triggering the NF-κB/NLRP3 inflammasome axis, leading to release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, interferon-gamma) that further impair synaptic plasticity, suppress brain-derived neurotrophic factor, and disrupt neurotransmitter metabolism. Additionally, stroke-induced hyperactivation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis causes sustained cortisol elevation, which damages hippocampal neurons and disrupts serotonin receptor function, while gut microbiota dysbiosis reduces production of short-chain fatty acids critical for brain health. Therapeutic interventions including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, ketamine, and anti-inflammatory approaches target these interconnected mechanisms to restore neurochemical balance and promote recovery.

title: Analysis of the characteristics of endometrial fluid samples in recurrent pregnancy loss patients based on 16S rRNA gene sequencing technology
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This 16S rRNA gene sequencing study reveals that endometrial microbiota dysbiosis is a key factor in recurrent pregnancy loss, with specific bacterial shifts distinguishing RPL patients from healthy controls. While overall microbial richness (alpha diversity) was similar between groups, beta diversity analysis identified distinct microbial community structures, with pathogenic genera including Vibrio, Pseudoalteromonas, and elevated Gardnerella vaginalis enriched in RPL samples, whereas protective Lactobacillus crispatus was reduced. Dysbiotic microbiota disrupts the endometrial antimicrobial barrier, triggers immune cell imbalances, and promotes inflammatory responses that compromise the embryo-endometrial microenvironment necessary for successful implantation. The study underscores that dysbiosis frequently co-occurs with chronic endometritis and is associated with both recurrent implantation failure and early pregnancy loss, establishing the endometrial microbiota as a critical regulator of reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that restoring a healthy microbial balance, particularly through promoting beneficial Lactobacillus dominance, may be a therapeutic strategy to improve pregnancy outcomes in recurrent loss patients.

title: Assessing the relationship between gut microbiota and hyperprolactinemia: A bidirectional two-sample Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study reveals a bidirectional association between specific gut microbiota composition and hyperprolactinemia, a condition characterized by elevated prolactin levels that causes infertility, galactorrhea, hypogonadism, and menstrual changes. Several bacterial taxa emerged as pathogenic contributors, including Ruminococcus gauvreauii, Eisenbergiella, and Ruminococcaceae UCG014, which increase hyperprolactinemia risk, while protective taxa such as Acidaminococcaceae, Eubacterium ruminantium, Anaerofilum, and Erysipelotrichaceae UCG003 reduce it. The protective bacteria exert their effects through production of short-chain fatty acids and other metabolites that modulate inflammatory immune responses and regulate metabolic homeostasis. Notably, hyperprolactinemia is associated with endometriosis in infertile women, along with depression and anxiety, suggesting gut dysbiosis may contribute to this reproductive endocrine disorder through microbial dysbiosis and altered metabolite production. Current treatment with dopamine agonists addresses symptom management, but understanding these microbiota associations offers potential for microbiota-targeted interventions. These findings highlight the importance of gut microbiota composition in hyperprolactinemia pathogenesis and its broader implications for reproductive and metabolic health.

title: Mechanism of Microbiota-Gut-Brain in Perimenopausal Depression: An Inflammatory Perspective
summary:
# Summary

Perimenopausal depression arises from oestrogen fluctuations and declining ovarian function, creating a physiological state particularly vulnerable to dysbiosis-mediated neuroinflammation through the microbiota-gut-brain axis. Dysbiotic microbiota—characterized by reduced beneficial bacteria (Lactobacillus, Akkermansia, Roseburia) and elevated pro-inflammatory taxa—produces decreased short-chain fatty acids and altered monoamine neurotransmitter availability while dysregulating serotonin, tryptophan, and kynurenine metabolism. This microbiota dysfunction triggers neuroinflammation via TLR4/NF-κB, NLRP3 inflammasome, and JAK-STAT pathway activation, promoting microglial activation that impairs neurogenesis and synaptic function. Current hormone replacement therapy effectively reduces depressive symptoms but carries significant risks including breast cancer, endometrial cancer, thrombosis, and cardiovascular disease, making probiotics and prebiotics promising alternative therapeutics with the potential to restore eubiotic balance and resolve perimenopausal depression through anti-inflammatory mechanisms.

title: The Relationship Between the Vaginal Microbiota and the Ovarian Cancer Microenvironment: A Journey from Ideas to Insights
summary:
# Summary

The vaginal microbiota profoundly influences gynecologic cancer risk through dysbiosis-driven mechanisms linking bacterial composition to ovarian, cervical, and endometrial malignancies. A healthy, Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota maintains vaginal integrity and supports fertility, whereas dysbiotic shifts characterized by pathogenic bacteria such as Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, and Prevotella bivia (Community Status Type IV) promote chronic inflammation, epithelial barrier dysfunction, and immune dysregulation. Dysbiotic microbiota generate carcinogenic metabolites including pro-tumorigenic short-chain fatty acids (acetate and propionate) while depleting the anti-inflammatory metabolite butyrate, simultaneously producing bacterial enzymes that degrade the protective mucin layer and facilitating pathogenic penetration. Multiple oncogenic bacteria and viruses, including Chlamydia trachomatis, HPV, Epstein-Barr virus, and cytomegalovirus, directly induce genotoxic effects and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, compromising DNA repair mechanisms and cellular adhesion. Experimental evidence demonstrates that restoring microbiota balance through broad-spectrum antibiotics suppresses tumor progression and alters metabolite profiles, while specific bacterial signatures including Burkholderia show diagnostic potential as cancer biomarkers, opening therapeutic avenues targeting microbial dysbiosis to prevent and manage gynecologic malignancies.

title: The Gut Microbiota–Sex–Immunity Axis in Non-Communicable Diseases
summary:
# Summary

The gut microbiota plays a fundamental role in immune regulation, metabolic homeostasis, and epithelial barrier function, with these activities significantly influenced by biological sex through sex hormone-microbiota interactions. Estrogens promote microbial diversity and anti-inflammatory responses by upregulating tight junction proteins (occludin and claudin-1), enhancing commensal bacteria ratios, and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-6, whereas androgens are associated with pro-inflammatory conditions and distinct bacterial compositions favoring Firmicutes in men and Bacteroidetes in women. Dysbiosis—characterized by imbalanced microbiota composition—contributes to multiple non-communicable diseases including autoimmune disorders, metabolic disorders, cardiovascular disease, and neurodegenerative conditions, with sex differences significantly influencing disease susceptibility and severity. Microbial metabolites, particularly short-chain fatty acids and secondary bile acids, modulate systemic inflammation and immune regulation through pathways including histone deacetylase inhibition and PPAR-gamma expression, with butyrate showing stronger immunomodulatory effects in males than females. Sex chromosome-encoded immune genes (TLR7, FOXP3) and genetic variants (NOD2/CARD15, Toll-like receptor polymorphisms) further modulate disease outcomes through microbiota-dependent mechanisms, establishing a complex gut microbiota–sex–immunity axis critical to understanding sex-based differences in non-communicable disease susceptibility.

title: Dietary Influence on Bacterial Vaginosis
summary: Bacterial vaginosis results from dysbiosis of the vaginal microbiota characterized by decreased Lactobacillus species and increased anaerobic bacteria such as Gardnerella, which is notably associated with endometriosis. Lactobacillus maintains a protective low vaginal pH that inhibits pathogenic bacterial growth, and dietary factors significantly influence this microbial balance through multiple mechanisms. Nutrient deficiencies in vitamins A, C, D, E, calcium, and betaine increase BV susceptibility, while increased consumption of fruits, vegetables, dietary fiber, and whole grains—rich in phytochemicals and antioxidants—reduce BV risk by supporting Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota and decreasing oxidative stress. Conversely, high glycemic index diets, excess sodium, and sugar-sweetened beverages promote BV, whereas probiotics, dairy, and plant-based proteins foster beneficial Lactobacillus colonization and reduce inflammation. Persistent BV infection carries serious complications including pelvic inflammatory disease, preterm birth, sexually transmitted infections, and infertility, underscoring the clinical importance of dietary intervention as a modifiable risk factor for maintaining vaginal health and preventing associated morbidities.

title: Effects of exogenous protease supplementation of diets containing animal proteins or not on Campylobacter jejuni colonization and on the intestinal microbiota of broiler chickens
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how exogenous protease supplementation affects *Campylobacter jejuni* colonization and intestinal microbiota composition in broiler chickens fed plant-based or animal protein diets. Protease supplementation of vegetarian protein diets reduced *C. jejuni* cecal colonization and prevented hepatic dissemination by increasing protein digestibility, which lowered intestinal digesta viscosity and feed transit time—factors that limit bacterial establishment. The protease-supplemented diet altered cecal microbiota composition by increasing butyrate-producing bacteria (Faecalibacterium, Anaerostipes, and UBA1819) while reducing potential pathobionts (Tuzzerella, Monoglobus), with butyrate providing direct protective effects on the intestinal epithelial barrier against *C. jejuni* invasion. Additionally, the protease treatment may enhance colonization resistance through increased amino acid absorption, reducing nutrient availability for *C. jejuni*, which strictly depends on specific amino acids (serine, glutamine, asparagine, aspartate, and proline) to fuel its metabolism. These findings suggest that dietary protease supplementation represents a promising intervention to control *Campylobacter* in poultry while simultaneously promoting a protective microbiota composition.

title: Cervical microbiota diversity and functional shifts in the development of cervical high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals significant microbiota dysbiosis in women with high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL), characterized by increased diversity and shifts in bacterial composition. Pathogenic genera including Parvimonas, Fastidiosipila, and Pseudomonas are elevated in HSIL, while beneficial taxa such as Porphyromonas, Elizabethkingia, Bacteroides, and Akkermansia are depleted. The dysbiotic microbiota in HSIL exhibits altered metabolic functions, with enriched pathways in glycine, serine, threonine, and sulfur metabolism, along with increased glycosidase activity and decreased oxidoreductase activity, patterns consistent with metabolic dysregulation linked to cancer development. These functional changes, combined with epithelial barrier disruption and altered immune responses, create an environment favoring HPV persistence, particularly of high-risk HPV16/18 strains. The findings support therapeutic potential for Lactobacillus-based interventions, particularly Lactobacillus crispatus and L. rhamnosus, which can promote HPV clearance and improve cervical cytology, suggesting that restoring protective Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota may reduce cervical dysplasia risk.

title: Relationship Between Gut Microbiota and Phenylalanine Levels: A Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: Phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) catalyzes the conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine using tetrahydrobiopterin as an essential cofactor, and PAH deficiency causes phenylketonuria (PKU), leading to elevated blood phenylalanine that results in developmental delays, intellectual disability, and seizures. This Mendelian randomization study identifies a causal link between gut microbiota composition and circulating phenylalanine levels, demonstrating that the Family XIII AD3011 group, a butyrate-producing bacterium from the Firmicutes phylum, is associated with reduced phenylalanine levels, likely through butyrate's inhibition of histone deacetylase and potential upregulation of PAH gene expression. Conversely, several taxa including Christensenellaceae R-7 group, Eisenbergiella, Lachnospiraceae UCG004, Lactobacillus, and Turicibacter are associated with elevated phenylalanine levels. PKU patients exhibit characteristic dysbiosis with reduced Faecalibacterium and Bacteroides abundance but increased Prevotella, Akkermansia, and Peptostreptococcicee, likely exacerbated by phenylalanine-restricted dietary intervention. These findings reveal how gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids regulate phenylalanine metabolism and suggest that microbiome-targeted therapies complementing traditional treatments like sapropterin dihydrochloride and pegvaliase may offer novel therapeutic avenues for managing PKU.

title: Evaluation of microecological therapy in endometriosis through modulation of the gut microbiota
summary:
# Microecological Therapy for Endometriosis: Gut Microbiota as a Therapeutic Target

Gut microbiota dysbiosis is significantly associated with endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The dysbiosis characteristic of endometriosis involves reduced beneficial bacteria (Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus) and elevated pathogenic bacteria (Enterobacteriaceae and Clostridium), which disrupts three critical functions: intestinal barrier integrity, systemic immune homeostasis, and estrogen metabolism. Dysbiosis-induced increases in intestinal permeability allow lipopolysaccharide translocation, activating the NF-κB pathway and driving pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-6, TNF-α) that promotes lesion growth, while dysregulated bacterial β-glucuronidase activity enhances estrogen deconjugation and reabsorption, creating a hyperestrogen environment that exacerbates ectopic endometrial growth. Microecological therapy using synbiotics containing Bifidobacterium longum, Lactobacillus acidophilus, Lactobacillus rhamnosus, and prebiotics (inulin and fructooligosaccharides) effectively reverses these pathological processes: it restores beneficial microbiota abundance, significantly reduces serum inflammatory markers (IL-6, TNF-α, LPS), lowers circulating estradiol levels, strengthens intestinal barrier function, and enhances mucosal immunity markers including CD4+ T lymphocytes and secretory IgA. Clinical outcomes demonstrate that microecological therapy substantially improves postoperative gastrointestinal recovery, reduces postoperative pain and complications, providing a novel adjunctive therapeutic approach targeting the microbi

title: Applications of metabolomics in cow health assessment
summary: Metabolomics has emerged as a powerful analytical tool for early disease detection and health assessment in dairy cattle by identifying disease-specific metabolic biomarkers. Bacterial pathogens including Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and mycoplasmas cause mastitis, a prevalent disease affecting 25-60% of dairy cows worldwide with substantial economic losses. Subclinical and clinical mastitis present distinct metabolic signatures in milk and serum, with elevated amino acids (valine, serine, tyrosine, phenylalanine, isoleucine, proline) and organic acids (lactate, citrate, short-chain fatty acids) characterizing subclinical disease, while clinical mastitis additionally shows reduced glucose, L-carnitine, and citrate alongside increased arginine and multiple elevated metabolites including acetate, formate, benzoate, and β-hydroxybutyrate. Other metabolic diseases like ketosis, acidosis, hypocalcemia, and lameness display characteristic metabolic profiles—ketosis with elevated plasma and urinary β-hydroxybutyrate and acetone, acidosis with increased ruminal lactate and histamine, and pre-lame conditions with elevated serum amino acids and carbohydrate metabolites. Balanced nutrition in carbohydrates, proteins, fats, minerals, and vitamins prevents these metabolic disorders, highlighting how metabolomics can guide both early disease detection and preventive management strategies in dairy cattle.

title: Implication of the Vaginal Microbiome in Female Infertility and Assisted Conception Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Vaginal dysbiosis—characterized by reduced Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium alongside elevated pathogenic genera including Prevotella, Burkholderia, and Pseudomonas—is significantly associated with female infertility and reduced assisted reproductive technology (ART) success rates. Infertile women exhibit higher microbial diversity compared to fertile controls, with recurrent implantation failure (RIF) patients showing even greater diversity and enrichment of anaerobic bacteria such as Mobiluncus, Peptoniphilus, and Varibaculum. Dysbiotic microbiota alterations correlate with multiple reproductive pathologies including tubal disease, ovulatory dysfunction, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including aneuploid pregnancy loss and preterm delivery. The dysbiotic microbiota dysregulates critical metabolic pathways—including colanic acid biosynthesis, fermentation pathways, and N-acetylated sugar degradation—that are linked to vaginal pH disruption and reduced implantation success. Machine learning models trained on vaginal microbial composition achieved over 90% accuracy in predicting infertility status, suggesting microbiota profiling as a potential diagnostic tool. These findings implicate vaginal dysbiosis as a treatable factor in reproductive failure and highlight the therapeutic potential of microbiota-targeted interventions to improve fertility outcomes.

title: Association Between the Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota and Metabolic Syndrome: Mediation Effects of Albumin and Systemic Immune‐Inflammation Index
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how the Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota (DI-GM)—a composite measure of 14 dietary components—influences metabolic syndrome risk in a nationally representative cohort of 59,842 participants. Higher DI-GM scores, reflecting diets rich in whole grains, dietary fibers, and fermented dairy products while low in processed and red meats, were significantly associated with reduced metabolic syndrome risk and its components including abdominal obesity, elevated blood pressure, and abnormal fasting glucose. The protective effects of improved gut microbiota composition operate partially through serum albumin and systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), with these markers mediating 7.44% and 2.20% of the total protective effect respectively. Mechanistically, beneficial bacteria like Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus enhance gut barrier integrity and suppress chronic inflammation via the TLR4/NF-κB pathway, while short-chain fatty acids produced from dietary fiber fermentation regulate glucose and lipid metabolism through G-protein coupled receptors. These findings establish dietary optimization of gut microbiota as a modifiable intervention pathway for preventing metabolic syndrome and its downstream complications including cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.

title: The Mediating Role of Bioactive Molecules in Gut Microbiota–Bone Metabolism Crosstalk
summary:
# Summary

The gut microbiota regulates bone metabolism through bioactive molecules, particularly short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) including butyrate and propionate, which are produced by specific bacterial taxa and modulate osteoporosis risk across multiple pathways. SCFAs exert osteoprotective effects by inhibiting osteoclast differentiation through downregulation of TRAF6 and NFATc1, suppressing RANKL synthesis, and engaging G protein-coupled receptors (GPR43, GPR41, GPR109A) that attenuate inflammatory signaling and promote regulatory T cell differentiation. Additionally, butyrate improves intestinal barrier integrity through tight junction protein upregulation, enhances calcium absorption via transporter expression (TRPV6, calbindin-D9k), and promotes serotonin biosynthesis, while also activating the SCFA–GPR41–p38 MAPK axis with therapeutic potential for postmenopausal osteoporosis. Calcium intake and specific interventions like tuna bone powder and short-chain fructooligosaccharides increase SCFA-producing bacteria abundance and lower luminal pH, enhancing calcium ionization and bioavailability. Sex steroid hormones, particularly estrogen, interact with this microbiota-mediated axis, while SCFA supplementation restores IGF-1 concentrations and bone mineral density in dysbiotic models, establishing the gut–bone axis as a critical target for osteoporosis prevention and treatment.

title: Endometrial Microbiome and Reproductive Receptivity: Diverse Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

The endometrial microbiome plays a critical role in reproductive success, with Lactobacillus dominance associated with endometrial homeostasis, favorable implantation rates, and successful pregnancy outcomes. Conversely, dysbiosis characterized by increased diversity and enrichment of anaerobic bacteria such as Gardnerella, Atopobium, Prevotella, and Streptococcus is linked to chronic endometritis, implantation failure, and adverse in vitro fertilization results. The endometrial microbiota regulates multiple reproductive mechanisms including cytokine signaling, epithelial barrier integrity, receptivity-associated gene expression, and local immune responses that collectively influence embryo implantation and pregnancy maintenance. Non-Lactobacillus dominant microbiota significantly reduces implantation and pregnancy rates, while specific dysbiotic bacteria such as Serratia marcescens, Staphylococcus, and Ureaplasma are associated with implantation failure and miscarriage. These findings suggest that endometrial microbiome profiling represents a promising biomarker for predicting reproductive success and that microbiota dysbiosis may underlie reproductive pathologies including endometriosis, implantation failure, and pregnancy loss.

title: Microbial regulators of physiological and reproductive health in women of reproductive age: their local, proximal and distal regulatory roles
summary:
# Summary

Vaginal health in women of reproductive age depends critically on maintaining a Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota that produces lactic acid to inhibit pathogenic organisms and preserve microbial homeostasis. Estrogen stimulates glycogen accumulation in the vaginal epithelium, which Lactobacillus species ferment to produce lactic acid; however, *Lactobacillus iners*, which lacks the capacity to produce protective metabolites like D-lactic acid and hydrogen peroxide due to genome reduction, can drive dysbiosis by transitioning the microbiota from CST III to the pathogenic CST IV community. CST IV bacteria, including *Gardnerella vaginalis*, *Dialister* spp., and *Prevotella* spp., deplete lactic acid and produce biogenic amines that further inhibit *Lactobacillus* growth and elevate vaginal pH, while also secreting sialidases that degrade the protective mucin layer. This dysbiotic state promotes pro-inflammatory responses through TLR4 recognition of bacterial lipopolysaccharides, activating NF-κB signaling and cytokine production. Imbalanced vaginal microbiota is strongly associated with bacterial vaginosis, cervical cancer, infertility, and preterm delivery, with environmental factors including menstruation, unprotected intercourse, antibiotic use, and vaginal douching driving dysbiosis. Restoring microbial homeostasis through beneficial *Lactobacillus* species dominance, particularly *L. crispatus*, represents a critical intervention strategy for protecting gynecological and reproductive health.

title: The Role of Microbiota in Ovarian Cancer: Implications for Treatment Response and Therapeutic Strategies
summary:
# Summary

Emerging evidence demonstrates that both vaginal and gut microbiota dysbiosis significantly promote ovarian cancer development and progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Dysbiotic states characterized by Lactobacillus depletion and enrichment of anaerobic bacteria such as Pseudomonadota disrupt epithelial and mucosal barriers, leading to elevated DNA damage, chronic inflammation, and activation of pro-tumorigenic pathways including TLR4/NF-κB signaling and estrogen reactivation via estrobolome overactivity. Microbial metabolites including lipopolysaccharides amplify oncogenic signaling, while beneficial short-chain fatty acids like butyrate are depleted, reducing their histone deacetylase inhibitory and pro-apoptotic effects. Notably, Akkermansia supplementation and selective intravaginal antibiotics show promise in restoring antitumor immunity and reducing tumor burden, while aberrant microbiota composition correlates with chemotherapy resistance, particularly platinum resistance associated with lactate-producing taxa expansion. These findings establish the microbiota as a modifiable determinant of ovarian cancer risk, progression, and treatment response, with important implications for developing microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies to enhance clinical outcomes.

title: Characterizing probiotic profiles using genomic and metabolomic insights into chicken cecum-derived Bacillus subtilis YB-114246
summary:
# Summary

Bacillus subtilis YB-114246, isolated from chicken cecum, is a multifunctional probiotic strain with comprehensive metabolic capabilities for enhancing poultry health and productivity. The strain produces high levels of digestive enzymes (cellulase, amylase, protease, and lipase) and functional metabolites including riboflavin, amino acids, phospholipids, eicosanoids, and secondary metabolites (non-ribosomal peptides, lantipeptides, terpenes, and bacteriocins), which collectively improve nutrient absorption and utilization. Genomic analysis revealed 917 genes involved in carbohydrate, amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid metabolism, with B. subtilis YB-114246 possessing elevated expression of metabolic genes compared to related strains, particularly for secondary metabolite biosynthesis and nutrient transport. The strain optimizes intestinal bacterial composition, enhances immune and antioxidant capacity, improves digestive abilities, and significantly boosts laying performance in hens and broiler chickens, with riboflavin and other metabolites showing strong positive correlations with productive outcomes. Adherence-related genes and defense proteins encoded in the genome support intestinal colonization and survival, establishing this strain as an effective probiotic through integrated mechanisms of metabolite production, enzymatic function, and host-microbe interaction.

title: Nettle (Urtica cannabina L.) polysaccharides as a novel dietary supplement: enhancing systemic antioxidant status via modulation of the gut–liver axis
summary:
# Summary

Urtica cannabina polysaccharides (UP) function as a dietary supplement that enhances systemic antioxidant status primarily through modulation of the gut–liver axis. Low-dose UP supplementation significantly improved growth performance, serum lipid profiles, and antioxidant enzyme activities including superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and catalase, while simultaneously reducing oxidative stress markers such as malondialdehyde. UP administration fundamentally altered gut microbiota composition by increasing beneficial bacteria including Parabacteroides, Dubosiella, and Ruminococcaceae while decreasing potentially harmful taxa such as Lachnospiraceae and Desulfovibrionaceae, with the increased bacteria positively correlating with enhanced antioxidant biomarkers. These microbiota changes were accompanied by elevated short-chain fatty acids (particularly acetate and butyrate) and enriched bile acid metabolism pathways, establishing a mechanistic link between microbial composition, metabolite production, and host antioxidant capacity. The dose-dependent effects suggest that low-dose UP (300 mg/kg) provides optimal benefits for metabolic health and antioxidant defense through prebiotic-like modulation of the microbiota–liver–host axis.

title: The female reproductive tract-gut axis in gastrointestinal parasitic infection
summary:
# Summary

The female reproductive tract and gut microbiota maintain critical bidirectional communication that regulates reproductive health through overlapping immune and hormonal mechanisms. Sex steroid hormones, particularly estrogen and progesterone, tightly regulate the FRT immune landscape and cervical mucus composition, while simultaneously the gut microbiome influences FRT immunology through microbial metabolites like short-chain fatty acids that modulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Intestinal helminth infections disrupt this delicate gut-FRT axis by causing dysbiosis, which impairs estrogen homeostasis and dysregulates FRT immunity, ultimately resulting in adverse reproductive outcomes including delayed pregnancy, reduced fetal weight, and increased susceptibility to sexually transmitted infections. The dysbiosis-induced shift toward type 2 immunity via elevated IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13 can paradoxically weaken mucosal defense while exacerbating pathogenic infection severity, and proinflammatory cytokines like TNF-α further compromise reproductive function by suppressing gonadotropin-releasing hormone release. These findings establish the gut-FRT axis as a central yet understudied regulator of female reproductive health with implications for understanding how parasitic infections and microbiota alterations contribute to reproductive disorders.

title: Microbiota insights in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, presenting with chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and digestive symptoms, and increasingly recognized as immune-mediated with systemic associations to autoimmune diseases including lupus, multiple sclerosis, and inflammatory bowel disease. The condition involves multiple pathogenic mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, impaired macrophage clearance, pro-inflammatory cytokines, progesterone resistance, hyperestrogenism, neovascularization, and altered endometrial stem cell function. Recent research reveals significant gut microbiota dysbiosis in endometriosis patients, characterized by reduced α-diversity, elevated Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio, increased pathogenic bacteria (Shigella, Escherichia, Bacteroides), and decreased protective bacteria (Lachnospira, Paraprevotella, Ruminococcaceae). Fecal microbiota transfer from endometriosis-affected mice to healthy mice induced larger lesions, and Western diet-induced dysbiosis worsened disease severity, establishing causality between microbiota alterations and endometriosis progression. A critical mechanistic finding is that dysbiotic microbiota in endometriosis produce reduced levels of the anti-inflammatory short-chain fatty acid n-butyrate, which inhibits endometriotic lesion growth through histone deacetylase inhibition and immune regulation, while butyrate supplementation shows promise as a therapeutic intervention. These findings establish dysbiosis as both a consequence and contributing factor to endometriosis pathogenesis, highlighting the microbiota-immune axis as a potential therapeutic target.

title: Genetically Predicted Causal Risk Factors for Preeclampsia: A Comprehensive Mendelian Randomization Study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies multiple genetic causal factors influencing preeclampsia risk through immune, metabolic, and microbial pathways. Key protective factors include elevated CD62L− monocytes, which impair immune cell homing, and increased CCL19 levels, which enhance regulatory T cell immunosuppressive capacity. Beneficial gut bacteria including Coprococcus catus, Bifidobacterium species, and related taxa produce short-chain fatty acids—particularly butyrate—that promote regulatory T cell differentiation and intestinal barrier integrity, collectively reducing preeclampsia risk. Conversely, risk factors include elevated BAFF-R on B cells, pro-inflammatory CD66b monocytes, Ruminococcaceae taxa (which correlate with elevated leptin and blood pressure), and specific metabolites like guanidinoacetate and 2-linoleoylglycerophosphocholine. The pathophysiology involves dysregulated angiogenic markers (sFlt-1 and soluble endoglin) that suppress vascular endothelial growth factor, combined with disrupted immune balance favoring pro-inflammatory responses and impaired trophoblast invasion. These findings suggest that preeclampsia involves interconnected immune dysregulation and gut dysbiosis, with microbiota-derived metabolites and immune cell populations serving as potential therapeutic targets.

title: Gut Microbiota and Short-Chain Fatty Acids: Key Factors in Pediatric Obesity and Therapeutic Targets
summary:
# Gut Microbiota and Short-Chain Fatty Acids in Pediatric Obesity

Dysbiosis of the gut microbiota, characterized by alterations in the Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio, is strongly associated with pediatric obesity and compromises the production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), particularly butyrate, propionate, and acetate. Specific bacterial genera including Bacteroides, Firmicutes, Faecalibacterium, Roseburia, Bifidobacterium, and Lactobacillus play essential roles in SCFA generation, processes that are impaired by consumption of fast food and saturated fats but enhanced by dietary fiber intake. SCFAs exert multi-target anti-obesity effects through multiple mechanisms: they inhibit histone deacetylases, bind to GPR41 and GPR43 receptors on colonocytes and adipocytes, stimulate incretin hormone secretion (GLP-1 and PYY), suppress appetite-stimulating ghrelin, and promote leptin signaling, collectively reducing food intake while increasing energy expenditure through enhanced lipolysis, fatty acid oxidation, and thermogenesis. Microbiota-modulating interventions including probiotics, synbiotics, prebiotics, and butyrate supplementation demonstrate therapeutic potential for reducing obesity and metabolic syndrome by restoring SCFA-producing capacity and intestinal barrier integrity. These findings establish the gut microbiota and its metabolites as key therapeutic targets for pediatric obesity management.

title: Lactobacillus iners dominates the vaginal microbiota of healthy Italian women of reproductive age
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes the vaginal microbiota of healthy Italian women of reproductive age, revealing that Lactobacillus iners dominates (43.4% prevalence) rather than L. crispatus as observed in Northern European populations, indicating important geographic and population-specific variations. Lactobacillus species, particularly L. iners and L. crispatus, produce lactic acid and antimicrobial molecules that maintain genital health by preventing pathogenic colonization, while dysbiotic microbiota dominated by anaerobic bacteria such as Gardnerella vaginalis, Prevotella, and Fannyhessea are associated with bacterial vaginosis. Sex hormones, especially 17-beta estradiol and luteinizing hormone, significantly influence bacterial composition by suppressing anaerobic species while promoting Lactobacillus dominance, with L. crispatus showing increased stability and prevalence during the ovulatory phase. Additional factors including age, pregnancy history, hormonal contraceptive use, and menstrual product choice independently shape microbiota composition, with menstrual cup use and contraceptive use favoring L. crispatus dominance. The microbiota exhibits dynamic changes across menstrual cycle phases, with individual host factors rather than menstrual phase alone being the primary determinant of community structure and stability.

title: Preliminary insights into the gut microbiota of patients with rheumatoid arthritis in Vietnam
summary: This study characterizes the gut microbiota dysbiosis in Vietnamese patients with rheumatoid arthritis, revealing distinct bacterial signatures associated with disease activity and severity. Multiple gram-positive bacterial genera including Lactococcus, Solobacterium, Faecalibaculum, and Corynebacterium were significantly elevated in RA patients, while protective bacteria such as Bacteroides and Bacteroidaceae were reduced. Several pathogenic bacteria directly drive inflammation through distinct mechanisms: Eggerthella lenta exacerbates arthritis by stimulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-9, IL-17, IL-23, IFN-γ, TNF-α) while suppressing regulatory T cells, while Actinomyces and Corynebacterium species trigger inflammation via TLR2/TLR4 and NF-κB pathway activation. The microbiota composition significantly correlates with clinical disease markers including DAS28 scores, rheumatoid factor, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies, with Subdoligranulum showing positive association with ACPA levels. Environmental and lifestyle factors including smoking, pollution, and obesity interact with microbiota dysbiosis to promote RA development, highlighting the critical role of gut bacterial composition in disease pathogenesis.

title: Shaoyao Gancao decoction alleviates functional constipation by inhibiting Escherichia-Shigella expansion, modulating gut microbiota, and suppressing dysbiosis-induced endocannabinoid production: evidence from a self-controlled pilot study
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

Shaoyao Gancao decoction (SGD), a traditional Chinese medicine formula, effectively treats functional constipation by rebalancing dysbiotic gut microbiota and suppressing pathological endocannabinoid signaling. The herbal preparation reduces pathogenic Escherichia-Shigella and Proteobacteria while promoting beneficial bacteria including Ruminococcus, Roseburia, and Veillonella, thereby restoring short-chain fatty acid production and improving stool frequency and consistency. SGD's active components—white peony root and licorice—exert immunomodulatory effects by inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokines interleukin-6 and nitric oxide, which drive dysbiosis and metabolic dysfunction. The treatment suppresses arachidonic acid and unsaturated fatty acid metabolism pathways, thereby attenuating retrograde endocannabinoid signaling that normally inhibits intestinal motility through acetylcholine suppression. Responders to SGD therapy showed enrichment of bacteria positively correlated with short-chain fatty acid production, while non-responders retained dysbiotic taxa including Megasphaera and Oscillibacter that produce endocannabinoid precursors. These findings reveal an integrated mechanism whereby SGD restores microbiota-dependent intestinal motility through synchronized reduction of pathogenic bacteria, inflammatory mediators, and lipid signaling molecules implicated in constipation pathogenesis.

title: Surgical site infections and sepsis in gynecological surgery
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

Surgical site infections (SSIs) in gynecological procedures represent a significant clinical challenge, with bacterial overgrowth and microbiota dysbiosis playing central roles in their pathogenesis. SSIs are independent risk factors for sepsis and subsequent organ dysfunction, including acute respiratory distress syndrome, acute kidney insufficiency, and encephalopathy, through mechanisms involving dysregulated immune activation, excessive reactive oxygen species production, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. A healthy vaginal microbiome dominated by Lactobacillus species—which produce protective hydrogen peroxide and lactic acid—normally prevents pathogenic colonization, but bacterial vaginosis and dysbiosis involving organisms like Gardnerella vaginalis and Atopobium vaginae substantially increase infection risk following procedures such as hysterectomy and hysteroscopy. Perioperative risk factors including diabetes, obesity, anemia, and general anesthesia increase SSI susceptibility, while Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols incorporating targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis, glycemic control via continuous insulin infusion, and regional anesthesia significantly reduce infection rates. The innate immune response to pathogenic lipopolysaccharides and damage-associated molecular patterns activates pattern recognition receptors and inflammasomes, triggering excessive cytokine release (IL-1, IL-6, TNF) that paradoxically contributes to tissue damage and organ failure in severe sepsis.

title: Boron Bioavailability Revisited: From Plasma-Accessible Species to Microbiota-Accessible Complexes—Implications for Nutritional Essentiality
summary:
# Summary
