Boron regulates gut homeostasis primarily through microbiota-accessible boron complexes (MABCs) rather than plasma-accessible boron, operating via distinct local and systemic mechanisms. Chemically, boron functions as a Lewis acid binding to cis-diols, catechols, polyols, and glycans to form organoboron conjugates that stabilize microbial quorum sensing, reinforce the colonic mucus barrier through borate–diol crosslinking with mucin glycans, and promote short-chain fatty acid-centered microbial ecology. Deficiency or low intake of MABCs causes dysbiosis, barrier fragility, and low-grade inflammation along gut–organ axes, conditions reversible through MABC-rich diets, while adequate boron intake supports eubiosis, microbial stability, mucosal integrity, and immunometabolic balance. These microbiota-dependent benefits of boron are linked to broader health outcomes in bone, immune, metabolic, and neurobehavioral function, whereas dysbiosis associated with boron insufficiency contributes to inflammatory bowel disease, metabolic syndrome, neuroinflammation, and autoimmunity. This framework reconceptualizes boron as a nutritional factor essential for maintaining host–microbiota symbiosis and systemic health through colonic bioavailability rather than systemic absorption.

title: In the Mouth or in the Gut? Innovation Through Implementing Oral and Gastrointestinal Health Science in Chronic Pain Management
summary:
# Summary

This review establishes multiple interconnected pathways linking oral and gastrointestinal health to chronic pain development. Chronic pain is fundamentally characterized by low-grade systemic inflammation and is strongly associated with both poor oral health and altered intestinal microbiota composition across diverse pain conditions including fibromyalgia, migraine, low back pain, and visceral pain. Dysbiosis—involving depletion of beneficial bacteria like Faecalibacterium, Lachnospiraceae, and Roseburia alongside elevated pro-inflammatory taxa—compromises gut barrier integrity, allowing bacterial lipopolysaccharides to translocate systemically and activate innate immune responses through toll-like receptor 4 signaling, thereby perpetuating pain sensitization. Lifestyle factors including poor sleep, stress, physical inactivity, and obesity amplify this inflammatory cascade by both directly triggering inflammation and promoting dysbiosis, with sleep disruption particularly creating a bidirectional relationship with microbiome composition through alterations in appetite hormones and microbial metabolite production via the gut-brain axis. The review thus proposes that targeting oral health, dietary patterns that restore beneficial microbiota diversity (e.g., fiber-rich diets), and sleep improvement represent innovative therapeutic strategies to mitigate the low-grade systemic inflammation underlying chronic pain conditions.

title: Sex-Specific Diet–Microbiota Interactions in Ageing: Implications for Healthy Longevity
summary:
# Summary

The gut microbiota functions as a central regulator of aging and longevity through metabolic, immune, and neuroendocrine pathways, with diet serving as a major modulator of microbial composition. During aging, the microbiota undergoes characteristic dysbiosis marked by reduced abundance of beneficial organisms like Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, and expansion of proinflammatory taxa including Proteobacteria, changes associated with frailty, cognitive decline, and cardiovascular dysfunction. Sex-specific differences emerge prominently, with women displaying higher microbial diversity and SCFA-producing species throughout adulthood, whereas men show earlier immunosenescence with elevated baseline inflammatory markers; menopause diminishes female advantages through estrogen decline, which reduces IL-10 while increasing IL-6 and TNF-α. Dysbiotic shifts trigger intestinal barrier dysfunction and lipopolysaccharide translocation, activating TLR-4 signaling cascades that amplify NF-κB and NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent pro-inflammatory cytokine production, creating a pathological loop that accelerates aging. Fiber-rich diets promote beneficial Bacteroidetes and short-chain fatty acid production, whereas high-fat Western diets reduce diversity and promote proinflammatory taxa, highlighting diet as a critical intervention point for supporting healthy longevity across the lifespan.

title: Interaction Between CTLA-4, FOXO-3, and PTPN-22 Variants and Environmental Factors in Type 1 Diabetes—Observational Association Study
summary:
# Summary

This observational study examines how genetic variants in immune-regulatory genes CTLA-4, FOXO-3, and PTPN-22 interact with environmental factors to influence autoimmune thyroid disease and metabolic dysfunction in a type 1 diabetes context. Specific alleles—including CTLA-4 rs3087243 G and FOXO-3 rs2802292 T—significantly increase autoimmune thyroid disease risk, while these same variants confer metabolic advantages such as lower HbA1c levels when carriers follow a vegetarian diet. The study reveals a complex gene-environment interaction whereby stress-induced probiotic use paradoxically increased autoimmune thyroid disease risk in certain genotype carriers, whereas vitamin D supplementation showed protective effects. Additionally, the research demonstrates that metabolic pathways involving FOXO-3 variants link to fatty liver disease risk, particularly in males, while short-chain fatty acids like butyrate promote immune tolerance through regulatory T-cell differentiation. Overall, the findings highlight the dual involvement of these immune-regulatory genes in both immune checkpoint function and metabolic homeostasis, suggesting that personalized dietary and supplementation strategies should account for individual genetic backgrounds to optimize disease prevention.

title: Pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic potential of the microbiome in premature ovarian insufficiency
summary: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is a multifactorial condition associated with significant reproductive and systemic consequences, including infertility, depression, anxiety, cognitive decline, and increased risks of osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease. Microbiota dysbiosis plays a central pathogenic role in POI through multiple interconnected mechanisms: dysbiotic microbiota generates reduced short-chain fatty acids while increasing lipopolysaccharides, compromising intestinal barrier integrity and triggering pro-inflammatory immune responses. This dysbiosis-driven inflammation elevates pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β and tumor necrosis factor-α, which directly induce granulosa cell and oocyte apoptosis while suppressing aromatase activity, ultimately leading to follicular atresia and impaired ovarian function. The microbiota-ovary axis regulates the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis through microbial metabolites that modulate endocrine function, immunity, and oxidative stress pathways, with hormonal fluctuations reciprocally shaping reproductive tract microbial composition. Therapeutic interventions utilizing microbial metabolites and microbiota remodeling show promise, as demonstrated by studies showing that young mouse microbiota reverses reproductive aging phenotypes, suggesting that restoring eubiotic microbiota composition may represent an effective therapeutic strategy to preserve ovarian function and prevent POI progression.

title: A pilot study of cervicovaginal microbiome patterns associated with embryo implantation outcomes in endometriosis-associated infertility
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study demonstrates that cervicovaginal microbiota composition significantly associates with embryo implantation success in endometriosis-related infertility. Women with endometriosis show distinct microbial dysbiosis characterized by depleted Lactobacillus and increased pathogenic bacteria (Gardnerella, Streptococcus, Atopobium), which correlates with implantation failure during assisted reproductive technology. Successful frozen embryo transfer pregnancies were enriched for beneficial taxa including Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, alongside lower cervical microbial diversity and higher degree of microbial individuality (DMI), suggesting that stable, person-specific cervical microbial signatures promote implantation. The mechanistic basis involves Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota supporting mucosal homeostasis through lactic acid and indole-3-lactic acid production, which modulates Treg/Th17 immune balance and suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-6—processes disrupted in dysbiosis-associated implantation failure. Differential metabolic pathways involving lipid metabolism and signal transduction in the vagina, and cell growth/transcription pathways in the cervix, further distinguish successful from failed outcomes. These findings establish cervical microbiota profiling as a promising minimally invasive biomarker for predicting implantation success in endometriosis patients undergoing fertility treatment.

title: Rapid Point-of-Care Test Kit for Bacterial Vaginosis: Detection of Vaginolysin and Clue Cells Using Paper Strips and a Smartphone
summary: Bacterial vaginosis, primarily caused by overgrowth of *Gardnerella vaginalis*, represents a significant clinical problem due to its association with sexually transmitted infections, miscarriage, preterm delivery, pelvic inflammatory disease, and postpartum endometriosis. The pathogenic bacterium produces vaginolysin, a pore-forming toxin that binds to the complement regulatory molecule CD59 and causes hemolysis, with elevated vaginolysin levels serving as a marker of unhealthy vaginal microenvironment and correlating with *G. vaginalis* concentration. This article describes a novel point-of-care diagnostic platform combining lateral flow immunoassay detection of vaginolysin with smartphone-enabled microscopy visualization of clue cells, both reliable biomarkers of bacterial vaginosis that improve upon traditional clinical criteria. The assay employs cellulose nanobeads on nitrocellulose membranes with anti-vaginolysin antibodies to achieve rapid toxin detection comparable to ELISA methodology. This integrated diagnostic approach addresses a critical clinical need by enabling rapid, accurate identification of bacterial vaginosis while minimizing the risk of overtreatment, thereby improving patient outcomes and reducing the substantial morbidity associated with this common vaginal dysbiosis.

title: Correlation between intestinal flora and glucuronidase indexes and sex hormone levels and their diagnostic efficacy evaluation for premature ovarian failure
summary: Premature ovarian failure (POF) is characterized by significant dysbiosis of the intestinal microbiota, particularly marked reductions in protective Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species, alongside decreased gut microbial β-glucuronidase (gmGUS) activity. This dysbiosis correlates with the hormonal hallmarks of POF: reduced estradiol and anti-Müllerian hormone paired with elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone levels. The gut-ovary axis appears mechanistically central, as gmGUS activity and beneficial bacterial taxa positively correlate with estrogen levels while inversely correlating with gonadotropin levels, suggesting dysbiosis impairs estrogen metabolism and reactivation. A diagnostic model combining gmGUS activity, Lactobacillus, and Bifidobacterium levels achieved high predictive accuracy (area under the ROC curve of 0.90) for POF identification. These findings establish intestinal dysbiosis as both a biomarker and potential therapeutic target for POF, proposing microbiota assessment as a valuable diagnostic adjunct for this condition affecting 1-5% of women worldwide.

title: Comprehensive Analysis of Vaginal and Gut Microbiome Alterations in Endometriosis Patients
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive microbiome study reveals that endometriosis is associated with distinct dysbiosis patterns in both the vaginal and gut microbiota that may contribute to disease pathogenesis. In the vaginal microbiome, endometriosis patients show reduced levels of the protective bacterium Lactobacillus alongside elevated opportunistic pathogens including Gardnerella, Bifidobacterium, and Klebsiella, changes that correlate with elevated luteinizing and follicle-stimulating hormones. The gut microbiome displays even more pronounced dysbiosis, characterized by reduced diversity and depletion of beneficial taxa such as Ruminococcus, Prevotella, and Oscillibacter, while pathogenic Dialister is enriched and serves as an endometriosis biomarker. Machine learning analyses demonstrate that gut microbiome features substantially outperform both vaginal microbiome composition and traditional hormone measurements for endometriosis diagnosis, suggesting dysbiosis-driven mechanisms involving altered estrogen metabolism and systemic inflammation are central to disease development. These findings establish the microbiota as a critical diagnostic tool and potential therapeutic target in endometriosis management.

title: The critical relationship between vaginal microecology and Ureaplasma urealyticum: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study examines how Ureaplasma urealyticum (UU) disrupts vaginal microecology and causes reproductive tract disease. UU infection is strongly associated with bacterial vaginosis, vaginitis, and pelvic inflammatory disease, and provokes elevated inflammatory markers including IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-6. The pathogen proliferates in dysbiotic vaginal environments characterized by reduced Lactobacillus populations, decreased hydrogen peroxide production, and diminished vaginal cleanliness; multivariate analysis identified low cleanliness (odds ratio 3.00) and hydrogen peroxide positivity (odds ratio 7.24) as independent risk factors for UU infection. Conversely, hydrogen peroxide-producing Lactobacillus vaginalis protects vaginal microecological balance and prevents UU and Mycoplasma co-infections. Beyond local infection, UU causes reproductive tract inflammation, infertility, and preterm premature rupture of membranes, while vaginal dysbiosis more broadly associates with cervical cancer, polycystic ovary syndrome, and postpartum pelvic floor dysfunction. These findings highlight vaginal microecological assessment—measuring pH, hydrogen peroxide, leukocyte esterase, sialidase, β-glucuronidase, and N-acetyl glucosidase—as critical for identifying dysbiosis-related disease risk in women.

title: Gut virome dysbiosis contributes to premature ovarian insufficiency by modulating gut bacteriome
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that gut virome dysbiosis contributes to premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) through modulation of the estrobolome and disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis. Viral-like particles (VLPs) regulate bacterial communities, particularly affecting estrogen-metabolizing bacteria that control HPO hormone levels and subsequently ovarian function. POI patients exhibit characteristic virome dysbiosis marked by reduced virulent phages and increased horizontal gene transfer of resistance and virulence factors, with transferable VLPs from POI patients impairing ovarian function in healthy recipients, while VLPs from healthy women improved POI outcomes. The study identifies specific bacterial compositional changes in POI, including reductions in beneficial species (Helicobacter, Odoribacter, Alistipes) and increases in pro-inflammatory bacteria (Clostridium XIVa, Barnesiella, Bacteroides, Mucispirillum), alongside evidence that sleep deprivation accelerates POI progression through virome-mediated gut barrier dysfunction and systemic inflammation. These findings reveal the gut virome as a critical microbiota-independent regulator of reproductive health and suggest potential therapeutic targets for POI beyond current hormone replacement therapy approaches.

title: Vaginal bacteria-derived extracellular vesicles diffuse through human cervicovaginal mucus to enable microbe-host signaling
summary:
# Summary

Bacteria-derived extracellular vesicles (bEVs) diffuse efficiently through cervicovaginal mucus and mediate microbe-host communication central to female reproductive outcomes. A Lactobacillus-dominated vaginal microbiome is associated with optimal gynecologic and obstetric outcomes, while polymicrobial dysbiosis increases risk for sexually transmitted infections, pelvic inflammatory disease, and preterm birth. The study demonstrates that bEVs penetrate the cervicovaginal mucus barrier more effectively than whole bacteria, with Gardnerella vaginalis-produced sialidases weakening mucus barrier properties to facilitate bEV diffusion. Following internalization by vaginal, endometrial, and placental cells, different bacterial-derived bEVs elicit distinct inflammatory responses—dysbiosis-associated species (G. vaginalis and M. mulieris) promote greater cell growth and upregulate pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, IL-1β, TNFα), while commensal L. crispatus-derived bEVs induce more measured immune activation. These findings establish bEVs as key vehicles for translating vaginal microbiota composition into systemic reproductive consequences through both bacterial virulence factor transport and modulation of host epithelial and immune responses.

title: Protective Role of Dietary Short-Chain Fatty Acid Propionate against Autoimmune Responses and Pathology of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus in MRL-lpr Mice
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the short-chain fatty acid propionate, produced by gut bacteria and significantly reduced in lupus-prone MRL-lpr mice, plays a protective role against systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) pathology. Propionate treatment ameliorated autoimmune responses by reducing serum autoantibodies (antinuclear and anti-dsDNA antibodies), decreasing activated CD4 T cells and follicular helper T cells, and alleviating kidney inflammation and glomerulonephritis. The therapeutic mechanism involves propionate's inhibition of histone deacetylase and NF-κB signaling, which suppresses plasma cell differentiation and B cell development while promoting anti-inflammatory pathways. Propionate supplementation restored dysbiotic microbiota composition by increasing microbial diversity and beneficial bacterial taxa, and transcriptomic analysis revealed widespread changes in renal gene expression favoring mitochondrial function and fatty acid metabolism while suppressing inflammatory regulators. These findings establish that microbial-derived short-chain fatty acids, particularly propionate, represent a critical link between gut dysbiosis and SLE pathogenesis, with potential therapeutic implications for autoimmune disease management through microbiota-targeted interventions.

title: Vaginal microecological characterization of women of childbearing age at different altitudes: a multi-omics exploration
summary: This multi-omics study reveals that altitude significantly reshapes the vaginal microecological landscape in women of childbearing age through hypoxia-induced changes. While Lactobacillus dominance normally characterizes healthy vaginal microbiota, high altitude exposure decreases Lactobacillus abundance while elevating specialized anaerobic bacteria including Porphyromonas, Anaerococcus, and Peptostreptococcus, along with distinct metabolites such as ectoine, thiamine, and taurine. The altitude-induced hypoxic environment impairs cellular metabolism and promotes microbiota dysbiosis, which notably facilitates endometriosis development by increasing cell adhesion capacity and disrupting the hormonal modulation of local immune responses. Estrogen and progesterone normally stabilize vaginal microbiota composition through glycogen accumulation that supports Lactobacillus dominance, but hypoxia undermines this protective mechanism. Disruption of vaginal microenvironment balance consequently increases susceptibility to vaginitis, miscarriage, preterm labor, and sexually transmitted infections. These findings indicate that high-altitude residence creates a microecological vulnerability that may compromise female reproductive tract health through interconnected microbiota and metabolomic alterations.

title: Common cervicovaginal sequencing methods result in discordant molecular diagnoses of bacterial vaginosis and reveal strain level effects of Gardnerella vaginalis
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that different cervicovaginal sequencing methods produce discordant bacterial vaginosis diagnoses by analyzing strain-level effects of key microbiota species. Bacterial vaginosis, characterized by replacement of protective Lactobacillus with polymicrobial communities including Gardnerella vaginalis, Dialister, and Parvimonas species, is associated with HIV transmission, pre-term birth, pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis. While Lactobacillus species maintain vaginal health through lactic acid production and pH regulation, pathogenic species show strain-dependent associations with Amsel's criteria including elevated pH, clue cells, and positive whiff tests, with metagenomic sequencing revealing previously undetectable intra-community strain variations. Community state type IV, dominated by polymicrobial anaerobes, correlates with bacterial vaginosis diagnosis, though specific subtypes (IVA, IVB, IVC) differ in their associations with individual diagnostic criteria. These findings demonstrate that traditional antibiotics like metronidazole and clindamycin effectively treat bacterial vaginosis, but the molecular complexity revealed by metagenomics and metatranscriptomics suggests that strain-level resolution is critical for accurate diagnosis and understanding of microbiota-associated reproductive conditions.

title: Microbial Contributions to Primate Reproduction
summary:
# Summary

Microbial communities play a previously underestimated role in primate reproduction across multiple biological levels, influencing mate choice, fertilization success, and pregnancy outcomes. Odor-producing bacteria including Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and Corynebacterium colonize scent glands and body surfaces, shaping sexual selection both before and after copulation, while mating system structure directly impacts microbial diversity—monogamous species show reduced microbial diversity and lower sexually transmitted infection risk compared to polygamous species. The female reproductive tract microbiota critically regulates fertility through Lactobacillus-dominated communities that enhance sperm quality, motility, and viability by modulating pH and the vaginal environment, whereas opportunistic pathogens like Ureaplasma, Mycoplasma, and Enterococcus reduce semen parameters and increase infertility risk. Vaginal dysbiosis characterized by increased microbial diversity is associated with bacterial vaginosis and adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm delivery and newborn mortality. Long-term sexual partners exhibit convergence in vaginal and seminal microbiota composition, suggesting microbial transmission and co-evolution during reproduction.

title: Could the estrobolome have a role in endometriosis pathogenesis and infertility? A systematic review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent condition strongly associated with gut microbiota dysbiosis and estrobolome alterations, which are also linked to infertility. The estrobolome, a collection of estrogen-metabolizing bacteria, produces enzymes including β-glucuronidase, β-glucosidase, and β-galactosidase that deconjugate estrogen, and dysbiosis-driven alterations in these pathways increase estrogen bioavailability and contribute to the hyperestrogenic environment characteristic of endometriosis. Dysbiosis exacerbates the inflammatory features of endometriosis through multiple mechanisms: it disrupts the gut epithelial barrier, promotes bacterial translocation, reduces production of protective metabolites (short-chain fatty acids, bile acids, and tryptophan derivatives), elevates inflammatory cytokines, and persistently activates immune cells that produce pro-inflammatory mediators including IL-1β, IL-6, and TGFβ. Studies demonstrate that women with endometriosis exhibit specific dysbiotic patterns, including elevated taxa from Desulfobacterota, Eubacteriales, and Eggerthellaceae alongside increased estrogen metabolites and elevated β-glucuronidase levels, while reduced beneficial bacteria such as Paraprevotella clara are diminished. Experimental evidence from mouse models demonstrates that β-glucuronidase injection directly increases endometriotic lesion size and number, validates the mechanistic link between estrobolome dysfunction and disease progression.

title: Integrated metagenomic and metabolomic analyses reveal tenacissoside G as a potential non-antimicrobial treatment for bovine endometritis
summary:
# Summary

Bovine endometritis is characterized by dysbiosis involving pathogenic bacteria including *Fusobacterium necrophorum*, *Trueperella pyogenes*, and multiple other species, which collectively trigger elevated inflammatory markers (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8) and impaired fertility. Metagenomic and metabolomic profiling revealed that *Fusobacterium necrophorum* dominance in clinical endometritis is inversely associated with tenacissoside G (TG), a plant-derived compound enriched in healthy cattle. The compound suppresses *Fusobacterium necrophorum* growth through NF-κB pathway inhibition, simultaneously downregulating pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-6, TNF-α, inducible nitric oxide synthase) and matrix-degrading enzymes while enhancing type II collagen deposition. This mechanism represents a non-antimicrobial therapeutic approach that simultaneously targets pathogenic bacteria and restores tissue integrity, potentially circumventing the microbiome disruption and antimicrobial resistance associated with conventional antibiotic treatment.

title: Glycine attenuates sepsis-induced white matter injury by modulating gut microbiota
summary:
# Summary

Sepsis induces white matter injury in neonates through dysbiosis-driven neuroinflammation, a process that glycine attenuates by restoring gut microbiota composition and suppressing microglial activation. Glycine, produced by beneficial bacteria including Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, and Clostridium, reduces sepsis-induced dysbiosis by promoting beneficial taxa such as Bacteroides and Lactobacillus while suppressing pathogenic Proteobacteria and Escherichia-Shigella. Mechanistically, glycine preserves myelin integrity by maintaining myelin basic protein expression and inhibiting microglial activation, while simultaneously downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-1β, and TGF-β in brain tissue. The compound operates along the gut-brain axis as a metabolic signaling molecule and inhibitory neurotransmitter, modulating host immune homeostasis and reducing systemic inflammation linked to white matter pathology. These findings suggest that glycine supplementation or microbiota restoration represents a therapeutic strategy to prevent sepsis-associated neurodevelopmental complications such as learning deficits in vulnerable preterm infants.

title: Prognostic value of uterine artery Doppler ultrasonography combined with serum PECAM-1 and PLGF levels and gut microbiota colony count for predicting outcomes in hypertensive disorder complicating pregnancy patients
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates combined biomarkers and gut microbiota changes as predictors of adverse outcomes in hypertensive disorder complicating pregnancy (HDCP). Serum levels of PECAM-1 and PLGF, which regulate endothelial function and vascular maturation respectively, were significantly reduced in patients with HDCP and adverse pregnancy outcomes compared to controls. Concurrently, gut microbiota dysbiosis characterized by elevated pathogenic bacteria (Escherichia coli and Enterococcus) and depleted beneficial commensals (Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium) was associated with HDCP development and poor perinatal outcomes. The dysbiosis-driven reduction in protective bacteria impairs intestinal health and metabolic regulation, thereby exacerbating vascular dysfunction and hemodynamic compromise. Integrated assessment of uterine artery Doppler indices, vascular biomarkers, and microbiota composition provides enhanced prognostic capacity for identifying pregnant women at high risk of severe complications including intrauterine growth restriction and fetal compromise.

title: Microbiota-Driven Immune Dysregulation Along the Gut–Lung–Vascular Axis in Asthma and Atherosclerosis
summary:
# Summary

Gut dysbiosis contributes to both asthma and atherosclerosis through disruption of the gut–lung–vascular axis, characterized by depletion of beneficial bacteria such as Faecalibacterium, Roseburia, and Bacteroides that produce short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), and enrichment of pathobionts including Proteobacteria, Haemophilus, and Moraxella. This microbial imbalance promotes epithelial and endothelial barrier dysfunction, metabolic endotoxemia via production of pro-inflammatory metabolites like trimethylamine N-oxide and lipopolysaccharides, and skewing of adaptive immunity toward Th2/Th17-mediated inflammation. SCFAs normally support regulatory T-cell differentiation and anti-inflammatory pathways, but their depletion in dysbiosis permits persistent low-grade inflammation shared by both diseases. Dietary interventions, particularly Mediterranean and plant-based diets rich in fiber, restore microbial diversity and SCFA-producing taxa, while Western-style and high-fat/high-fructose diets exacerbate dysbiosis and barrier dysfunction. Common medications including corticosteroids, antibiotics, and proton-pump inhibitors further modulate host–microbiota interactions, potentially worsening dysbiosis despite therapeutic benefits in controlling acute disease.

title: Dysbiosis-Mediated Regulation of Stem Cells the First Hit for Cancer Generation
summary:
# Summary

Microbial dysbiosis contributes to cancer susceptibility through multiple interconnected mechanisms, with the dysbiotic microenvironment exerting profound effects on stem cell fate and genomic stability. Dysbiotic-derived metabolites and signaling molecules modulate critical stem cell pathways including Hedgehog, Wnt/β-catenin, and Notch, while simultaneously reactivating pluripotency genes such as Nanog, Sox2, and Oct4, promoting the expansion of cancer stem cells. Pathogenic bacteria enriched in dysbiotic states induce persistent inflammatory responses characterized by elevated IL-6, IL-1β, IL-17, and TNF-α, generating reactive oxygen species that cause DNA damage and metabolic shifts toward aerobic glycolysis. Additionally, dysbiotic communities produce oncogenic compounds including N-nitrosamines, Bacteroides fragilis toxin, and colibactin that directly damage DNA, while dysbiosis-mediated alterations in glucuronide-processing pathways dysregulate steroid hormone bioavailability—particularly relevant to endometrial conditions. The dysbiotic microenvironment is linked to multiple cancer types including colorectal, cervical, bladder, prostate, breast, and endometrial cancers, and paradoxically exacerbates chemotherapy resistance by sustaining stemness-associated signaling. Beneficial commensals like Akkermansia muciniphila and Clostridium species produce anti-inflammatory metabolites including short-chain fatty acids and indolepropionic acid that protect against these pathogenic processes.

title: Puberty Starts in the Gut: Intestinal Clues to Early Puberty-Rethinking Biomarkers in Pediatric Endocrinology
summary:
# Summary

Emerging evidence reveals that gut microbiota dysbiosis plays a critical role in central precocious puberty (CPP) by disrupting multiple neuroendocrine pathways. Children with CPP exhibit characteristic alterations in microbial composition, including reduced abundance of short-chain fatty acid (SCFA)-producing commensals like Bacteroides and increased levels of Alistipes, Ruminococcus, and Lachnoclostridium. The dysbiotic microbiota enhances estrogen reabsorption through elevated β-glucuronidase activity and reduces SCFA production, which normally support metabolic homeostasis, anti-inflammatory tone, and proper hypothalamic function. These microbial alterations dysregulate the kisspeptin-GnRH-gonadotropin axis through multiple mechanisms, including systemic low-grade inflammation (mediated by IL-6 and TNF-α), disrupted leptin signaling, and altered energy metabolism sensing. Notably, microbiota-targeted interventions can restore hormonal balance and delay HPG axis activation, positioning the gut microbiota as both a modulator and potential biomarker of early pubertal onset. The clinical consequences of CPP include accelerated bone maturation, compromised adult height, and increased lifetime risks of hypertension, cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and estrogen-dependent cancers.

title: Multi-omics elucidation of Lactiplantibacillus plantarum NKK20 in preventing PCOS via the gut-ovary axis: SCFAs-mediated microbiota-metabolite-immune crosstalk
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study demonstrates that *Lactiplantibacillus plantarum* NKK20 (LP) effectively prevents polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in mice through a gut-ovary axis mechanism centered on short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) production and immune rebalancing. PCOS is characterized by gut dysbiosis with reduced butyrate biosynthesis, which triggers chronic low-grade inflammation and hormonal dysfunction including elevated testosterone, luteinizing hormone, and an abnormal LH/FSH ratio. LP treatment reverses these pathologies by enriching beneficial bacteria (Bacteroidetes, Akkermansia, Muribaculum), augmenting butyrate production, and reinforcing intestinal barrier integrity through upregulation of tight junction proteins ZO-1 and occludin. The mechanistic benefits include suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, MCP-1) while upregulating the anti-inflammatory IL-10, suppression of NF-κB/MAPK signaling cascades, activation of the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant axis, and remodeling of bile acid metabolism via the farnesoid X receptor pathway. These coordinated changes culminate in restored metabolic homeostasis, reduced circulating endotoxin, normalized hormonal profiles, and improved insulin sensitivity and lipid metabolism. The findings establish LP as a promising probiotic intervention for PCOS management via microbiota-metabolite-immune crosstalk.

title: Microbiome analysis of the cystic fluid in ovarian endometrioma: new avenues for the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of the disease
summary: Ovarian endometrioma is a complex manifestation of endometriosis strongly associated with subfertility, chronic pelvic pain, and cyst rupture, with mounting evidence linking the disease to immune dysregulation, inflammatory responses, and endocrine disturbances. This study reveals a unique and highly diverse microbiome within endometrioma cystic fluid, with microbial composition showing greatest similarity to peritoneal fluid and displaying a distinct anatomical gradient of diversity across reproductive tract compartments. Dysbiotic microbial communities contribute to endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple interconnected pathways, including promotion of inflammation and estrogen-mediated hormonal imbalances, while disruption of immune homeostasis by dysbiosis leads to elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines. Specific bacterial taxa show clinically relevant associations: Hydrogenophaga, Phenylobacterium, Caulobacter, Acinetobacter, Testudinibacter, Sphingomonas, and Bifidobacterium correlate with dysmenorrhea severity, while Pseudoalteromonas, Vibrio, Shewanella, and Brevundimonas correlate with CA125 levels. Mechanistically, enriched Escherichia-Shigella in endometrial fluid activate Toll-like receptor 4 signaling, driving chronic inflammatory responses that facilitate ectopic endometrial tissue survival. These findings establish the cystic fluid microbiome as a novel target for diagnosis and therapeutic intervention in endometriosis.

title: Comprehensive 16S rRNA gene sequencing and meta-transcriptomic analyses of the female reproductive tract microbiota: two molecular profiles with different messages
summary:
# Summary

The endometrium, previously thought to be sterile, harbors a complex microbiota distinct from the vagina despite common assumptions about Lactobacillus dominance in this tissue. This study compared DNA-based 16S rRNA gene sequencing with RNA-based meta-transcriptomic analysis to characterize the endometrial microbiome, revealing that the two approaches capture fundamentally different microbial information. While 16S rRNA sequencing identifies total DNA including from non-viable organisms, meta-transcriptomics selectively detects metabolically active bacteria, enabling superior detection of dysbiotic pathogens such as Klebsiella and Listeria exclusively identified by RNA analysis. The endometrial microbiota showed markedly reduced Lactobacillus and increased diversity compared to the vagina, with distinct dysbiotic profiles characterized by correlated abundance of Gardnerella, Fannyhessea, and Ureaplasma. These findings demonstrate that sampling methodology and analytical approach significantly influence microbiota profiling, suggesting that RNA-based approaches provide more clinically relevant characterization of endometrial dysbiosis associated with conditions like chronic endometritis and endometriosis.

title: Resource landscape shapes the composition and stability of the human vaginal microbiota
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the composition and stability of the human vaginal microbiota are fundamentally determined by the availability and distribution of nutritional resources between bacterial communities. The vaginal environment produces two classes of resources: shared glycogen derivatives accessible to all bacteria, and private resources (sialic acid and fucose from mucus) that only certain bacteria like Gardnerella vaginalis can metabolize through enzymatic cleavage. Lactobacillus crispatus-dominated healthy communities (CST I) thrive when shared glycogen is abundant and lactic acid production suppresses pathogens, whereas Gardnerella-dominated dysbiotic communities (CST IV) associated with bacterial vaginosis require high levels of private resources to establish dominance. Mathematical modeling reveals bistability and hysteresis effects: CST IV communities are remarkably resistant to transition back to CST I unless both private resource availability is substantially reduced and external seeding of Lactobacillus bacteria is introduced, explaining why bacterial vaginosis is difficult to treat. These findings suggest that interventions targeting glycogen availability or reducing access to sialic acid metabolites could promote stable lactobacilli-dominant microbiota and prevent dysbiosis-associated adverse gynecological outcomes.

title: Precise probiotic therapy: Advances, bottlenecks, and the road to microbiome-informed nutrition
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how dietary components and precision probiotic therapy can restore microbiota balance and improve health outcomes. Dietary fibers, prebiotics (inulin, fructooligosaccharides, galactooligosaccharides, resistant starch), and polyphenols like epigallocatechin gallate promote beneficial bacteria such as Bifidobacteria and Lactobacilli, which produce short-chain fatty acids that strengthen the intestinal epithelial barrier, reduce pathogenic colonization, and enhance immune responses. Individual responsiveness to probiotics depends critically on host genetic factors (FUT2 secretor status, TLR2 and TLR9 polymorphisms), baseline microbiota composition, metabolic capacity, sex hormones including estrogen, and lifestyle factors such as diet and medication exposure, with specific strains like Bifidobacterium animalis B960 demonstrating capacity to enhance therapeutic drug efficacy. Microencapsulation and optimized dosing improve probiotic viability and short-chain fatty acid production, while longitudinal monitoring enables personalized intervention refinement by revealing dynamic colonization trajectories and probiotic-host compatibility. The article underscores that effective microbiome-informed nutrition requires patient stratification based on predictive biomarkers and integration of dietary modifications with targeted probiotic therapies to prevent metabolic, inflammatory, and immune-mediated disorders.

title: Detection of Novel Thermotolerant Tepidimonas sp. Bacteria in Human Respiratory Specimens, Hong Kong, China, 2024
summary:
# Summary

A novel thermotolerant bacterium, *Tepidimonas hongkongensis*, was identified in respiratory specimens from hospitalized patients in Hong Kong, with evidence of environmental persistence in hospital water systems. The organism was predominantly isolated from patients with chronic lung disease and malignancies, presenting clinically with cough, fever, and shortness of breath, and was associated with serious complications including community-acquired pneumonia and pulmonary metastasis. Genomic analysis revealed that *T. hongkongensis* possesses multiple virulence determinants including genes encoding adhesion factors (pilB, pilC, pilT), secretion systems (type II and VI), alginate biosynthesis machinery, and antimicrobial resistance genes (aadA2, blaOXA-2), alongside stress-response genes enabling survival at elevated temperatures. Additionally, *Tepidimonas* species have been detected across diverse human microbiomes—including endometrial, urinary, gut, and sinus microbiota—and are implicated in conditions ranging from endometriosis to chronic rhinosinusitis, suggesting a broader role in microbiota dysbiosis-associated diseases. These findings establish *T. hongkongensis* as an emerging opportunistic pathogen with both clinical significance in respiratory infections and potential relevance to microbiome-associated inflammatory conditions.

title: Gut microbiota composition and systemic immune-inflammatory marker correlations in infertile women with endometriosis: a pilot case–control study
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This pilot case-control study demonstrates a significant association between gut microbiota dysbiosis and endometriosis in infertile women with chronic pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea. Women with endometriosis exhibited altered microbial composition characterized by enrichment of pro-inflammatory bacterial taxa including Lachnospira, Veillonella, Streptococcus, Parasutterella, and Enterococcus, alongside depletion of protective commensals such as Alistipes and Desulfovibrionaceae. Multiple bacterial taxa correlated with elevated systemic inflammatory markers: Streptococcus and Veillonella positively correlated with macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF), while Bilophila and Enterococcus correlated with elevated TNF-α and IL-6, all of which were significantly elevated in endometriosis patients compared to controls. The dysbiotic microbiota composition may promote chronic systemic inflammation and tissue injury through altered estrogen metabolism and short-chain fatty acid production, thereby contributing to endometriosis pathogenesis and associated infertility. These findings suggest that microbial dysbiosis represents a mechanistic link between gut microbiota imbalance and the systemic immune dysregulation characteristic of endometriosis, potentially opening avenues for microbiota-targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: An Unusual Case of Unexplained Infertility: Co-colonization of the Uterus and Seminal Fluid
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 35-year-old woman with unexplained infertility caused by chronic endometritis resulting from co-colonization of her uterus and partner's seminal fluid with pathogenic bacteria, specifically *Enterococcus faecalis* and *Escherichia coli*. Chronic endometritis, defined as persistent mild inflammation of the endometrium induced by intrauterine bacterial infection and microbial dysbiosis, is strongly associated with infertility, particularly in cases of recurrent IVF failure and unexplained infertility. The inflammation characteristically leads to multiple symptoms including pelvic pain, dysfunctional uterine bleeding, and dyspareunia, with diagnosis confirmed by endometrial biopsy revealing five or more CD138+ plasma cells per high-power field. The patient's infertility was successfully treated through sequential antibiotic courses targeting both partners—the patient received azithromycin and moxifloxacin while her partner received amoxicillin/clavulanic acid—followed by frozen embryo transfer, which resulted in successful pregnancy. This case illustrates the emerging recognition that bacterial transmission from seminal fluid to the endometrium can establish chronic infection and demonstrates the importance of screening for bacteriospermia and endometrial dysbiosis in couples with unexplained infertility.

title: The effects of starter feed on intestinal fungi and non-targeted metabolomics of blood in calf yaks
summary:
# Summary

Starter feed administration to calf yaks significantly restructured intestinal fungal communities and altered systemic metabolite profiles through coordinated microbiota and metabolic pathway changes. The feeding regimen enhanced beneficial fungi including Plectosphaerella, Mortierella, and Aspergillus while simultaneously suppressing pathogenic species such as Comoclathris, Arthrographis, and Cryptococcus neoformans. These microbiota shifts correlated with activation of multiple metabolic pathways including fat and vitamin digestion, glycerophospholipid metabolism, autophagy, and VEGF signaling, accompanied by elevated concentrations of immunomodulatory metabolites (dexamethasone, benzamidine, norethindrone acetate, and tamoxifen) and reduced levels of potentially harmful compounds (dinoterb and monoisobutyl phthalate). The starter feed promoted rumen development through enhanced carbohydrate and protein fermentation, with butyric acid production stimulating rumen growth and specialized bacterial taxa facilitating nutrient decomposition. These findings demonstrate that dietary intervention modulates the intestinal microbiota ecosystem to improve nutrient absorption and energy metabolism while establishing a metabolic environment associated with reduced inflammatory markers.

title: Gut microbiota as a key regulator in endometriosis: mechanisms, therapeutic opportunities, and future perspectives
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbiota dysbiosis represents a critical driver of endometriosis, contributing to disease development and progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The dysbiotic microbiota in endometriosis patients is characterized by an elevated Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio alongside increased abundances of pathogenic taxa including Proteobacteria, Actinobacteria, and specific genera such as Ruminococcus and Streptococcus, which show stage-dependent variations. Dysbiosis compromises intestinal barrier integrity and triggers the lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-TLR4 inflammatory pathway, wherein bacterial LPS enters systemic circulation and activates endometriotic stromal cells to produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-8) and prostaglandins (PGE2), promoting lesion formation and pain while exacerbating immunoinflammatory dysregulation and estrogen bioavailability. Conversely, therapeutic restoration of eubiotic microbiota through dietary interventions, probiotics, prebiotics, and microbial transplantation effectively reduces endometriosis symptoms, as does short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) restoration via commensal producers like Clostridium butyricum and Bifidobacterium, which modulate immunity through G protein-coupled receptor signaling. These findings establish gut microbiota as a mechanistically central and therapeutically targetable regulator of endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
summary:
# Summary: Lipid Metabolism in Homeostasis and Disease

Lipid metabolism is essential for maintaining cellular homeostasis and regulates critical immune functions through multiple pathways, including the activity of macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils. Dysregulation of lipid metabolism is associated with cancer, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and autoimmune conditions including rheumatoid arthritis. Specific lipid metabolites control immune cell proliferation, differentiation, and activation, with fatty acids providing energy storage, cholesterol supporting membrane structure, and phospholipids facilitating cell signaling. Gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs)—acetate, propionate, and butyrate—produced from dietary fiber regulate immune responses through histone deacetylase inhibition and G-protein coupled receptor activation, promoting regulatory T cells and B cell antibody production while suppressing inflammatory macrophage polarization. Additionally, long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids from fish oils, particularly DHA and EPA, inhibit CD4+ T cell function, while Western diets low in fiber and SCFAs promote dysbiosis and impair immune regulation. These findings demonstrate that lipid metabolism integrates nutritional, microbial, and immunological processes to maintain homeostasis and prevent disease.

title: Effect of equine-derived Lactobacillus M11 on the reproductive performance of KM pregnant female mice
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that equine-derived Lactobacillus M11 enhances reproductive performance in pregnant mice through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The probiotic strain improves metabolic support, activates key metabolic pathways including glycine/serine/threonine and arginine/proline metabolism, and enhances antioxidant capacity during pregnancy. At the microbial level, L. M11 modulates gut microbiota composition by enriching beneficial Bacillota phyla while regulating Pseudomonadota-mediated immune responses, with specific bacteria like s_Clostridiaceae_bacterium participating in gut-reproductive axis signaling. Higher doses of L. M11 elevated serum albumin levels and optimized nutrient absorption, while lower doses showed transient metabolic adjustments. The equine-derived Lactobacillus strain appears to interact distinctly with the reproductive system by modulating reproductive tract microbiota composition, enhancing immune responses in reproductive organs, and potentially affecting reproductive hormone synthesis, positioning it as a probiotic intervention for restoring microecological balance and improving reproductive function.

title: Modulation of gut microbiota and short-chain fatty acids by probiotics attenuates inflammation in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Probiotic intervention attenuates endometriosis by reshaping gut microbiota composition and modulating short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) metabolism through a microbiota-metabolism-immunity axis. Probiotic supplementation significantly reduces inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 while enriching beneficial bacteria including Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus, while suppressing pathogenic taxa such as Parabacteroides distasonis and Escherichia coli. These microbiota alterations enhance microbial network stability and modify SCFA production profiles, though notably reducing butyrate and caproic acid while increasing isocaproic acid. SCFAs, particularly butyrate, exert anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine production and promoting regulatory T cell differentiation to maintain immune tolerance. The study demonstrates that modulating gut microbiota composition and function can influence endometriosis occurrence and progression, offering a therapeutic strategy to address the chronic inflammation and dysbiosis-related immune dysregulation underlying this estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease.

title: Impact of gut microbiota on atypical endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer: a comprehensive analysis of microbial composition and metabolomic profiling
summary: This comprehensive analysis reveals that gut microbiota dysbiosis plays a significant role in the progression from normal endometrium to atypical endometrial hyperplasia (AEH) and ultimately to endometrial cancer (EC). Specific pathogenic bacteria including Klebsiella, Megamonas, Escherichia, and Akkermansia were identified as biomarkers associated with these conditions, with Klebsiella showing the most dramatic increase from 0.264% in controls to 6.092% in EC cases. The study demonstrates that dysbiotic microbiota promote tumorigenesis through multiple mechanisms: the bacterial enzyme β-glucuronidase regulates the estrogen-gut microbiome axis, Succinivibrio directly promotes EC development via altered fatty acid metabolism, and dysbiosis triggers enrichment of glycerophospholipid metabolism pathways. Additionally, gut microbiota regulate immune responses and inflammatory responses through short-chain fatty acids and other metabolites, while microbial-derived compounds like urolithin A influence cellular cytoskeletal dynamics. These findings establish the gut microbiota as a critical regulator of lipid metabolism and immune homeostasis, with dysbiosis creating a permissive microenvironment for endometrial malignant transformation.

title: Dual Role of the Endometrial Microbiome-Immune Axis: From Endometrial Homeostasis to Reproductive Disorders
summary:
# Summary

The endometrial microbiota maintains reproductive health through a bidirectional microbiome-immune axis, wherein Lactobacillus species dominate the healthy microbiota and promote immune homeostasis, regulate the endometrial barrier through tight junction proteins, and suppress inflammatory responses by activating dendritic cells and inducing regulatory T cells. Dysbiosis—an imbalance of microbial communities—disrupts this equilibrium by abnormally activating local immune responses and eliminating immune tolerance mechanisms, ultimately leading to chronic endometritis and infertility. Beyond Lactobacillus, other commensals such as Bacteroides fragilis, Bifidobacterium, and Akkermansia muciniphila contribute to immune regulation through production of anti-inflammatory cytokines and short-chain fatty acids that promote barrier function and appropriate immune cell polarization. Conversely, pathogenic overgrowth of Escherichia coli and anaerobes characterizes dysbiotic states and correlates with inflammatory disease development. This research underscores how the microbiota-immune interaction controls endometrial homeostasis and reveals dysbiosis as a critical mechanistic driver of reproductive disorders including endometriosis-associated conditions.

title: Association Between Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota and the Risk of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: Mediating Role of Anti‐ and Pro‐Inflammatory Dietary Patterns
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dietary patterns significantly influence pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) risk through modulation of gut microbiota composition and inflammatory status. Higher Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota (DI-GM) scores, reflecting diets rich in fiber and antioxidants, were associated with decreased PID risk, while higher Dietary Inflammatory Index scores correlated with increased risk. The mechanism involves dietary components promoting beneficial bacteria such as Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus, which produce short-chain fatty acids that enhance intestinal barrier integrity and prevent pathogenic translocation to the pelvis. Inflammatory dietary patterns, including processed meats and sugar-sweetened beverages, mediated approximately 27% of the association between gut microbiota quality and PID development by promoting dysbiosis and systemic inflammation. These findings suggest that dietary modifications optimizing gut microbial communities represent a potentially effective nutritional strategy to reduce PID incidence and its complications including infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain.

title: The influence of washed microbiota transplantation on menstruation in female patients of childbearing age
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that washed microbiota transplantation (WMT) effectively restores gut microbial diversity and improves menstrual health in women of childbearing age by rebalancing dysbiotic communities. The gut microbiota regulates menstruation through the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and estrogen absorption, with dysbiosis contributing to menstrual disorders, premenstrual syndrome, and endometriosis-related dysmenorrhea via disrupted gut-brain-endocrine signaling. WMT treatment increased beneficial bacteria (Dialister, Bifidobacterium, Faecalibacterium, Roseburia) while reducing potentially pathogenic taxa (Bacteroides, Escherichia-Shigella, Prevotella), correlating with improved menstrual symptoms and reduced menstrual distress scores. Beyond menstrual improvements, WMT significantly enhanced overall quality of life across multiple domains—including general health, social functioning, vitality, and mental health—while reducing depression and anxiety symptoms. These findings establish microbiota restoration as a therapeutic approach for menstrual dysfunction and highlight the critical role of gut-brain-endocrine axis balance in female reproductive health.

title: Impact of hormonal treatments for endometriosis on the reproductive microbiome: a systematic review
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth and is associated with reproductive microbiota dysbiosis marked by decreased beneficial Lactobacilli and increased pathogenic bacteria such as Gardnerella and Streptococcus species. The reproductive microbiome normally provides critical defense against infections and immune modulation, while dysbiotic alterations contribute to inflammation, immunological dysfunction, and infertility in women with endometriosis. Hormonal treatments significantly impact the reproductive microbiome through distinct mechanisms: combined oral contraceptives enhance healthy Lactobacilli and reduce bacterial vaginosis-associated taxa by upregulating estrogen-dependent antimicrobial peptides, while GnRH agonists create a hypo-estrogenic environment that decreases antimicrobial peptide secretion and paradoxically fosters pathogenic bacteria dominance including Gardnerella and Streptococcaceae. Although GnRH agonists and progestins are clinically more effective at reducing endometriosis progression than combined oral contraceptives, their adverse effects on microbiota composition may compromise reproductive outcomes and favor bacterial species associated with infertility. This systematic review highlights a critical trade-off in endometriosis treatment: while hypo-estrogenic therapies successfully suppress disease implants, they simultaneously promote unfavorable microbiota alterations that could impair fertility and increase infection risk.

title: Interplay of Microbiome, Oxidative Stress and Inflammation in Health and Disease
summary:
# Summary

The human microbiome plays a fundamental dual role in both maintaining health and promoting disease through its influence on oxidative stress, inflammation, and immune regulation. Microbial metabolites such as butyrate, hydrogen sulfide, and indole derivatives, along with specific probiotic strains, contribute to redox homeostasis and modulate key signaling pathways including Nrf2/Keap1, NF-κB, and MAPK that control oxidative stress responses and inflammatory processes. Dysbiosis—disruption of normal microbiota composition and function—is associated with impaired host health and creates a pathogenic feedback loop where oxidative stress and inflammation further alter microbiome diversity, leading to increased production of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) that trigger DNA damage, protein damage, and sustained inflammatory responses. The excessive ROS/RNS production activates pattern recognition receptors and signaling cascades that amplify inflammation, while simultaneously impairing antioxidant enzyme systems (SOD, GPx, CAT) and generating harmful byproducts like malondialdehyde. These interconnected mechanisms linking microbiota dysregulation, oxidative stress, and inflammation have been implicated in multiple chronic diseases including neurodegenerative diseases, cancer, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes mellitus. Restoring microbiota balance through probiotics and metabolite supplementation represents a therapeutic strategy to re-establish redox homeostasis and interrupt the pathogenic cycle of oxidative stress-driven inflammation.

title: Microbiome, Epigenetics, and Nutritional Factors in Shaping Perinatal Pregnancy Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Maternal nutrition and gut microbiome composition are central regulators of fetal development and perinatal outcomes, with maternal dietary patterns and microbiome-derived metabolites influencing placental function and fetal growth. Dysbiosis of the vaginal or gastrointestinal microbiome triggers pro-inflammatory expression of cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β) and enzymes (COX-2, iNOS), whereas healthy microbiota dominated by Lactobacillus species modulates NF-κB and MAPK signaling to maintain immune homeostasis. Short-chain fatty acids produced by commensal bacteria—particularly butyrate—inhibit histone deacetylases and enhance histone acetylation, while activating metabolic pathways (AMPK, PGC-1α) that promote fatty acid oxidation and antioxidant enzyme expression through mechanisms involving epigenetic regulation via DNA methylation and microRNA expression. Multiple signaling pathways including NF-κB, MAPK, Nrf2, and PPAR-α/γ coordinately regulate immunometabolic activity under control of epigenetic mechanisms, with omega-3 fatty acids and microbial metabolites suppressing pro-inflammatory cascades. Oral dysbiosis and periodontal infections disrupt this balance by translocating lipopolysaccharides that engage TLR2/TLR4 signaling, triggering systemic inflammation and disrupting placental lipid metabolism, highlighting how microbiota composition at multiple body sites shapes the intrauterine immunometabolic environment critical for healthy pregnancy outcomes.

title: Gut Microbiota Alterations and Reproductive Tract Dysbiosis in Endometriosis: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Endometriosis and Gut Microbiota Dysbiosis: Key Findings

Endometriosis is consistently associated with reproducible gut microbiota dysbiosis characterized by elevated Proteobacteria, Firmicutes, and pathogenic taxa (particularly Escherichia coli, Streptococcus, and Klebsiella), alongside depletion of beneficial organisms including Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, and Faecalibacterium. The dysbiotic microbiota functionally drives disease pathology through multiple mechanisms: elevated β-glucuronidase activity enhances estrogen deconjugation and enterohepatic recirculation, while the enrichment of gram-negative bacteria promotes lipopolysaccharide-mediated immune activation and pro-inflammatory pathways. Loss of protective commensals reduces short-chain fatty acid production and metabolic capacity, whereas dysbiosis compromises intestinal epithelial barrier integrity and increases permeability, allowing translocation of microbial antigens that perpetuate systemic inflammation and immune dysregulation. Microbial dysbiosis correlates with endometriosis severity, disease stage, and clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, with advanced disease showing distinct microbial signatures including elevated Lactobacillus jensenii, Anaerococcus, and Fusobacterium, and bacterial colonization detected in peritoneal fluid. These findings support the hypothesis that microbiota-derived estrogen metabolism alterations and dysbiosis-induced inflammation represent key mechanisms linking gut dysbiosis to endometriosis development and progression.

title: A Dive into the Invisible: The Vaginal and Endometrial Microbiota in Gynecologic and Obstetric Disorders: A Narrative Review
summary: This narrative review synthesizes evidence on how microbiota dysbiosis contributes to endometriosis and related gynecologic disorders. Women with endometriosis display characteristic microbiota alterations including reduced protective Lactobacillus species and elevated pathogenic bacteria (Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae) in the vagina, alongside shifts toward pro-inflammatory communities (Fusobacterium, Streptococcus, Enterococcus) in the endometrium. Dysbiosis initiates a pathogenic cascade wherein impaired intestinal barrier function permits lipopolysaccharide translocation that activates TLR4 signaling, NF-κB, and COX-2 pathways to promote angiogenesis, fibrosis, and lesion survival. The gut estrobolome, enriched in β-glucuronidase-producing bacteria, enhances estrogen deconjugation and circulating estrogen levels, driving endometrial proliferation. Vitamin D/VDR signaling suppresses this inflammatory cascade by inhibiting NF-κB activation and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) while promoting beneficial taxa; conversely, vitamin D deficiency worsens dysbiosis and inflammation. Similar microbiota dysbiosis mechanisms underlie adenomyosis and uterine fibroids, establishing microbiota rebalancing as a potential therapeutic target across multiple reproductive disorders.

title: Dietary Fiber in Endometriosis: Mechanisms, Evidence, and Potential Clinical Benefits—Up-to-Date Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition characterized by dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility risk, driven by hormonal dysregulation and pelvic pain. Dietary fiber modulates endometriosis risk and symptom severity through multiple interconnected mechanisms: it reduces systemic estrogen concentrations by inhibiting enterohepatic circulation and increasing fecal estrogen excretion, thereby decreasing estradiol and estrone levels. Fiber fermentation by the gut microbiota generates short-chain fatty acids, particularly butyrate, which strengthens the intestinal epithelial barrier, inhibits class I histone deacetylases, and promotes regulatory T cell programs that dampen inflammation through NF-κB and NLRP3 repression. Additionally, specific bacterial taxa including butyrogenic Firmicutes (Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Roseburia) and mucin-modulating species (Akkermansia muciniphila) enhance barrier function and regulate estrogen metabolism. Observational studies demonstrate that higher dietary fiber intake, particularly from fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and legumes, is associated with significantly reduced endometriosis risk and may contribute to pain reduction and symptom improvement, supporting dietary fiber as a potential adjunctive therapeutic strategy for endometriosis management.

title: β-Glucuronidase at the Microbiota—Host Interface: Dual Regulatory Roles and Precision Modulation by Natural Products
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbial β-glucuronidase (GUS) plays a critical role at the microbiota-host interface by hydrolyzing glucuronide conjugates, thereby regulating xenobiotic metabolism and enterohepatic circulation of endogenous substances including estrogens. Dysregulated GUS activity is associated with adverse health outcomes including drug-induced toxicity, inflammation, and cancer progression; aberrant GUS activity in particular enhances estrogen reabsorption and reactivation in endometrial and breast cancers, driving tumor progression while also reactivating detoxified chemotherapeutic drugs and causing gastrointestinal toxicity. GUS enzymes are produced by diverse gut microorganisms, predominantly from the phyla Bacteroidota and Bacillota, with approximately 25–26% of gut bacterial genomes encoding GUS variants that exhibit distinct subcellular localizations and substrate specificities. Natural products have emerged as precision modulators of GUS activity, capable of directly inhibiting the enzyme and remodeling GUS-producing microbial populations, offering therapeutic potential for controlling dysbiosis-associated pathologies. These findings highlight the dual regulatory roles of GUS at the microbiota-host interface and underscore the therapeutic promise of targeting GUS activity through natural product-based interventions to modulate microbiota dysbiosis and associated disease states.

title: The Gut-Prostate Axis: Decoding the Interplay of Environmental Factors, Microbial Metabolites, and Hormonal Regulation in Prostate Cancer Pathogenesis
summary:
# Summary

The gut microbiota regulates prostate cancer pathogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving microbial metabolites and hormonal signaling. Dysbiosis—characterized by altered abundance of taxa including Bacteroides, Alistipes, and Lachnospira—promotes cancer progression via short-chain fatty acids that upregulate insulin-like growth factor 1, subsequently activating MAPK and PI3K pathways that crosstalk with androgen receptor signaling. Additionally, bacterial genotoxins such as cytotoxic necrotizing factor 1, cytolethal distending toxin, and colibactin induce DNA damage and genomic instability, with colibactin-induced double-strand breaks specifically associated with oncogenic TMPRSS2:ERG gene fusion. Dietary interventions, particularly omega-3 fatty acid supplementation and low-fat diets, mitigate cancer risk by reshaping microbial composition and reducing pro-tumorigenic metabolite production, while high-fat diets promote cancer growth through mast cell-dependent histamine signaling and enhanced SCFA synthesis. These findings establish the gut-prostate axis as a critical regulatory network where microbiota-derived metabolites and microbial enzymes like β-glucuronidase coordinate hormonal metabolism and mutagenic processes to influence disease outcomes.

title: Vaginal and endometrial microbiota dysbiosis in patients with chronic endometritis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary: Chronic endometritis is characterized by dysbiosis of both vaginal and endometrial microbiota, marked by reduced Lactobacillus abundance and increased opportunistic pathogens including Gardnerella, Ureaplasma, Enterococcus, and Sphingomonas. This dysbiotic shift alters the intrauterine immune microenvironment and is strongly associated with adverse reproductive outcomes including recurrent implantation failure, recurrent pregnancy loss, and infertility, as well as clinical symptoms such as pelvic pain and abnormal uterine bleeding. Meta-analytic findings revealed significantly elevated detection rates of Ureaplasma (odds ratio 6.30) and Enterococcus (odds ratio 4.93) in chronic endometritis patients compared to controls, alongside increased microbial diversity as measured by Chao1 indices. The endometrial microbiota dysbiosis observed in chronic endometritis shows compositional similarities with dysbiosis patterns documented in endometriosis, adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, and endometrial cancer, suggesting shared microbial-immune mechanisms across reproductive pathologies. These findings establish microbiota dysbiosis as a central pathobiologic feature of chronic endometritis that warrants investigation as a therapeutic target beyond current broad-spectrum antibiotic approaches.

title: Underexplored maternal microbiomes: immune, metabolic, and microbial pathways shaping pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

The maternal microbiome functions as a coordinated, multi-site network that critically shapes pregnancy outcomes through immune regulation, metabolic processes, and fetal development. Maternal microbial communities, particularly Lactobacillus species in the cervicovaginal region and SCFA-producing bacteria in the gut, regulate immune tolerance and nutrient metabolism while producing bioactive compounds that support placental development and fetal programming. Sex steroid hormones including estradiol and progesterone modulate the composition of specific bacterial taxa, with progesterone increasing SCFA-producing species like Bifidobacterium and both hormones inhibiting pathogenic organisms such as *Escherichia coli* and *Streptococcus agalactiae*. Microbiota dysbiosis—characterized by depletion of protective *Lactobacillus* species and overgrowth of pathogenic bacteria—is associated with multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm birth, miscarriage, preeclampsia, and gestational diabetes mellitus. Specific commensals such as *Akkermansia muciniphila* and *Bifidobacterium* species produce extracellular vesicles and metabolites that cross the placental barrier to promote fetal immune development and prevent preeclampsia-like phenotypes, while paternal microbiota disruptions can impair germline quality and fetal outcomes through reversible intergenerational mechanisms.

title: Burning Mouth Syndrome and the Oral Microbiome: Unveiling Potential Links
summary:
# Summary

Oral microbiota dysbiosis is implicated in burning mouth syndrome (BMS) through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving immune dysregulation, hormonal imbalance, and neuropathic pain pathways. Dysbiotic oral communities show reduced protective species like *Streptococcus mitis*, *S. salivarius*, and *Lactobacillaceae*, which normally suppress pathogens and regulate thyroid hormone secretion, leaving the oral cavity vulnerable to pathogenic bacteria that elevate pro-inflammatory interleukin-6 and bacterial lipopolysaccharides that activate pain-sensing TRPV1 channels. BMS predominantly affects premenopausal women experiencing estrogen and progesterone fluctuations, and dysbiosis exacerbates this hormonal dysregulation by altering cortisol, catecholamine, and serotonin/dopamine-producing pathways that control stress responses and bacterial virulence. Additionally, dysbiotic microbiota fail to synthesize sufficient vitamin B12 and cannot effectively downregulate inflammatory cytokines through protective species like *Lactobacillus plantarum*, collectively driving peripheral and central neuropathic pain mechanisms characteristic of BMS.

title: The characteristics of gut microbiota composition in patients with depression and relationship with clinical symptoms
summary: This study demonstrates that gut microbiota dysbiosis is significantly associated with major depressive disorder through the gut-brain axis, which facilitates bidirectional communication affecting hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function and host metabolism. Patients with depression exhibit reduced microbial diversity and characteristic compositional changes, including elevated Bacteroides species and decreased Parabacteroides compared to healthy controls, while Firmicutes phyla and Agathobacter are enriched in non-depressed individuals. Several bacterial taxa including Bacteroides, Parabacteroides, and Lachnospiraceae show promise as diagnostic biomarkers for identifying depressive disorder. The study further reveals that antidepressant treatment associates with distinct shifts in specific genera including Oceanobacillus, Turicibacter, Kroppenstedtia, and unclassified Peptostreptococcaceae, suggesting that therapeutic response correlates with microbiota remodeling. These findings support the microbiota-gut-brain axis hypothesis and establish specific bacterial community signatures as potential biomarkers for depression diagnosis and treatment monitoring.

title: Contribution of endometrial microbiome to inflammation-mediated infertility in women undergoing ART
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometrial microbiome composition significantly impacts assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes through inflammation-mediated mechanisms. Women who fail to achieve pregnancy after ART exhibit increased microbial diversity with reduced Lactobacillus abundance and elevated pathogenic bacteria including Prevotella and Corynebacterium species, whereas successful pregnancies are associated with Lactobacillus-dominated, low-diversity microbiota. ART procedures themselves, particularly ovarian stimulation and progesterone supplementation, reduce protective Lactobacilli while promoting dysbiotic species, creating a microenvironment that impairs endometrial receptivity. Although butyrate—a short-chain fatty acid produced by dysbiotic bacteria—can enhance epithelial receptivity markers and decidualization at physiological levels, excessive butyrate compromises epithelial barrier integrity and triggers pro-inflammatory cytokine release alongside antimicrobial peptide induction. The dysbiotic endometrial microbiota activates dysregulated IL-17 inflammatory pathways linked to subfertility, whereas commensal Lactobacillus-dominated communities maintain lactic acid production that supports eubiosis and immune homeostasis required for successful embryo implantation and early pregnancy establishment.

title: The Women’s Microbiome: Molecular Insights, Clinical Gaps, and Future Frontiers in Precision Health with Implications for Gulf Cooperation Council Populations
summary:
# Summary

Female sex hormones, particularly estrogen and progesterone, act as ecological regulators of microbial ecosystems, establishing bidirectional communication through the estrobolome—the collection of microbial genes involved in estrogen metabolism. Key bacterial taxa such as Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium produce lactic acid and metabolites including short-chain fatty acids and indole derivatives that modulate immune function, epithelial integrity, and neuroendocrine pathways through enzymatic activity including microbial β-glucuronidase-mediated estrogen deconjugation. Dysbiosis characterized by reduced Lactobacillus dominance and altered β-glucuronidase-producing taxa is associated with multiple reproductive and metabolic disorders including polycystic ovary syndrome, gestational diabetes, and preterm birth, while dysregulation of estrogen metabolism has been implicated in estrogen-dependent malignancies. Microbial metabolites regulate critical host processes including neurotransmitter availability, hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis activity, and immune tolerance, with particular relevance during hormonally dynamic periods such as pregnancy and postpartum, where maternal microbiota composition influences both maternal insulin sensitivity and fetal immune development. These findings underscore the microbiota as a key modulator of reproductive health and suggest that targeted dietary interventions and microbial-based therapies represent promising precision health strategies for women's health.

title: Probiotic Modulation of the Gut–Ovary and Gut–Myometrium Axes: An In Vitro Study
summary: This in vitro study demonstrates that a combined probiotic blend of Lactobacillus crispatus, Limosilactobacillus fermentum, and Bifidobacterium bifidum exerts synergistic effects on reproductive and metabolic health through multiple interconnected pathways. The probiotics enhanced intestinal cell viability and barrier function while increasing butyrate production by 23–51%, which subsequently reduced oxidative stress and inflammatory markers (ROS by 49%, TNF-α by 74%) and improved myometrial proliferation by up to 78%.
