In ovarian cells, the probiotic mixture activated key signaling pathways (ERK/MAPK and PI3K/AKT), normalized expression of reproductive genes (PAK1, ERβ, PAX8), and significantly increased luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone secretion compared to single strains. These findings support a gut–ovary and gut–myometrium axis mechanism whereby dysbiosis-induced inflammation, oxidative stress, and endocrine imbalances compromise reproductive function, while targeted probiotic intervention restores intestinal barrier integrity, enhances short-chain fatty acid production, and modulates hormone metabolism and signaling. The results suggest potential therapeutic applications for microbiota-based interventions in reproductive disorders such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis-associated subfertility.

title: Evidence-Based Nutraceuticals in Human Health and Longevity Medicine
summary: This review examines evidence-based nutraceutical approaches in longevity medicine, with emphasis on dietary components that modulate aging pathways and microbiota composition. Polyphenol-rich foods, including leafy greens, legumes, nuts, seeds, berries, and olive oil, enhance cardiometabolic and cognitive health while regulating inflammatory and oxidative processes through modulation of gut microbiota. Fiber and plant bioactives promote microbial diversity and metabolic homeostasis, while adequate vitamin and mineral intake supports genomic stability, mitochondrial function, antioxidant defenses, and epigenetic regulation. Dietary quality influences key nutrient-sensing pathways (AMPK, sirtuins, mTOR) that foster autophagy and cellular resilience, with dysbiosis-associated pathobiont metabolites conversely linked to insulin resistance and atherosclerosis. Mediterranean, Nordic, and Asian dietary patterns—characterized by minimally processed plant foods and healthy fats—are associated with reduced chronic disease risk, while specific nutraceuticals like epigallocatechin gallate show promise in targeting endometriosis-related inflammatory and proliferative pathways. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that plant-based nutrition integrated with prebiotics, probiotics, and postbiotics promotes both metabolic health and healthy aging through coordinated effects on microbiota, immune function, and nutrient-sensing pathways implicated in longevity.

title: The role of probiotics in the treatment of endometriosis (ProMetrioS): a randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled cross-over trial
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic condition characterized by dysbiosis and associated with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility in approximately 30% of patients, involves dysregulation of the estrobolome—the bacterial community responsible for estrogen metabolism—and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines that perpetuate chronic inflammation. Patients with endometriosis show reduced microbial diversity with increased abundance of Blautia, Dorea, and Streptococcus and decreased levels of beneficial bacteria such as Paraprevotella, Lachnospira, and Turicibacter. The ProMetrioS trial investigates whether the multispecies probiotic formulation OMNi-BiOTiC® Stress can ameliorate endometriosis symptoms by restoring microbial balance, upregulating anti-inflammatory genera like Ruminococcus and Coprococcus, and enhancing anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antidepressive effects. Specific probiotic strains including Lactobacillus gasseri OLL2809 promote IL-12 secretion, which suppresses endometriotic lesion development, while Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species modulate the gut-brain axis to address the depression affecting over 50% of patients with painful endometriosis. This randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled cross-over study aims to provide the first evidence for multispecies probiotic therapy in treating endometriosis and improving quality of life through microbiota-directed intervention.

title: The Role of Gut Microbiota in Postmenopausal Women: Implications for Lipid Metabolism and Targeted Nutritional Interventions
summary:
# Summary

Menopause triggers a complex cascade of metabolic dysregulation in women through the interplay of estrogen deficiency and gut microbiota dysbiosis. The decline in estrogen during menopause impairs multiple lipid regulatory mechanisms—including reduced hepatic LDL receptor expression, decreased HDL synthesis, and increased lipoprotein lipase activity—while simultaneously promoting central obesity and dyslipidemia. Concurrent with these hormonal changes, menopause-associated dysbiosis shifts the microbial community toward a higher Firmicutes-to-Bacteroidetes ratio with reduced diversity, diminishing the production of beneficial short-chain fatty acids (particularly propionate and butyrate) that normally suppress hepatic cholesterol synthesis and maintain intestinal barrier function. The dysbiotic state increases circulating lipopolysaccharides, which trigger metabolic endotoxemia and chronic low-grade inflammation, further exacerbating adverse lipid profiles and metabolic dysfunction. Targeted nutritional interventions—including fiber-rich, Mediterranean, plant-based, and DASH diets supplemented with prebiotics, probiotics, and polyphenols—can restore microbial diversity and SCFA production, thereby improving lipid profiles and reducing systemic inflammation in postmenopausal women.

title: Integrative multi-omics analysis reveals probiotic-induced microbiota shifts in women with gestational diabetes
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This integrative multi-omics study demonstrates that probiotic supplementation effectively treats gestational diabetes mellitus by reshaping the gut microbiota and ameliorating multiple pathogenic mechanisms. Probiotics significantly increased beneficial bacteria including Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium while reducing pathogenic Escherichia/Shigella, leading to a 3.1-fold increase in butyrate and 2.5-fold increase in acetate production. These short-chain fatty acids improved insulin sensitivity and enhanced gut barrier function by upregulating tight junction proteins (ZO-1 and CLDN1), while simultaneously reducing systemic inflammation through decreased TNF-α and IL-6 and increased anti-inflammatory cytokines IL-10 and TGF-β. Probiotic treatment also directly enhanced insulin receptor and AKT signaling while reducing oxidative stress through antioxidant defense mechanisms, addressing the multiple contributors to gestational diabetes including insulin resistance, chronic low-grade inflammation, and oxidative stress. These findings indicate that probiotics represent a non-invasive therapeutic intervention for gestational diabetes through comprehensive microbiota-mediated metabolic and immunological restoration.

title: Comparative analysis of renal dysfunction, immune remodeling, and gut microbiota profiles in dietary models of metabolic liver disease
summary:
# Summary

Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) demonstrates a bidirectional relationship with chronic kidney disease and renal dysfunction through a mechanism termed "metabolic inflammation" or metaflammation. Hepatic steatosis induced by dietary excess triggers lipotoxic stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and oxidative damage, leading hepatocytes to release pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) and chemokines (CCL2, CCL5) that compromise renal endothelial integrity and activate renal immune cells. Dietary models, particularly the choline-deficient high-fat diet (CDAHFD), cause more severe hepatic and renal pathology than high-fat diet (HFD) alone by disrupting one-carbon metabolism and elevating homocysteine, while both diets induce gut dysbiosis characterized by reduced short-chain fatty acid-producing bacteria and enrichment of pathogenic taxa that promote endotoxin release and activate TLR4-NFκB signaling in liver and kidney. The loss of butyrate and propionate-producing bacteria eliminates critical regulators of intestinal barrier function and anti-inflammatory Treg differentiation, allowing lipopolysaccharide translocation that perpetuates systemic inflammation and renal structural injury.

title: Endometrial Microbiome and Implantation: From Basic Knowledge to Clinical Medicine
summary:
# Summary

The endometrial microbiome plays a critical role in implantation success, with Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota serving as a hallmark of eubiosis associated with enhanced endometrial receptivity and improved pregnancy outcomes. Non-Lactobacillus-dominant patterns, particularly those characterized by anaerobic overgrowth such as Gardnerella vaginalis, are linked to implantation failure and recurrent implantation failure, making the endometrial microbiome a promising therapeutic target for reproductive dysfunction. Lactobacillus species, especially L. crispatus, maintain a protective microenvironment through multiple mechanisms including lactic acid and bacteriocin production, immunomodulation, and pathogen exclusion, while dysbiosis in both the vaginal and gut compartments is associated with adverse outcomes including early pregnancy loss, endometriosis, and polycystic ovary syndrome. Emerging therapeutic strategies beyond standard antibiotics include probiotics, prebiotics such as lactoferrin, postbiotics with anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties, and bacteriophage therapy to selectively eliminate pathogenic bacteria while preserving beneficial microbiota. Dietary interventions featuring high-fiber, plant-based patterns promote short-chain fatty acid-producing microbiota that strengthen epithelial barrier function and attenuate systemic inflammation, offering a preventive approach to maintaining eubiosis across the gut-reproductive axis.

title: Bacterial microbiome analysis of vaginal, cervical, and endometrial samples in patients with adenomyosis during the window of implantation
summary:
# Summary

This microbiome analysis revealed significant dysbiosis in adenomyosis patients characterized by increased Lactobacillus iners colonization and decreased protective lactobacilli species (L. gasseri, L. jensenii) and Gardnerella vaginalis. Elevated L. iners abundance was strongly associated with adenomyosis symptoms including dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and chronic pelvic pain, while beneficial lactobacilli species showed protective associations with reduced symptom severity. Beyond L. iners, pathogenic anaerobic bacteria including Prevotella, Dialister, and Peptoniphilus species were linked to dysmenorrhea and abnormal bleeding. The aberrant microbiota composition in adenomyosis likely contributes to disease pathology through multiple mechanisms: altered bacteria promote proinflammatory mediator release, compromise immune surveillance, induce DNA damage and mutations, and facilitate pathological uterine contractions that may enhance endometrial invasion into the myometrium. These findings suggest that vaginal and endometrial dysbiosis represents a critical component of adenomyosis pathogenesis and may underlie poor treatment responses and adverse reproductive outcomes observed in affected patients.

title: Deciphering the mitochondria–gut microbiota axis in irritable bowel syndrome: pathogenic insights and therapeutic frontiers
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbiota dysbiosis and mitochondrial dysfunction form a bidirectional axis that drives irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) pathogenesis. Dysbiotic microbiota impair mitochondrial function through metabolite signaling, reducing ATP production, increasing reactive oxygen species (ROS), and triggering intracellular calcium imbalance, while mitochondrial dysfunction simultaneously promotes dysbiosis by compromising immune regulation. This axis activates pathogenic immune responses through T cells and neutrophils, and dysbiosis-induced alterations in bacterial metabolites and neurotransmitters disrupt serotonin signaling, enteric glial cell function, and intestinal motility. Therapeutic interventions targeting this axis—including probiotics, traditional Chinese medicine, and dietary strategies like ketogenic diets—restore both microbiota composition and mitochondrial biogenesis, reducing inflammation and alleviating IBS symptoms. The clinical relevance is underscored by elevated pathogenic bacterial taxa (Anaerovorax, Clostridiales, Coriobacteriaceae) and increased stress-axis biomarkers (cortisol, ACTH) in IBS patients, as well as associations between mitochondrial DNA copy number and disease severity.

title: Uterine Endometrial Microbiome and Chronic Endometritis in Relation to Anti‐β2‐Glycoprotein I (β2GPI)/Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA)‐DR Autoantibodies in Women With Recurrent Implantation Failure and Recurrent Pregnancy Loss: A Cross‐Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined the relationship between anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR autoantibodies, endometrial microbiome composition, and chronic endometritis in women experiencing recurrent implantation failure (RIF) and recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL). Anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR antibodies were significantly associated with endometrial dysbiosis characterized by increased prevalence of reproductive-failure-related bacteria, particularly Lactobacillus iners, Prevotella, Anaerococcus, and Ureaplasma species, while healthy Lactobacillus-dominant microbiota were reduced. The authors propose that endometrial dysbiosis and chronic endometritis-induced local inflammation promote expression of the β2GPI/HLA-DR antigen complex, triggering autoantibody production that subsequently impairs implantation and placentation. Anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR antibody positivity emerged as a potential biomarker of abnormal intrauterine environment, with antibiotic treatment of chronic endometritis and immunomodulatory therapy (low-dose aspirin and heparin) shown to improve reproductive outcomes in affected women.

title: Bacterial Infections Role in Gynecological Cancers Development: Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines bacterial infections as risk factors for gynecological cancers through microbiota dysbiosis mechanisms. Multiple bacterial species show strong associations with specific cancer types: *Chlamydia trachomatis* and *Mycoplasma genitalium* with ovarian cancer, *Bacteroides*, *Porphyromonas*, *Prevotella*, *Atopobium*, *Anaerococcus*, and *Pseudomonas* with endometrial cancer, and *Gardnerella vaginalis*, *Prevotella bivia*, *Mycoplasma genitalium*, and *Fusobacterium* spp. with cervical cancer. The molecular mechanisms involve bacterial toxins and virulence factors that trigger oncogenic pathways; for example, *Chlamydia* stimulates reactive oxygen species production and inhibits DNA damage response, while simultaneously promoting P53 degradation and HSP60-mediated caspase activation. Dysbiosis—characterized by reduction in protective *Lactobacillus* species and proliferation of anaerobic bacteria—compromises the vaginal mucus barrier through bacterial sialidase enzymes, increasing epithelial susceptibility to HPV and facilitating carcinogenic transformation. Chronic inflammation induced by bacterial infections and biofilm formation further promote carcinogenesis through NF-κB signaling and other oncogenic pathways. These findings highlight microbiota composition as a critical factor in gynecological cancer development.

title: The Use of Spice Herbs May Reduce Chronic Inflammation and Improve the Quality of Life of Women with Metabolic Syndrome—A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Metabolic syndrome is a multifactorial condition characterized by obesity, hyperglycemia, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and insulin resistance that drives chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and angiogenesis through dysregulation of adipose tissue metabolism. Visceral adipose tissue produces elevated levels of pro-inflammatory adipocytokines, including leptin and visfatin, which activate inflammatory pathways via TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, while simultaneously reducing anti-inflammatory adiponectin levels—a pattern particularly pronounced in women where estrogen deficiency exacerbates adipose tissue dysfunction. This metabolic inflammation involves activation of the TLR4/NF-κB signaling cascade by free fatty acids and other danger-associated molecules, driving obesity-related inflammation that increases risk for estrogen-dependent neoplasms including endometrial cancer, breast cancer, cervical cancer, and endometriosis. Spice herbs including basil and spearmint reduce chronic inflammation by suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines, while dietary interventions incorporating Mediterranean diet patterns, polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fatty acids, and dietary fiber enhance anti-inflammatory responses through increased production of short-chain fatty acids by beneficial microbiota such as Bifidobacterium. These nutritional and herbal approaches offer potential therapeutic strategies to attenuate the inflammatory burden of metabolic syndrome and improve quality of life in affected women, particularly those at heightened risk for inflammation-associated gynecological diseases.

title: Integrated Analysis of Gut Microbiota and Metabolome Profiling Reveals the Effects of Feeding Approaches on Infants' Gut Microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

Breastfeeding and formula feeding establish distinct microbial compositions in infant gut environments, with breastfeeding promoting Proteobacteria-dominated communities enriched in taxa including Escherichia, Staphylococcus, Haemophilus, and Rothia, while formula feeding promotes Actinobacteria dominance with increased Enterococcus and Mediterraneibacter. Breastfeeding downregulates arachidonic acid metabolism pathways—including synthesis of thromboxane B2, prostaglandins A2, and related eicosanoids—thereby maintaining a lower inflammatory tone through modulation of the microbial-metabolic axis. The bioactive constituents of breast milk, particularly human milk oligosaccharides, facilitate colonization by beneficial bacteria including Bifidobacterium and Bacteroides, which drive short-chain fatty acid biosynthesis and microbial production of immunomodulatory metabolites like indole-3-lactic acid that activate cytoprotective pathways. These microbiota-dependent metabolic processes support immune maturation and infant development while providing protection against diarrheal and respiratory infections. The distinct microbial ecosystems established by feeding approach fundamentally shape the early-life gut microenvironment and carry implications for long-term health trajectories.

title: Exploratory analyses of cervicovaginal mucus O-glycan composition and microbiota profiles in unexplained infertility
summary:
# Summary

This exploratory study examined cervicovaginal mucus (CVM) O-glycan composition and vaginal microbiota profiles in women with unexplained infertility, revealing critical interactions between these two vaginal ecosystem components. The CVM, composed primarily of gel-forming mucins (MUC2, MUC5AC, MUC5B) and transmembrane mucins (MUC1, MUC16), is extensively modified by O-glycosylation and serves as both a protective barrier and selective filter for sperm, with its properties regulated by mucin proportions and glycan composition. Analysis identified 57 distinct O-glycan structures dominated by core 1 and 2 structures, with approximately 85% being fucosylated and 44% containing sialic acid residues, which showed significant associations with dominant vaginal taxa. Lactobacillus crispatus dominated the healthy vaginal microbiota and demonstrated competitive binding to specific glycan structures, contributing to improved reproductive outcomes including higher in vitro fertilization success rates and uncomplicated pregnancies. In contrast, microbial dysbiosis characterized by reduced Lactobacillus species and increased organisms like Prevotella and Gardnerella—which encode fucosidases and sialidases that degrade terminal sugar residues—compromised CVM barrier integrity and associated with reduced fecundity. These findings underscore how O-glycan structure variation establishes a molecular dialogue between the CVM glycome and the vaginal microbiota, fundamentally influencing reproductive health and fertility outcomes.
